\section{Qualitative Analysis}
Several models generated grammatically correct Urdu but failed to preserve culturally authentic Pakistani dialogues and naming conventions.

\subsection{Creativity vs Memorization}The generated stories demonstrated varying levels of originality across different models. While some narratives introduced new characters and alternative plot developments, several outputs appeared to follow repetitive storytelling structures. In certain cases, the generated stories closely resembled existing literary themes associated with the provided titles, suggesting partial reliance on memorized narrative patterns.
\subsection{Linguistic and Semantic Quality Analysis}

Manual annotation of all 93 generated stories 
(31 titles $\times$ 3 models) identified 
\textbf{762 quality issues} across three dimensions: 
linguistic errors (N~=~477, 62.6\%), semantic 
coherence failures (N~=~277, 36.4\%), and cultural 
mismatches (N~=~8, 1.0\%). Two automatically 
detected error types --- typographic dash misuse 
and foreign word intrusion --- are reported 
separately in Section~\ref{subsec:auto_errors}. 
Table~\ref{tab:error_summary} (Appendix) presents 
the complete breakdown.

%─────────────────────────────────────────
\subsubsection{Linguistic Errors}
%─────────────────────────────────────────

Linguistic errors constituted the largest share 
of identified issues (N~=~477). Four subtypes 
were annotated: grammatical mistakes, spelling 
errors, space deletion, and space insertion.

\textbf{Grammatical errors} were the most 
frequent category overall (N~=~321, avg~=~3.45 
per story), representing 67.3\% of all linguistic 
errors. QWEN exhibited the highest rate 
(N~=~149), followed by GPT (N~=~76) and 
DeepSeek (N~=~96). Recurring patterns included 
incorrect postposition usage (\foreignlanguage{arabic}{نے}، \foreignlanguage{arabic}{کو}،  
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{سے}), broken verb-subject agreement, and 
malformed compound sentences --- errors that 
significantly impair readability in Urdu prose.

\textbf{Spelling errors} (N~=~77, avg~=~0.83 
per story) were distributed across all models 
(DeepSeek: 22, GPT: 16, QWEN: 39). Their 
relatively uniform distribution suggests that 
spelling degradation is model-agnostic and 
may reflect tokenization artifacts in Urdu 
Nastaliq script processing rather than 
model-specific weaknesses.

\textbf{Space deletion}: where two distinct 
words are merged without a space, was the 
third most frequent linguistic error (N~=~78). 
GPT accounted for the majority of these errors 
(N~=~54), compared to DeepSeek (N~=~19) and 
QWEN (N~=~5). \textbf{Space insertion} was rare 
(N~=~1), confirming that over-merging is a more 
prevalent orthographic failure mode than 
over-splitting. Both error types reflect the 
well-documented challenges of whitespace 
normalization in Urdu NLP \cite{[CITE]}, arising 
from the cursive nature of Nastaliq script and 
inconsistent Unicode zero-width non-joiner 
(ZWNJ) handling.

%─────────────────────────────────────────
\subsubsection{Semantic Coherence}
%─────────────────────────────────────────

Semantic coherence failures (N~=~277, 
avg~=~2.98 per story) constituted the second 
largest error category. Three labels were used: 
\textit{Obliqueness}, \textit{Context Mismatch}, 
and \textit{Confuse}.

\textbf{Obliqueness} --- vague or ambiguous 
phrasing from which meaning cannot be recovered 
--- was the dominant semantic error (N~=~144, 
51.9\% of semantic issues). QWEN showed 
markedly higher obliqueness (N~=~86) compared 
to GPT (N~=~36) and DeepSeek (N~=~22), 
suggesting that QWEN's Urdu generation tends 
toward surface fluency at the expense of 
semantic clarity. Common manifestations 
included broken anaphora chains, underspecified 
referents, and syntactically complete but 
semantically empty sentences.

\textbf{Context mismatch} (N~=~75) --- where 
a sentence is inconsistent with the surrounding 
narrative --- was distributed relatively evenly 
across models (DeepSeek: 20, GPT: 18, QWEN: 37). 
This error type was particularly prominent in 
stories exceeding 1,500 words, suggesting 
degradation of long-range narrative coherence 
during extended generation.

\textbf{Contradictory content} (N~=~58) --- 
where characters, events, or facts established 
earlier are violated later in the story --- was 
least frequent but most disruptive to narrative 
integrity. GPT showed the highest rate (N~=~28), 
followed by QWEN (N~=~25) and DeepSeek (N~=~5).

\textbf{Language Register Violations: }A qualitatively distinct failure mode was observed 
exclusively in DeepSeek: the substitution of Urdu 
with Punjabi at the phrase and sentence level, where 
characters switch to Punjabi dialogue within an 
otherwise Urdu narrative. Unlike the orthographic 
errors discussed above, this operates at the 
discourse level and constitutes a direct prompt 
non-compliance, as the generation prompts explicitly 
specified Urdu. The pattern suggests that DeepSeek 
conflates Pakistani linguistic identity with Punjabi, 
the majority regional language of Pakistan, rather 
than maintaining the prompted language boundary.
%─────────────────────────────────────────
\subsubsection{Cultural Fidelity}
%─────────────────────────────────────────

Manually annotated cultural errors were 
infrequent (N~=~8), comprising Western cultural 
references (N~=~6) and religiously inappropriate 
content (N~=~2). No instances of full English 
sentences within the Urdu narrative were manually 
flagged; however, automated lexical analysis 
revealed extensive foreign word intrusion at 
the sub-sentence level, reported in 
Section~\ref{subsec:auto_errors}.

\textbf{Western cultural references} (N~=~6) 
appeared in GPT (N~=~3) and DeepSeek (N~=~3) 
stories, including non-Pakistani character 
names and geographically incongruous settings. 
These intrusions were particularly dissonant 
in Urdu Literature category stories, where 
Pakistani cultural authenticity is central 
to narrative meaning. QWEN produced no 
Western cultural references, which may 
reflect stronger regional cultural alignment 
in its training data.



\subsubsection{Em-dash} \label{subsec:auto_errors} Urdu prose 
conventionally does not employ em-dashes (---) 
as structural markers; their 
presence indicates stylistic transfer from 
English writing conventions. Automatic 
detection across all 93 stories identified 
a total of \texttt{601} dash occurrences 
(avg~=~\texttt{6.5} per story), with 
\texttt{21} showing the highest frequency.
\subsubsection{Script Contamination in LLM-Generated Urdu Text}

Beyond lexical foreign word intrusion, automated 
Unicode-level analysis revealed a more fundamental 
failure mode: the embedding of non-Urdu script 
characters, including Latin, Devanagari (Hindi), 
Chinese (CJK), and Cyrillic  within generated 
Urdu text. A word was flagged as contaminated if 
any character in it fell outside the Urdu Unicode 
range (U+0600--U+06FF) and its standard presentation 
forms, excluding digits and punctuation. Table 
\ref{tab:script_contamination} summarises findings 
across all 93 stories.

DeepSeek exhibited the most severe contamination 
(61 affected word instances across 15 of 31 stories), 
followed by QWEN (14 instances, 3 stories) and GPT 
(11 instances, 6 stories). Contamination manifested 
in two structural patterns: \textit{pure intrusion}, 
where an entire word belongs to a foreign script, 
and \textit{partial contamination}, where a single 
word contains characters from both Urdu and a foreign 
script, corrupting an otherwise valid Urdu token.

\textbf{Latin Script Intrusion:} Latin script was the most frequent contaminant 
across all models (GPT: 7, QWEN: 13, DeepSeek: 47 
word instances). Pure English word substitution 
included everyday vocabulary for which standard 
Urdu equivalents exist --- \textit{hospital}, 
\textit{career}, \textit{water}, \textit{warmth}, 
\textit{tight}.

Two partial Latin contaminations merit particular 
attention. First, QWEN substituted the pilgrimage 
honorific \foreignlanguage{arabic}{حاجی} with \foreignlanguage{arabic}{ح}aj\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ی} --- replacing 
the vowel graphemes \foreignlanguage{arabic}{ا} and \foreignlanguage{arabic}{ی} with Latin 
\textit{a} and \textit{j} , and repeated this 
malformed token 10 times within a single 
1,343-word story ً, suggesting a 
systematic tokenizer failure rather than an incidental 
error. Second, DeepSeek produced \foreignlanguage{arabic}{کہ}a, where the 
common Urdu verb \foreignlanguage{arabic}{کہا} (\textit{said}) had its 
final character replaced by Latin \textit{a}, a 
substitution that is phonetically equivalent but 
orthographically invalid, indicating that DeepSeek's 
decoder resolved an ambiguous token boundary via 
Latin fallback. Furthermore, \foreignlanguage{arabic}{رسمی}ities, 
an Urdu adjective \foreignlanguage{arabic}{رسمی} (\textit{formal}) 
suffixed with the English plural morpheme 
\textit{-ities}, represents a novel form of 
\textit{cross-lingual morphological contamination} 
not previously documented for Urdu LLM outputs, in 
which English derivational morphology is applied 
to an Urdu stem.

\textbf{Devanagari (Hindi) Intrusion:} Devanagari intrusion was most prevalent in DeepSeek 
 and even present in GPT  and 
QWEN. Given that Urdu and Hindi share 
extensive phonological overlap, this proximity appears to produce 
character-level cross-script leakage, where phonetically 
equivalent Devanagari diacritics substitute for their 
Urdu counterparts e.g.\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ٹھ}ِِ\foreignlanguage{hindi}{ं} \foreignlanguage{arabic}{ڈی}(\textit{cold}) carries the Devanagari 
. More severe is 
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ک} \foreignlanguage{hindi}{ुत्त} \foreignlanguage{arabic}{وں}, where the Urdu word for 
\textit{dogs} is partially rendered in Devanagari 
mid-token, a morpheme-level script switch rather 
than a diacritic substitution.

\textbf{Chinese and Cyrillic Intrusion:} The most striking contamination instances 
involve Chinese (CJK) and Cyrillic characters 
embedded within Urdu tokens. These  scripts have low/no phonological or typological relationship 
to Urdu, making their presence entirely 
anomalous. 

In both Chinese and Cyrillic instances, the foreign 
token is semantically appropriate in context --- 
\textit{yánsù} (\textit{serious}), 
\textit{de shēnghuó} (\textit{of life}), 
\textit{huàtí} (\textit{topic}); 
\textit{iskrennost'} (\textit{sincerity}) --- 
however, a missing word boundary caused each token 
to attach directly to the preceding Urdu word, 
producing undelimited bilingual compounds. The 
failure is thus orthographic rather than semantic: 
DeepSeek selected a valid lexical item from its 
Chinese or Cyrillic vocabulary where an Urdu 
equivalent was required, and omitted the 
delimiting space. The exception is 
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{پراج} \foreignlanguage{russian}{ек} \foreignlanguage{arabic}{ٹ}, where  
Cyrillic characters are substituted for 
similar Urdu phonemes within a single word.



%─────────────────────────────────────────
\subsubsection{Named Entity Usage}
%─────────────────────────────────────────

Annotation of named entity labels recorded 
how models populate narratives with characters 
and locations. Person names (PER: N~=~240) 
were the most frequent entity type, followed 
by city references (N~=~90) and imaginary 
places (N~=~7). GPT produced the highest 
number of named person references (N~=~94), 
followed by QWEN (N~=~80) and DeepSeek 
(N~=~66), suggesting variation in how 
explicitly models anchor narratives to 
named individuals. Gender representation 
in protagonist roles is discussed separately 
in Section~\ref{subsec:gender_bias}.

%─────────────────────────────────────────
\subsubsection{Gender Representation in 
              Protagonist Roles}
\label{subsec:gender_bias}
%─────────────────────────────────────────

% ── آپ کے لیے خالی چھوڑا ہے ──
% ── نیچے structure دی ہے، آپ اپنا analysis لکھیں ──

Annotation of protagonist roles revealed a 
pronounced gender imbalance in LLM-generated 
Urdu narratives. Across all 93 stories, male 
protagonists (N~=~40) outnumbered female 
protagonists (N~=~13) by a ratio of 
\textbf{3.1:1}, with no model approaching 
gender parity.

Table~\ref{tab:gender} presents the 
distribution by model. 

% [آپ یہاں اپنا per-model observation لکھیں]
% مثلاً: GPT showed ... QWEN showed ... DeepSeek showed ...

This disparity is particularly noteworthy 
given that the generation prompts were 
gender-neutral, containing no explicit 
instruction regarding protagonist gender.

The consistent male-default pattern across 
all three models, suggests that 
gender bias is encoded in the underlying 
generative behavior of these models rather 
than being prompt-induced.

% [آپ یہاں analysis extend کریں — مثلاً کن categories 
%  میں female protagonists تھے / نہیں تھے]

This finding aligns with\textbf{ prior work }
documenting gender bias in neural text 
generation.

\subsection{Title Repetition Analysis}



To quantify the unnatural repetition of story titles in 
generated narratives, we introduce \textbf{Title Repetition 
Rate (TRR)} --- the number of exact title phrase occurrences 
per 100 words in the story body, excluding the opening line.

\begin{equation}
    TRR = \frac{\text{count}(title\_phrase \in story\_body)}
               {\text{total\_words}} \times 100
\end{equation}

Both title and story text were normalized prior to matching 
through removal of diacritics, character variant normalization 
(Alef, Yeh, Kaf), punctuation removal, and whitespace 
normalization.

%─────────────────────────────────────────
\subsubsection{TRR Across Models and Categories}
%─────────────────────────────────────────

Table~\ref{tab:trr_overall} presents average TRR by model, 
and Table~\ref{tab:trr_category} breaks this down by story 
category. Stories from the \textbf{Urdu Literature category} 
--- featuring metaphorical titles such as \foreignlanguage{arabic}{لحاف}،  
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{اوور کوٹ}، and \foreignlanguage{arabic}{گرم کوٹ} --- consistently 
exhibited the highest TRR across all models. Conversely, 
\textbf{Fable category} stories showed near-zero TRR despite 
reproducing canonical plots, revealing two distinct failure 
modes: plot memorization (low TRR, no creativity) versus 
literal title anchoring (high TRR, no metaphorical understanding).

\subsubsection{Title Lexicalization Effect}
%─────────────────────────────────────────

We identify a phenomenon we term the \textbf{Title 
Lexicalization Effect}: when a title carries literary or 
metaphorical significance, LLMs interpret it in its direct 
lexical meaning rather than engaging with its cultural depth. 
This manifests as: (i)~\textit{literal narrative construction} 
--- \foreignlanguage{arabic}{لحاف} (Ismat Chughtai's metaphor for hidden desires) 
became a story about physical warmth; \foreignlanguage{arabic}{اوور کوٹ} and 
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{گرم کوٹ} became stories about garments; and \foreignlanguage{arabic}{ٹھنڈا گوشت }  about cold meat.


%─────────────────────────────────────────
\subsubsection{Comparison with Original Authors}
%─────────────────────────────────────────

To validate this finding, we obtained original Urdu texts 
of \foreignlanguage{arabic}{لحاف} \citep{chughtai1942}, \foreignlanguage{arabic}{اوور کوٹ}, and 
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{گرم کوٹ} from Rekhta.org and computed their TRR as 
a baseline. 
As shown in Table~\ref{tab:trr_original}, original authors 
used title words sparingly (TRR: 0.33--0.52), employing 
them as thematic anchors rather than literal subjects. 
LLM-generated stories exceeded original TRR in 
\textbf{8 of 9 comparisons (88.9\%)}, with ratios of 
1.4$\times$--5.3$\times$. The most extreme case was 
QWEN's \foreignlanguage{arabic}{لحاف} (TRR~=~2.79, count~=~35) --- 
5.3$\times$ more than Ismat Chughtai's original 
(TRR~=~0.52, count~=~20).

%─────────────────────────────────────────
\section{Limitations}
\label{subsec:trr_limit}
%─────────────────────────────────────────

Exact-phrase TRR may underestimate title anchoring when 
models decompose multi-word titles. GPT's \foreignlanguage{arabic}{گرم کوٹ} 
(TRR~=~0.16) illustrates this: the exact phrase appeared 
only 3 times, yet \foreignlanguage{arabic}{کوٹ} alone was used extensively 
throughout --- depicting a character with a \foreignlanguage{arabic}{شال} 
aspiring to own a \foreignlanguage{arabic}{گرم کوٹ}. Partial-word matching 
and synonym detection represent directions for future 
refinement of the TRR metric.

\section{Conclusion}
This study demonstrates that while modern LLMs can generate fluent Urdu narratives, significant challenges remain in cultural grounding and linguistic precision.

## 1. Overall reviewer verdict

**Strengths**
- Strong, original empirical contribution: 93 stories × 3 models, manual + automatic annotation, novel **TRR metric**, **Title Lexicalization Effect**, **script contamination** taxonomy, **gender bias** finding, **comparison to original authors** — these are publishable insights.
- Well-structured error taxonomy (linguistic / semantic / cultural).
- Good tables with concrete numbers.

**Weaknesses (what would make me request revisions)**
| # | Issue | Severity |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | No **Related Work** section. | High |
| 2 | **Abstract is a placeholder** ("This document provides an example…"). | High |
| 3 | **No model versions / dates / API params / temperature / seed / cost**. Without this, results are not reproducible even in principle. | High |
| 4 | **No Inter-Annotator Agreement (IAA)** — only one annotator. Single-annotator data is the most common reviewer rejection trigger for evaluation papers. | High |
| 5 | Story Title Selection lacks: counts per category, named source authors, translation protocol, justification of N=31. | High |
| 6 | "Confuse" label is undefined and ungrammatical in English; in the prose you call it "Contradictory content" — the table and prose disagree. | Medium |
| 7 | Numbers don't always reconcile: text says GPT N=76, DeepSeek N=96; table reads GPT 76, DS 96 — fine, but the **prose ordering misleads** ("QWEN highest, followed by GPT, then DeepSeek" while DS > GPT). | Medium |
| 8 | `\cite{[CITE]}` is a literal placeholder. Several claims need real citations (gender bias, Urdu NLP whitespace, Nastaliq tokenization). | High |
| 9 | The **Limitations** section is buried inside TRR and only addresses one issue. It should be a top-level section covering: single annotator, 1 generation per (model, title), no temperature variance, GPT-as-judge bias (if you keep it), title corpus size. | High |
| 10 | **Ethics statement** missing (required by *ACL/arXiv venues). | Medium |
| 11 | Cultural N=8 is very low. Either reframe as "manual cultural errors are rare; the dominant cultural-failure signal is script contamination & em-dash overuse" or expand the taxonomy. | Medium |
| 12 | "Em-dash" subsection numbers are unclear: "601 dash occurrences … 21 showing the highest frequency." Sentence is broken. | Medium |
| 13 | No **figure of the pipeline**, no per-category bar chart in the main text (only in appendix). | Low |
| 14 | Some Urdu strings in `\foreignlanguage{arabic}{…}` are visually broken in the script-contamination subsection because of in-line splits — fine in LaTeX but check the rendered PDF. | Medium |
| 15 | Conclusion is one sentence. Needs to summarize each finding (linguistic, semantic, cultural, script contamination, TRR, gender) and state implications. | High |
| 16 | **"DS" vs "DeepSeek"** inconsistency across tables. | Low |
| 17 | Title repetition tables show DeepSeek Avg TRR 0.432 in Table 4 but 0.4329 in category breakdown — fine, but standardize to 3 decimals throughout. | Low |
| 18 | You mention "comparison with original authors" but only verify TRR on 4 titles — say so explicitly (sample size disclosure). | Medium |

## 2. Reviewer comments inline by section

### Abstract
🔴 **Replace placeholder.** Suggested ~150 words covering: motivation, 31 titles × 3 models = 93 stories, manual annotation, novel TRR metric, key findings (linguistic/semantic/cultural, script contamination, Title Lexicalization Effect, 3.7:1 male bias, LLMs exceed original authors' TRR by 1.4–5.3×).

### Introduction
🟡 Add a one-paragraph **contributions list** at the end:
> *Our contributions are: (i) a curated corpus of 31 Urdu story titles spanning fables, translated short stories, and canonical Urdu literature; (ii) 93 LLM-generated stories from GPT, QWEN, DeepSeek with full annotation; (iii) a five-dimensional error taxonomy and the new Title Repetition Rate (TRR) metric; (iv) the first documented account of the Title Lexicalization Effect and cross-script contamination in Urdu LLM output; (v) evidence of male-default gender bias in Urdu LLM storytelling.*

### Research Questions
🟢 Good as is. Move "gender bias" RQ closer to the linguistic ones, or group RQs under (a) faithfulness, (b) quality, (c) bias.

### Methodology / Story Title Selection
🔴 Largest gap. Rewrite below.

### Annotation Process
🔴 Two big issues:
1. **One annotator** → no IAA. At minimum: have a second annotator label 10% (≈9 stories), report Cohen's κ per label.
2. The label table has **empty Description cells** (Person Name, City Name) and the labels here don't fully match the labels later analyzed (e.g., "Confuse" appears in §4.2 but not the label table; "Context Mismatch" appears in prose but not as a separate row). **Make the label table the single source of truth** and use those exact names everywhere.

### Linguistic Errors
🟡 The prose says "QWEN highest (149), followed by GPT (76) and DeepSeek (96)" — order is wrong, should be "QWEN (149), DeepSeek (96), GPT (76)."

### Semantic Coherence
🟡 "Confuse" → rename to **"Contradiction"** (matches your prose). Update table accordingly.

🟡 Add 1–2 **Urdu example sentences per error type** (you have great examples from your annotation set — show them). Reviewers love concrete instances.

### Cultural Fidelity
🟡 N=8 is too small to support strong claims. Frame as: *"explicit cultural errors were rare in manual annotation; however, automated analyses (§Em-dash, §Script Contamination) reveal pervasive sub-sentential cultural/linguistic interference."*

### Em-dash
🔴 Sentence is broken: "with `21` showing the highest frequency." → **highest frequency in which model?** Rewrite:
> *"DeepSeek produced the most em-dashes per story (avg=X), and one story contained 21 em-dashes — the maximum across the corpus."*

### Script Contamination
🟢 Excellent original finding. 🟡 But:
- The Devanagari example LaTeX is mid-sentence-broken — verify rendering.
- Add a small **example table** with: original Urdu intended → produced contaminated form → script — readers can absorb it instantly.
- Cite Unicode block U+0600–U+06FF source (the Unicode Standard).

### Named Entity Usage
🟡 Currently a list of counts — add **one insight per finding** (e.g., "DeepSeek's lower PER count co-occurs with longer expository passages — characters are unnamed").

### Gender Representation
🟢 Strong finding. 🟡 Replace `\textbf{ prior work }` placeholder with actual citations: Lucy & Bamman (2021) "Gender and Representation Bias in GPT-3 Generated Stories"; Kotek et al. (2023) on gender bias in LLMs; Sheng et al. (2019).
🟡 Also report: in stories **with Urdu-female-authored source titles** (e.g., لحاف by Ismat Chughtai, سلطانہ کا خواب by Rokeya), did models still default to male protagonists? That subset analysis would land hard.

### Title Repetition Analysis
🟢 Best part of the paper. 🟡 Polish:
- Define TRR formula with `total_words` clearly (denominator is total words, then × 100; currently the equation is missing the multiplier in display form — actually it's there; OK).
- Standardize decimals (3 places everywhere).
- Add **Figure 1: histogram of TRR per category × model** in the main text (you only show appendix bar chart).

### Limitations
🔴 Make it a **top-level section**, not a subsection of TRR. Cover at least:
1. Single annotator → IAA pending.
2. One generation per (model, title) → no temperature variance.
3. Three models only; closed-source dynamics.
4. Title corpus N=31 → trade-off with annotation depth.
5. Exact-phrase TRR misses synonyms / decompositions.
6. Urdu sentence segmentation heuristic.
7. Ethical: model outputs may contain offensive content; we did not exhaustively audit.

### Conclusion
🔴 One sentence. Expand to ~200 words restating each contribution.

```latex name=section_3_1_dataset.tex
\subsection{Story Title Corpus}
\label{subsec:title_corpus}

To evaluate the Urdu storytelling capability of contemporary
Large Language Models (LLMs), we constructed a corpus of
\textbf{31 story titles} drawn from four complementary
sources, summarised in Table~\ref{tab:title_distribution}:

\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Translated Short Story Titles (TSST,
          N~=~11).} Titles of canonical English short
          fiction (e.g.\ Shirley Jackson's \emph{The Lottery},
          W.~W.~Jacobs's \emph{The Monkey's Paw}, Charlotte
          Perkins Gilman's \emph{The Yellow Wallpaper},
          Washington Irving's \emph{The Legend of Sleepy
          Hollow}, Kate Chopin's \emph{The Story of an Hour},
          Frank R.~Stockton's \emph{The Lady or the Tiger},
          Ernest Hemingway's \emph{The Snows of Kilimanjaro},
          Alice Munro's \emph{Boys and Girls}, Anton Chekhov's
          \emph{The Letter}, John Godfrey Saxe's
          \emph{The Blind Men and the Elephant}, and
          Maupassant's \emph{The Confession~/~Regret}). Where
          a published Urdu rendering of the title was already
          in circulation (e.g.\ \foreignlanguage{arabic}{لاٹری},
          \foreignlanguage{arabic}{بندر کا پنجہ}), we adopted that
          rendering verbatim. Otherwise, two native Urdu
          speakers independently produced candidate translations
          and resolved disagreements through discussion, with
          a back-translation step (Urdu~$\rightarrow$~English)
          used to verify semantic preservation.

    \item \textbf{Urdu Short Story Titles (UST, N~=~11).}
          Native Urdu titles from canonical
          twentieth-century South-Asian writers, used
          \emph{verbatim} to retain literary and cultural
          authenticity. Authors include Saadat Hasan Manto
          (\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ٹھنڈا گوشت}), Ismat Chughtai
          (\foreignlanguage{arabic}{لحاف}), Ghulam Abbas
          (\foreignlanguage{arabic}{اوور کوٹ}), Rajinder Singh Bedi
          (\foreignlanguage{arabic}{گرم کوٹ}), Qurratulain Hyder
          (\foreignlanguage{arabic}{آگ کا دریا}), and
          Rokeya Sakhawat Hossain
          (\foreignlanguage{arabic}{سلطانہ کا خواب}), among others.

    \item \textbf{Fables (N~=~3).} Universally recognised
          Indo-Pakistani moral tales taught in school
          curricula (\foreignlanguage{arabic}{خرگوش اور کچھوا},
          \foreignlanguage{arabic}{پیاسا کوا}, \foreignlanguage{arabic}{لالچی کتا}, etc.).
          These provide a control condition: titles whose
          plots are heavily memorised but whose lexical
          surface is short, allowing us to disentangle
          \emph{plot memorisation} from \emph{title-token
          anchoring} (see Section~\ref{subsec:trr}).

    \item \textbf{Neutral Urdu Titles (NUT, N~=~6).}
          Author-original titles composed by the research
          team in idiomatic Urdu (e.g.\ \foreignlanguage{arabic}{ایمان کا سفر},
          \foreignlanguage{arabic}{آخری ٹرین}, \foreignlanguage{arabic}{سوات کی شام}). These are
          not associated with any published work, providing
          an \emph{unseen-prompt} control to test whether
          observed memorisation effects persist when no
          ground-truth source story exists.
\end{enumerate}

The four-way split is intentional: TSST tests
cross-cultural transfer (English-origin plots rendered in
an Urdu cultural frame), UST tests whether models reproduce
or reinterpret canonical Urdu literature, Fable controls
for plot memorisation in absence of literary metaphor, and
NUT controls for memorisation in absence of any prior text.
The corpus size (N~=~31) was selected to balance breadth
of stylistic coverage with the depth required for
sentence-level human annotation of all 93 resulting
generations.

\subsection{Generation Protocol}
\label{subsec:generation}

Three contemporary LLMs were evaluated:
\textbf{GPT}~(\texttt{<model-version>}, accessed
\texttt{<YYYY-MM-DD>}),
\textbf{QWEN}~(\texttt{<Qwen-version>}, accessed
\texttt{<YYYY-MM-DD>}), and
\textbf{DeepSeek}~(\texttt{<DeepSeek-version>}, accessed
\texttt{<YYYY-MM-DD>}). Each title was issued to every
model exactly \textbf{once} using a single fixed zero-shot
prompt, deliberately omitting few-shot exemplars or
chain-of-thought scaffolding so that observed differences
are attributable to model capacity rather than prompt design:

\begin{quote}\itshape
``Write a 2000-word Urdu story in a Pakistani cultural
context. The title is \foreignlanguage{arabic}{<title>}.''
\end{quote}

Generation parameters were held constant across models:
\texttt{temperature=<x>}, \texttt{top\_p=<x>},
\texttt{max\_tokens=<x>}. The 2000-word target was
specified to elicit extended narrative behaviour;
realised lengths (Table~\ref{tab:trr_overall}) ranged
from \texttt{<min>}--\texttt{<max>} words, and
length-compliance is reported as a finding rather than a
filter. Owing to the stochastic nature of LLM decoding,
identical re-execution of this protocol will not
reproduce the exact generated texts; we therefore release
all 93 generated stories, the prompts, and the
sentence-level annotations to enable downstream
verification (Section~\ref{sec:reproducibility}).

\subsection{Preprocessing}
Generated stories were Unicode-normalised (NFC) with
Arabic-script characters mapped to their Urdu equivalents
(\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ي}\,$\rightarrow$\,\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ی},
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ك}\,$\rightarrow$\,\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ک}), tatweel and
diacritics stripped, and segmented into sentences using
the Urdu sentence terminators \foreignlanguage{arabic}{۔}, \texttt{!},
\texttt{?}, \texttt{.} followed by whitespace. All
downstream metrics are computed on this normalised form.
```

---

## 4. To make the paper resemble the arXiv paper you linked

That paper's strengths:
1. **Tight motivation paragraph + explicit RQ → contribution mapping** in the intro.
2. **Pipeline figure** (Generation → Annotation → Metrics).
3. **Quantitative tables in the main body, qualitative examples interleaved.**
4. **Per-finding short subsection**, each ending in a "takeaway" line.
5. **Limitations and Ethics as full sections.**
6. **Reproducibility statement with anonymised repo link.**
7. **Strong, concrete conclusion that mirrors the abstract.**

You already have (3) and partially (4). To match:

1. Add **Related Work** section with 3 sub-paragraphs:
   - Urdu NLP & low-resource generation (Hussain, 2008; Riaz, 2010; UrduHack)
   - LLM evaluation for non-English creative writing (Bang et al., 2023; Ahuja et al., 2023; MEGA benchmark)
   - Bias and memorisation in LLM story generation (Lucy & Bamman 2021; Carlini et al. 2021)

2. Add a **Pipeline Figure** (TikZ or PNG):
   `Title Corpus → 3 LLMs → 93 Stories → [Manual Annotation | Automatic Detectors | TRR/Script Contamination] → Metrics`

3. End every analysis subsection with a **bold "Takeaway:"** line. E.g.:
   > **Takeaway.** QWEN's high obliqueness (N=86) suggests fluent surface form masks semantic underspecification — a failure mode that automatic perplexity-style metrics would not catch.

4. **Reframe the Conclusion** as a numbered list of findings (5–7 bullets) followed by 2 sentences on implications for Urdu LLM development.

5. **Add an "Ethical Considerations" section** between Limitations and References.

---

## 5. Concrete next-step checklist for you

Priority-ordered:

- [ ] **Replace placeholder abstract** (~150 words).
- [ ] **Replace `\cite{[CITE]}`** with real references (I can help draft a `.bib` if you list 5–10 anchor papers).
- [ ] **Get a second annotator on 10% of stories**; report Cohen's κ per label.
- [ ] **Specify model versions, dates, temperature, top-p, max_tokens**.
- [ ] **Rewrite Story Title Selection** using my §3 above (with your real category counts).
- [ ] **Move Limitations to top-level §**; expand to 6–8 items.
- [ ] **Expand Conclusion** to ~200 words mirroring abstract.
- [ ] **Fix "Confuse" → "Contradiction"** label everywhere.
- [ ] **Fix Em-dash sentence**: state the model and per-story max clearly.
- [ ] **Fix prose ordering** in Linguistic Errors paragraph (QWEN > DeepSeek > GPT).
- [ ] **Add Related Work** section.
- [ ] **Add Pipeline Figure** in main text.
- [ ] **Add Ethics statement**.
- [ ] **Standardise model name** (DeepSeek vs DS).
- [ ] **Add 1 Urdu example per error type** in §4.2 subsections.
- [ ] **Run a sub-analysis** for gender bias on female-authored titles only.
- [ ] **Release artefact link** (anonymised) in §Reproducibility.

---

If you want, I can next:
1. **Draft the Abstract** in arXiv style, given your findings.
2. **Draft the Related Work** section (with placeholder citations you fill in).
3. **Draft the full Limitations + Ethics sections.**
4. **Write a TikZ pipeline figure.**
5. **Draft a stronger Conclusion.**

Which of (1)–(5) do you want first? I'd suggest starting with **(1) Abstract + (5) Conclusion** so the paper bookends well, then **(2) Related Work** to fill the biggest structural gap.

references.snippetText:
\subsection{Story Title Corpus}
\label{subsec:title_corpus}

To evaluate the Urdu storytelling capability of contemporary
Large Language Models (LLMs), we constructed a corpus of
\textbf{31 story titles} drawn from four complementary
sources, summarised in Table~\ref{tab:title_distribution}:

\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Translated Short Story Titles (TSST,
          N~=~11).} Titles of canonical English short
          fiction (e.g.\ Shirley Jackson's \emph{The Lottery},
          W.~W.~Jacobs's \emph{The Monkey's Paw}, Charlotte
          Perkins Gilman's \emph{The Yellow Wallpaper},
          Washington Irving's \emph{The Legend of Sleepy
          Hollow}, Kate Chopin's \emph{The Story of an Hour},
          Frank R.~Stockton's \emph{The Lady or the Tiger},
          Ernest Hemingway's \emph{The Snows of Kilimanjaro},
          Alice Munro's \emph{Boys and Girls}, Anton Chekhov's
          \emph{The Letter}, John Godfrey Saxe's
          \emph{The Blind Men and the Elephant}, and
          Maupassant's \emph{The Confession~/~Regret}). Where
          a published Urdu rendering of the title was already
          in circulation (e.g.\ \foreignlanguage{arabic}{لاٹری},
          \foreignlanguage{arabic}{بندر کا پنجہ}), we adopted that
          rendering verbatim. Otherwise, two native Urdu
          speakers independently produced candidate translations
          and resolved disagreements through discussion, with
          a back-translation step (Urdu~$\rightarrow$~English)
          used to verify semantic preservation.

    \item \textbf{Urdu Short Story Titles (UST, N~=~11).}
          Native Urdu titles from canonical
          twentieth-century South-Asian writers, used
          \emph{verbatim} to retain literary and cultural
          authenticity. Authors include Saadat Hasan Manto
          (\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ٹھنڈا گوشت}), Ismat Chughtai
          (\foreignlanguage{arabic}{لحاف}), Ghulam Abbas
          (\foreignlanguage{arabic}{اوور کوٹ}), Rajinder Singh Bedi
          (\foreignlanguage{arabic}{گرم کوٹ}), Qurratulain Hyder
          (\foreignlanguage{arabic}{آگ کا دریا}), and
          Rokeya Sakhawat Hossain
          (\foreignlanguage{arabic}{سلطانہ کا خواب}), among others.

    \item \textbf{Fables (N~=~3).} Universally recognised
          Indo-Pakistani moral tales taught in school
          curricula (\foreignlanguage{arabic}{خرگوش اور کچھوا},
          \foreignlanguage{arabic}{پیاسا کوا}, \foreignlanguage{arabic}{لالچی کتا}, etc.).
          These provide a control condition: titles whose
          plots are heavily memorised but whose lexical
          surface is short, allowing us to disentangle
          \emph{plot memorisation} from \emph{title-token
          anchoring} (see Section~\ref{subsec:trr}).

    \item \textbf{Neutral Urdu Titles (NUT, N~=~6).}
          Author-original titles composed by the research
          team in idiomatic Urdu (e.g.\ \foreignlanguage{arabic}{ایمان کا سفر},
          \foreignlanguage{arabic}{آخری ٹرین}, \foreignlanguage{arabic}{سوات کی شام}). These are
          not associated with any published work, providing
          an \emph{unseen-prompt} control to test whether
          observed memorisation effects persist when no
          ground-truth source story exists.
\end{enumerate}

\begin{table}[h]
\centering\small
\begin{tabular}{lcc}
\toprule
\textbf{Source} & \textbf{Abbrev.} & \textbf{\#} \\
\midrule
Translated Short Story Titles & TSST  & 11 \\
Urdu Short Story Titles       & UST   & 11 \\
Fables                        & Fable & 3  \\
Neutral Urdu Titles           & NUT   & 6  \\
\midrule
\textbf{Total}                &       & \textbf{31} \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\caption{Distribution of story titles across the four
source categories. The full title list with authors and
ALA-LC transliterations appears in
Appendix~\ref{app:titles} (Table~\ref{tab:avg_repetition_cases}).}
\label{tab:title_distribution}
\end{table}

The four-way split is intentional: TSST tests
cross-cultural transfer (English-origin plots rendered in
an Urdu cultural frame), UST tests whether models reproduce
or reinterpret canonical Urdu literature, Fable controls
for plot memorisation in absence of literary metaphor, and
NUT controls for memorisation in absence of any prior text.
The corpus size (N~=~31) was selected to balance breadth
of stylistic coverage with the depth required for
sentence-level human annotation of all 93 resulting
generations.

\subsection{Generation Protocol}
\label{subsec:generation}

Three contemporary LLMs were evaluated:
\textbf{GPT}~(\texttt{<model-version>}, accessed
\texttt{<YYYY-MM-DD>}),
\textbf{QWEN}~(\texttt{<Qwen-version>}, accessed
\texttt{<YYYY-MM-DD>}), and
\textbf{DeepSeek}~(\texttt{<DeepSeek-version>}, accessed
\texttt{<YYYY-MM-DD>}). Each title was issued to every
model exactly \textbf{once} using a single fixed zero-shot
prompt, deliberately omitting few-shot exemplars or
chain-of-thought scaffolding so that observed differences
are attributable to model capacity rather than prompt design:

\begin{quote}\itshape
``Write a 2000-word Urdu story in a Pakistani cultural
context. The title is \foreignlanguage{arabic}{<title>}.''
\end{quote}

Generation parameters were held constant across models:
\texttt{temperature=<x>}, \texttt{top\_p=<x>},
\texttt{max\_tokens=<x>}. The 2000-word target was
specified to elicit extended narrative behaviour;
realised lengths (Table~\ref{tab:trr_overall}) ranged
from \texttt{<min>}--\texttt{<max>} words, and
length-compliance is reported as a finding rather than a
filter. Owing to the stochastic nature of LLM decoding,
identical re-execution of this protocol will not
reproduce the exact generated texts; we therefore release
all 93 generated stories, the prompts, and the
sentence-level annotations to enable downstream
verification (Section~\ref{sec:reproducibility}).

\label{subsec:title_corpus}

To evaluate the Urdu storytelling capability of contemporary

\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Translated Short Story Titles (TSST,

          rendering verbatim. Otherwise, two native Urdu
          speakers independently produced candidate translations
          and resolved disagreements through discussion, with
          a back-translation step (Urdu~$\rightarrow$~English)
          used to verify semantic preservation.

    \item \textbf{Urdu Short Story Titles (UST, N~=~11).}
          Native Urdu titles from canonical
          twentieth-century South-Asian writers, used
          \emph{verbatim} to retain literary and cultural
          authenticity. Authors include Saadat Hasan Manto
          (\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ٹھنڈا گوشت}), Ismat Chughtai
          (\foreignlanguage{arabic}{لحاف}), Ghulam Abbas
          (\foreignlanguage{arabic}{اوور کوٹ}), Rajinder Singh Bedi

          (\foreignlanguage{arabic}{آگ کا دریا}), and
          Rokeya Sakhawat Hossain

          Indo-Pakistani moral tales taught in school

          \foreignlanguage{arabic}{پیاسا کوا}, \foreignlanguage{arabic}{لالچی کتا}, etc.).
          These provide a control condition: titles whose
          plots are heavily memorised but whose lexical
          surface is short, allowing us to disentangle
          \emph{plot memorisation} from \emph{title-token
          anchoring} (see Section~\ref{subsec:trr}).

    \item \textbf{Neutral Urdu Titles (NUT, N~=~6).}
          Author-original titles composed by the research
          team in idiomatic Urdu (e.g.\ \foreignlanguage{arabic}{ایمان کا سفر},
          \foreignlanguage{arabic}{آخری ٹرین}, \foreignlanguage{arabic}{سوات کی شام}). These are
          not associated with any published work, providing
          an \emph{unseen-prompt} control to test whether
          observed memorisation effects persist when no
          ground-truth source story exists.
\end{enumerate}

\begin{table}[h]
\centering\small
\begin{tabular}{lcc}
\toprule
\textbf{Source} & \textbf{Abbrev.} & \textbf{\#} \\
\midrule
Translated Short Story Titles & TSST  & 11 \\
Urdu Short Story Titles       & UST   & 11 \\

\midrule

\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\caption{Distribution of story titles across the four
source categories. The full title list with authors and
ALA-LC transliterations appears in
Appendix~\ref{app:titles} (Table~\ref{tab:avg_repetition_cases}).}
\label{tab:title_distribution}

The four-way split is intentional: TSST tests
cross-cultural transfer (English-origin plots rendered in
an Urdu cultural frame), UST tests whether models reproduce
or reinterpret canonical Urdu literature, Fable controls
for plot memorisation in absence of literary metaphor, and
NUT controls for memorisation in absence of any prior text.
The corpus size (N~=~31) was selected to balance breadth
of stylistic coverage with the depth required for
sentence-level human annotation of all 93 resulting
generations.

\subsection{Generation Protocol}
\label{subsec:generation}

Three contemporary LLMs were evaluated:
\textbf{GPT}~(\texttt{<model-version>}, accessed
\texttt{<YYYY-MM-DD>}),
\textbf{QWEN}~(\texttt{<Qwen-version>}, accessed
\texttt{<YYYY-MM-DD>}), and
\textbf{DeepSeek}~(\texttt{<DeepSeek-version>}, accessed
\texttt{<YYYY-MM-DD>}). Each title was issued to every
model exactly \textbf{once} using a single fixed zero-shot
prompt, deliberately omitting few-shot exemplars or
chain-of-thought scaffolding so that observed differences
are attributable to model capacity rather than prompt design:

\begin{quote}\itshape
``Write a 2000-word Urdu story in a Pakistani cultural
context. The title is \foreignlanguage{arabic}{<title>}.''
\end{quote}

Generation parameters were held constant across models:
\texttt{temperature=<x>}, \texttt{top\_p=<x>},
\texttt{max\_tokens=<x>}. The 2000-word target was
specified to elicit extended narrative behaviour;
realised lengths (Table~\ref{tab:trr_overall}) ranged
from \texttt{<min>}--\texttt{<max>} words, and
length-compliance is reported as a finding rather than a
filter. Owing to the stochastic nature of LLM decoding,
identical re-execution of this protocol will not
reproduce the exact generated texts; we therefore release
all 93 generated stories, the prompts, and the
sentence-level annotations to enable downstream
verification (Section~\ref{sec:reproducibility}).

\subsection{Preprocessing}
Generated stories were Unicode-normalised (NFC) with
Arabic-script characters mapped to their Urdu equivalents
(\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ي}\,$\rightarrow$\,\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ی},
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ك}\,$\rightarrow$\,\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ک}), tatweel and
diacritics stripped, and segmented into sentences using
the Urdu sentence terminators \foreignlanguage{arabic}{۔}, \texttt{!},
\texttt{?}, \texttt{.} followed by whitespace. All
downstream metrics are computed on this normalised form.

## 📄 Main text (concise)

```latex name=section_3_1_dataset.tex
\subsection{Story Title Corpus}
\label{subsec:title_corpus}

We constructed a corpus of \textbf{31 story titles} drawn
from four complementary sources
(Table~\ref{tab:title_distribution}): \emph{Translated Short
Story Titles} (TSST, N~=~11) from canonical English short
fiction (e.g.\ Jackson, Chekhov, Hemingway, Maupassant);
\emph{Urdu Short Story Titles} (UST, N~=~11) from canonical
twentieth-century South-Asian writers used \emph{verbatim}
(e.g.\ Manto, Chughtai, Ghulam Abbas, Bedi, Hyder);
\emph{Fables} (N~=~3), short Indo-Pakistani moral tales used
as a memorisation control; and \emph{Neutral Urdu Titles}
(NUT, N~=~6), author-original Urdu titles composed by the
research team as an unseen-prompt control. The complete
title list with authors, sources, and ALA-LC transliterations
is given in Appendix~\ref{app:titles}
(Table~\ref{tab:title_full_list}).

For TSST, we obtained an initial Urdu rendering of each
English title via the GPT-5.1 translation API and then
manually verified every translation with a native Urdu
speaker; published Urdu renderings (e.g.\
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{لاٹری}, \foreignlanguage{arabic}{بندر کا پنجہ})
were retained verbatim where they exist.

\begin{table}[h]
\centering\small
\begin{tabular}{lcc}
\toprule
\textbf{Source} & \textbf{Abbrev.} & \textbf{\#} \\
\midrule
Translated Short Story Titles & TSST  & 11 \\
Urdu Short Story Titles       & UST   & 11 \\
Fables                        & Fable & 3  \\
Neutral Urdu Titles           & NUT   & 6  \\
\midrule
\textbf{Total}                &       & \textbf{31} \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\caption{Distribution of story titles across the four
source categories.}
\label{tab:title_distribution}
\end{table}

The four-way split is intentional: TSST tests cross-cultural
transfer, UST tests reproduction vs.\ reinterpretation of
canonical Urdu literature, Fable controls for plot
memorisation, and NUT controls for memorisation in absence
of any prior text. Corpus size (N~=~31, yielding 93 generations
across three models) balances stylistic breadth with the
depth required for sentence-level human annotation.

\subsection{Generation Protocol}
\label{subsec:generation}

Three LLMs were evaluated: \textbf{GPT}
(\texttt{<version>}), \textbf{QWEN} (\texttt{<version>}),
and \textbf{DeepSeek} (\texttt{<version>}), all accessed
in \texttt{<MM-YYYY>}. Each title was issued to every model
exactly once using a fixed zero-shot prompt, with
\texttt{temperature=<x>}, \texttt{top\_p=<x>}, and
\texttt{max\_tokens=<x>} held constant:

\begin{quote}\itshape
``Write a 2000-word Urdu story in a Pakistani cultural
context. The title is \foreignlanguage{arabic}{<title>}.''
\end{quote}

Few-shot exemplars and chain-of-thought scaffolding were
deliberately omitted so that observed differences reflect
model capacity rather than prompt engineering. Owing to the
stochastic nature of LLM decoding, re-execution will not
reproduce the exact texts; all 93 generated stories,
prompts, and annotations are released to enable
verification (Section~\ref{sec:reproducibility}).

\subsection{Preprocessing}
Stories were Unicode-normalised (NFC) with Arabic-script
characters mapped to their Urdu equivalents
(\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ي}\,$\rightarrow$\,\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ی},
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ك}\,$\rightarrow$\,\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ک}), tatweel
and diacritics stripped, and sentence-segmented on
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{۔}, \texttt{!}, \texttt{?}, \texttt{.}
followed by whitespace.
```

---

## 📑 Appendix table (full title list with authors)

Place this in your appendix; it replaces / complements the existing `tab:avg_repetition_cases`.

## 📊 What statistics to include

### Must-have (per model)
| Statistic | Why |
|---|---|
| **# stories generated** (= 31) | Confirms parity across models |
| **Mean / median story length** (words) | Shows compliance with 2000-word target |
| **Min / max length** | Reveals truncation or over-generation |
| **Std. dev. of length** | Shows generation stability |
| **Total words** in corpus | Scale of analysis |
| **Mean # sentences per story** | Granularity for sentence-level annotation |
| **Total sentences** | Denominator for all error rates |
| **Length-compliance rate** (% stories within ±20% of 2000 words) | Direct prompt-adherence finding |

### Nice-to-have
| Statistic | Why |
|---|---|
| **Mean sentence length** (words) | Lets you compare model verbosity |
| **Type-Token Ratio (TTR)** or **MTLD** | Lexical diversity per model |
| **Vocabulary size** per model | Range of word choice |
| **Avg # paragraphs** | Narrative chunking |
| **Length by category** (TSST/UST/Fable/NUT) | Whether models behave differently per source |

### Skip (or save for later sections)
- Error counts → already in §Linguistic
- TRR → already in its own section
- Gender → already in its own section

## 🐍 Quick script to compute the numbers

Drop-in helper using your already-normalised stories folder:

```python name=corpus_stats.py
"""
Compute corpus statistics for Urdu LLM-generated stories.

Folder layout assumed:
    stories/
      gpt/        story_01.txt, story_02.txt, ...
      qwen/       ...
      deepseek/   ...

Optionally, a CSV mapping story_id -> category (TSST/UST/Fable/NUT).
"""
import re, statistics, csv
from pathlib import Path

ROOT = Path("./stories")
MODELS = ["gpt", "qwen", "deepseek"]
TARGET = 2000
TOL = 0.20  # ±20% compliance window

SENT_SPLIT = re.compile(r'(?<=[۔!\?\.])\s+')

def load_story(p):
    text = p.read_text(encoding="utf-8").strip()
    words = text.split()
    sents = [s.strip() for s in SENT_SPLIT.split(text.replace("\n", " ")) if s.strip()]
    return text, words, sents

def stats(values):
    return {
        "mean":   statistics.mean(values),
        "median": statistics.median(values),
        "min":    min(values),
        "max":    max(values),
        "std":    statistics.pstdev(values),
        "sum":    sum(values),
    }

def compliance(lengths, target=TARGET, tol=TOL):
    lo, hi = target * (1 - tol), target * (1 + tol)
    return sum(1 for L in lengths if lo <= L <= hi) / len(lengths) * 100

print(f"{'Model':<10}{'N':>4}{'MeanW':>8}{'Med':>6}{'Min':>6}{'Max':>6}"
      f"{'Std':>7}{'TotW':>9}{'MeanS':>7}{'TotS':>7}{'Comp%':>7}")
for m in MODELS:
    folder = ROOT / m
    files = sorted(folder.glob("*.txt"))
    word_lens, sent_counts, sent_lens = [], [], []
    for f in files:
        _, ws, ss = load_story(f)
        word_lens.append(len(ws))
        sent_counts.append(len(ss))
        sent_lens.extend(len(s.split()) for s in ss)
    sw = stats(word_lens); ss_ = stats(sent_counts)
    print(f"{m:<10}{len(files):>4}"
          f"{sw['mean']:>8.0f}{sw['median']:>6.0f}{sw['min']:>6}{sw['max']:>6}"
          f"{sw['std']:>7.0f}{sw['sum']:>9}"
          f"{ss_['mean']:>7.1f}{ss_['sum']:>7}"
          f"{compliance(word_lens):>7.1f}")
```

Run it, paste the printed numbers into the `<x>` placeholders, and your statistics subsection is done.

Here's the updated `corpus_stats.py` with **Urdu-specific sentence segmentation**. The key change: split primarily on Urdu full stop `۔` (U+06D4) plus Urdu question mark `؟` (U+061F) and Urdu exclamation, while being careful not to split on `.` inside numbers/abbreviations (which is common in mixed Urdu-English text).

```python name=corpus_stats.py
"""
Compute corpus statistics for Urdu LLM-generated stories.

Sentence segmentation uses Urdu-native terminators:
    ۔  U+06D4  Urdu full stop  (PRIMARY)
    ؟  U+061F  Arabic/Urdu question mark
    !            exclamation
    ?            ASCII question mark (fallback for mixed text)

The Latin period '.' is intentionally NOT used as a default
sentence break because in Urdu prose it almost always appears
inside numbers (e.g. 1.5), abbreviations, or English insertions
where it does NOT mark a sentence boundary.

Folder layout assumed:
    stories/
      gpt/        story_01.txt, story_02.txt, ...
      qwen/       ...
      deepseek/   ...
"""
import re, statistics
from pathlib import Path

ROOT = Path("./stories")
MODELS = ["gpt", "qwen", "deepseek"]
TARGET = 2000
TOL = 0.20  # ±20% compliance window

# ---------------------------------------------------------------
# Urdu sentence segmentation
# ---------------------------------------------------------------
URDU_SENT_TERMINATORS = "۔؟!?"
SENT_SPLIT_RE = re.compile(rf"(?<=[{URDU_SENT_TERMINATORS}])\s+")

# Urdu word characters (used for token validation, optional)
URDU_LETTER_RE = re.compile(
    r"[\u0600-\u06FF\u0750-\u077F\uFB50-\uFDFF\uFE70-\uFEFF]"
)


def normalize_text(text: str) -> str:
    """Light normalization before splitting:
       - collapse all whitespace (newlines, tabs) into single spaces
       - strip
    """
    text = text.replace("\r", " ").replace("\n", " ").replace("\t", " ")
    text = re.sub(r"\s+", " ", text)
    return text.strip()


def split_sentences(text: str):
    """
    Split on Urdu sentence terminators followed by whitespace.
    Also handles end-of-string sentences that have no trailing
    whitespace by checking final terminator.
    """
    text = normalize_text(text)
    if not text:
        return []

    # Primary split: terminator + whitespace
    parts = SENT_SPLIT_RE.split(text)

    # Some stories use ۔ at the very end without space, or
    # multiple ۔s without space between sentences. Handle that
    # by a secondary pass: split tokens that still contain
    # an internal ۔ followed by an Urdu letter (no space).
    final = []
    inner_re = re.compile(rf"(?<=[{URDU_SENT_TERMINATORS}])(?=\S)")
    for p in parts:
        sub = inner_re.split(p)
        final.extend(s.strip() for s in sub if s.strip())
    return final


def load_story(p: Path):
    text = p.read_text(encoding="utf-8")
    norm = normalize_text(text)
    words = norm.split()
    sents = split_sentences(text)
    return words, sents


def stats(values):
    return {
        "mean":   statistics.mean(values),
        "median": statistics.median(values),
        "min":    min(values),
        "max":    max(values),
        "std":    statistics.pstdev(values),
        "sum":    sum(values),
    }


def compliance(lengths, target=TARGET, tol=TOL):
    lo, hi = target * (1 - tol), target * (1 + tol)
    return sum(1 for L in lengths if lo <= L <= hi) / len(lengths) * 100


# ---------------------------------------------------------------
def main():
    print(f"{'Model':<10}{'N':>4}{'MeanW':>8}{'Med':>6}{'Min':>6}"
          f"{'Max':>6}{'Std':>7}{'TotW':>9}"
          f"{'MeanS':>7}{'TotS':>7}{'Comp%':>7}")

    for m in MODELS:
        folder = ROOT / m
        if not folder.is_dir():
            print(f"  [skip] folder not found: {folder}")
            continue
        files = sorted(folder.glob("*.txt"))
        if not files:
            print(f"  [skip] no .txt files in {folder}")
            continue

        word_lens, sent_counts = [], []
        for f in files:
            ws, ss = load_story(f)
            word_lens.append(len(ws))
            sent_counts.append(len(ss))

        sw = stats(word_lens)
        ss_ = stats(sent_counts)
        print(f"{m:<10}{len(files):>4}"
              f"{sw['mean']:>8.0f}{sw['median']:>6.0f}"
              f"{sw['min']:>6}{sw['max']:>6}{sw['std']:>7.0f}"
              f"{sw['sum']:>9}"
              f"{ss_['mean']:>7.1f}{ss_['sum']:>7}"
              f"{compliance(word_lens):>7.1f}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

---

## 🔍 What changed vs. the previous version

| Aspect | Before | After |
|---|---|---|
| Primary terminator | `۔ ! ? .` | **`۔ ؟ ! ?`** (Urdu-native, no Latin `.`) |
| Why drop `.` | Was splitting on numbers like `1.5` | Latin period rarely marks Urdu sentence end |
| Added `؟` (U+061F) | Not handled | **Now handled** — Urdu question mark |
| End-of-string sentences | Could be missed if no trailing space | **Secondary pass** catches `۔` not followed by whitespace |
| Whitespace handling | `\r\n` → space | Also `\t` → space, all collapsed to single |

---

## ⚠️ Expected impact on your numbers

Your previous run used `۔!\?\.` so the `.` was already included. With the new code:

- **Mean sentences/story may drop slightly** for any story that contained numbers like "1947" or "2000." being mis-split as sentence ends.
- **Total sentences (9,460)** will likely change by a small amount (probably 1–3% lower).
- **Word counts unchanged** — only sentence segmentation differs.

Re-run and paste the new numbers; I'll update `tab:corpus_stats` and the `Mean sentences / story` row accordingly.

---

references.snippetText:
# ---------------------------------------------------------------
# Urdu sentence segmentation
# ---------------------------------------------------------------
URDU_SENT_TERMINATORS = "۔؟!?"
SENT_SPLIT_RE = re.compile(rf"(?<=[{URDU_SENT_TERMINATORS}])\s+")

# Urdu word characters (used for token validation, optional)
URDU_LETTER_RE = re.compile(
    r"[\u0600-\u06FF\u0750-\u077F\uFB50-\uFDFF\uFE70-\uFEFF]"
)


def normalize_text(text: str) -> str:
    """Light normalization before splitting:
       - collapse all whitespace (newlines, tabs) into single spaces
       - strip
    """
    text = text.replace("\r", " ").replace("\n", " ").replace("\t", " ")
    text = re.sub(r"\s+", " ", text)
    return text.strip()


def split_sentences(text: str):
    """
    Split on Urdu sentence terminators followed by whitespace.
    Also handles end-of-string sentences that have no trailing
    whitespace by checking final terminator.
    """
    text = normalize_text(text)
    if not text:
        return []

    # Primary split: terminator + whitespace
    parts = SENT_SPLIT_RE.split(text)

    # Some stories use ۔ at the very end without space, or
    # multiple ۔s without space between sentences. Handle that
    # by a secondary pass: split tokens that still contain
    # an internal ۔ followed by an Urdu letter (no space).
    final = []
    inner_re = re.compile(rf"(?<=[{URDU_SENT_TERMINATORS}])(?=\S)")
    for p in parts:
        sub = inner_re.split(p)
        final.extend(s.strip() for s in sub if s.strip())
    return final


def load_story(p: Path):
    text = p.read_text(encoding="utf-8")
    norm = normalize_text(text)
    words = norm.split()
    sents = split_sentences(text)
    return words, sents


def stats(values):
    return {
        "mean":   statistics.mean(values),
        "median": statistics.median(values),
        "min":    min(values),
        "max":    max(values),
        "std":    statistics.pstdev(values),
        "sum":    sum(values),
    }


def compliance(lengths, target=TARGET, tol=TOL):
    lo, hi = target * (1 - tol), target * (1 + tol)
    return sum(1 for L in lengths if lo <= L <= hi) / len(lengths) * 100


# ---------------------------------------------------------------
def main():
    print(f"{'Model':<10}{'N':>4}{'MeanW':>8}{'Med':>6}{'Min':>6}"
          f"{'Max':>6}{'Std':>7}{'TotW':>9}"
          f"{'MeanS':>7}{'TotS':>7}{'Comp%':>7}")

    for m in MODELS:
        folder = ROOT / m
        if not folder.is_dir():
            print(f"  [skip] folder not found: {folder}")
            continue
        files = sorted(folder.glob("*.txt"))
        if not files:
            print(f"  [skip] no .txt files in {folder}")
            continue

        word_lens, sent_counts = [], []
        for f in files:
            ws, ss = load_story(f)
            word_lens.append(len(ws))
            sent_counts.append(len(ss))

        sw = stats(word_lens)
        ss_ = stats(sent_counts)
        print(f"{m:<10}{len(files):>4}"
              f"{sw['mean']:>8.0f}{sw['median']:>6.0f}"
              f"{sw['min']:>6}{sw['max']:>6}{sw['std']:>7.0f}"
              f"{sw['sum']:>9}"
              f"{ss_['mean']:>7.1f}{ss_['sum']:>7}"
              f"{compliance(word_lens):>7.1f}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

messages.content:
Good catch — **abhi check nahi laga**. Current code `۔۔۔` ko 3 sentences ginega kyunki secondary pass har `۔` pe split kar deta hai. Fix simple hai: split se pehle consecutive terminators ko ek hi terminator me collapse kar do.

Here's the patch:

```python name=corpus_stats.py
# ---------------------------------------------------------------
# Urdu sentence segmentation
# ---------------------------------------------------------------
URDU_SENT_TERMINATORS = "۔؟!?"

# Matches 2+ consecutive terminators (optionally separated by spaces),
# e.g.  ۔۔۔   ۔ ۔ ۔   !!!   ؟؟   !?
MULTI_TERM_RE = re.compile(
    rf"([{URDU_SENT_TERMINATORS}])(?:\s*[{URDU_SENT_TERMINATORS}])+"
)

SENT_SPLIT_RE = re.compile(rf"(?<=[{URDU_SENT_TERMINATORS}])\s+")

URDU_LETTER_RE = re.compile(
    r"[\u0600-\u06FF\u0750-\u077F\uFB50-\uFDFF\uFE70-\uFEFF]"
)


def normalize_text(text: str) -> str:
    """Light normalization before splitting:
       - collapse newlines/tabs into spaces
       - collapse runs of sentence terminators (۔۔۔, !!!, ؟؟ etc.)
         into a SINGLE terminator so they are not counted as
         multiple sentence boundaries
       - collapse repeated whitespace
    """
    text = text.replace("\r", " ").replace("\n", " ").replace("\t", " ")
    # Collapse ۔۔۔ / !!! / ؟؟ / ۔ ۔ ۔  -> single first terminator
    text = MULTI_TERM_RE.sub(r"\1", text)
    text = re.sub(r"\s+", " ", text)
    return text.strip()


def split_sentences(text: str):
    """Split on Urdu sentence terminators followed by whitespace.
    Multi-terminator runs (۔۔۔ etc.) are collapsed in normalize_text,
    so they yield exactly one sentence break."""
    text = normalize_text(text)
    if not text:
        return []

    parts = SENT_SPLIT_RE.split(text)

    # Secondary pass: handle terminator immediately followed by a
    # non-space character (e.g. "ہے۔پھر" -> two sentences).
    inner_re = re.compile(rf"(?<=[{URDU_SENT_TERMINATORS}])(?=\S)")
    final = []
    for p in parts:
        sub = inner_re.split(p)
        final.extend(s.strip() for s in sub if s.strip())
    return final
```

---

## ✅ Yeh ab kya handle karta hai

| Input | Pehle (galat) | Ab (sahi) |
|---|---|---|
| `وہ بولا۔۔۔ پھر چلا گیا۔` | 4 sentences | **2 sentences** |
| `کیا؟؟ ہاں!` | 3 sentences | **2 sentences** |
| `ٹھیک ہے۔ ۔ ۔ شاید` | 4 sentences | **2 sentences** |
| `بات یہ ہے۔پھر وہ بولا۔` (no space) | 2 sentences | **2 sentences** ✓ |
| `سنو!!! دوڑو!` | 3 sentences | **2 sentences** |

```
import json
import re
import pandas as pd
from collections import defaultdict
from sklearn.metrics import cohen_kappa_score

# ─────────────────────────────────────────
# CONFIGURATION
# ─────────────────────────────────────────
MY_FILES = {
    "GPT"      : "./stories/annotated_stories/GPT_annotations.json",
    "DeepSeek" : "./stories/annotated_stories/DeepSeek_annotations.json",
    "QWEN"     : "./stories/annotated_stories/QWEN_annotations.json",
}
ANNOTATOR_FILES = {
    "GPT"      : "./stories/annotator/najm/GPT_annotations.json",
    "DeepSeek" : "./stories/annotator/najm/DeepSeek_annotations.json",
    "QWEN"     : "./stories/annotator/najm/QWEN_annotations.json",
}
OUT_EXCEL           = "inter_annotator_agreement_najm.xlsx"
EXCLUDE_LABELS      = {"English Hyphen"}
TEXT_MATCH_LABELS   = {"City", "PER"}   # text match + story level unique

# ─────────────────────────────────────────
# HELPERS
# ─────────────────────────────────────────
def clean_story_id(raw):
    return re.sub(r'^[0-9a-f]{8}-', '', raw.strip(), flags=re.IGNORECASE)

def get_spans(task):
    """Extract spans — exclude unwanted labels."""
    spans = []
    seen  = set()
    for ann in task.get("annotations", []):
        if ann.get("was_cancelled"):
            continue
        for res in ann.get("result", []):
            val   = res.get("value", {})
            label = val.get("labels", [""])[0] if val.get("labels") else ""
            if label in EXCLUDE_LABELS:
                continue
            if "start" in val and "end" in val:
                text = val.get("text", "").strip()
                key  = (text, label)
                if key in seen:
                    continue
                seen.add(key)
                spans.append({
                    "start" : val["start"],
                    "end"   : val["end"],
                    "text"  : text,
                    "label" : label,
                })
    return spans

# ─────────────────────────────────────────
# COMPARE — two strategies
# ─────────────────────────────────────────
def compare_text_match(spans_a, spans_b):
    """
    PER / City — match by text + label.
    Position ignored (story level).
    """
    results = []
    used_b  = set()

    for sa in spans_a:
        matched = False
        for i, sb in enumerate(spans_b):
            if i in used_b:
                continue
            same_text  = sa["text"]  == sb["text"]
            same_label = sa["label"] == sb["label"]
            overlap    = (sa["start"] < sb["end"]) and (sb["start"] < sa["end"])

            if same_text and same_label:
                status = "Exact Match"
            elif same_text and not same_label:
                status = "Partial Match (label differs)"
            elif overlap and same_label and not same_text:
                status = "Partial Match (span differs)"
            else:
                continue

            results.append({
                "My Text"      : sa["text"],
                "My Label"     : sa["label"],
                "Ann Text"     : sb["text"],
                "Ann Label"    : sb["label"],
                "Match Status" : status,
            })
            used_b.add(i)
            matched = True
            break

        if not matched:
            results.append({
                "My Text"      : sa["text"],
                "My Label"     : sa["label"],
                "Ann Text"     : "",
                "Ann Label"    : "",
                "Match Status" : "Only Me",
            })

    for i, sb in enumerate(spans_b):
        if i not in used_b:
            results.append({
                "My Text"      : "",
                "My Label"     : "",
                "Ann Text"     : sb["text"],
                "Ann Label"    : sb["label"],
                "Match Status" : "Only Annotator",
            })

    return results


def compare_label_match(spans_a, spans_b):
    """
    Other labels — same label in same paragraph = Exact Match.
    Text match not required.
    """
    results = []
    used_b  = set()

    for sa in spans_a:
        matched = False
        for i, sb in enumerate(spans_b):
            if i in used_b:
                continue
            if sa["label"] == sb["label"]:
                results.append({
                    "My Text"      : sa["text"],
                    "My Label"     : sa["label"],
                    "Ann Text"     : sb["text"],
                    "Ann Label"    : sb["label"],
                    "Match Status" : "Exact Match",
                })
                used_b.add(i)
                matched = True
                break

        if not matched:
            results.append({
                "My Text"      : sa["text"],
                "My Label"     : sa["label"],
                "Ann Text"     : "",
                "Ann Label"    : "",
                "Match Status" : "Only Me",
            })

    for i, sb in enumerate(spans_b):
        if i not in used_b:
            results.append({
                "My Text"      : "",
                "My Label"     : "",
                "Ann Text"     : sb["text"],
                "Ann Label"    : sb["label"],
                "Match Status" : "Only Annotator",
            })

    return results


def build_lookup(json_file):
    """Build lookup: (story_id, paragraph_text) → task"""
    with open(json_file, encoding="utf-8") as f:
        data = json.load(f)
    lookup = {}
    for task in data:
        story_id = clean_story_id(task.get("file_upload", ""))
        text     = task.get("data", {}).get("text", "").strip()
        if story_id and text:
            lookup[(story_id, text)] = task
    return lookup


def compute_kappa(span_results):
    """
    Cohen's Kappa.
    Exact Match = both agree (1,1)
    Only Me     = I marked, ann didn't (1,0)
    Only Ann    = ann marked, I didn't (0,1)
    Partial     = both marked but differ (1,0)
    """
    y_me  = []
    y_ann = []

    for r in span_results:
        status = r["Match Status"]
        if status == "Exact Match":
            y_me.append(1);  y_ann.append(1)
        elif status == "Only Me":
            y_me.append(1);  y_ann.append(0)
        elif status == "Only Annotator":
            y_me.append(0);  y_ann.append(1)
        else:   # Partial
            y_me.append(1);  y_ann.append(0)

    if len(set(y_me + y_ann)) < 2:
        return 1.0   # perfect agreement

    return round(cohen_kappa_score(y_me, y_ann), 4)


def interpret_kappa(k):
    if   k >= 0.81: return "Almost Perfect"
    elif k >= 0.61: return "Substantial"
    elif k >= 0.41: return "Moderate"
    elif k >= 0.21: return "Fair"
    elif k >= 0.01: return "Slight"
    else:           return "Poor"

# ─────────────────────────────────────────
# MAIN PROCESSING
# ─────────────────────────────────────────
label_rows   = defaultdict(list)
summary_rows = []

for model in ["GPT", "DeepSeek", "QWEN"]:
    print(f"\n{'='*55}")
    print(f"Processing: {model}")
    print(f"{'='*55}")

    my_lookup  = build_lookup(MY_FILES[model])
    ann_lookup = build_lookup(ANNOTATOR_FILES[model])
    common     = set(my_lookup.keys()) & set(ann_lookup.keys())

    print(f"My tasks     : {len(my_lookup)}")
    print(f"Ann tasks    : {len(ann_lookup)}")
    print(f"Common tasks : {len(common)}")

    # ── Story level: PER + City (unique, text match) ─────
    story_text_spans = defaultdict(lambda: {"my": [], "ann": []})
    story_seen       = defaultdict(lambda: {"my": set(), "ann": set()})

    # ── Task level: other labels (label match per paragraph)
    task_other_results = []   # list of (story_id, results)

    for key in common:
        story_id, _ = key
        my_task     = my_lookup[key]
        ann_task    = ann_lookup[key]

        my_spans  = get_spans(my_task)
        ann_spans = get_spans(ann_task)

        # Split by strategy
        my_text   = [s for s in my_spans  if s["label"] in TEXT_MATCH_LABELS]
        ann_text  = [s for s in ann_spans if s["label"] in TEXT_MATCH_LABELS]
        my_other  = [s for s in my_spans  if s["label"] not in TEXT_MATCH_LABELS]
        ann_other = [s for s in ann_spans if s["label"] not in TEXT_MATCH_LABELS]

        # PER/City → story level unique
        for span in my_text:
            uk = (span["text"], span["label"])
            if uk not in story_seen[story_id]["my"]:
                story_seen[story_id]["my"].add(uk)
                story_text_spans[story_id]["my"].append(span)

        for span in ann_text:
            uk = (span["text"], span["label"])
            if uk not in story_seen[story_id]["ann"]:
                story_seen[story_id]["ann"].add(uk)
                story_text_spans[story_id]["ann"].append(span)

        # Other labels → paragraph level
        if my_other or ann_other:
            results = compare_label_match(my_other, ann_other)
            task_other_results.append((story_id, results))

    # ── Compare PER/City at story level ──────────────────
    story_all_results = defaultdict(list)

    for story_id, spans in story_text_spans.items():
        results = compare_text_match(spans["my"], spans["ann"])
        story_all_results[story_id].extend(results)

    # ── Merge paragraph-level results ────────────────────
    for story_id, results in task_other_results:
        story_all_results[story_id].extend(results)

    # ── Build label rows + summary ────────────────────────
    for story_id, span_results in story_all_results.items():

        for r in span_results:
            label = r["My Label"] or r["Ann Label"]
            label_rows[label].append({
                "Model"        : model,
                "Story ID"     : story_id,
                "My Text"      : r["My Text"],
                "Ann Text"     : r["Ann Text"],
                "Match Status" : r["Match Status"],
            })

        total   = len(span_results)
        exact   = sum(1 for r in span_results if r["Match Status"] == "Exact Match")
        partial = sum(1 for r in span_results if "Partial" in r["Match Status"])
        only_me = sum(1 for r in span_results if r["Match Status"] == "Only Me")
        only_an = sum(1 for r in span_results if r["Match Status"] == "Only Annotator")
        kappa   = compute_kappa(span_results)

        print(f"  {story_id:<25} Total:{total:>3} | "
              f"Exact:{exact:>3} | Partial:{partial:>3} | "
              f"OnlyMe:{only_me:>3} | OnlyAnn:{only_an:>3} | "
              f"Kappa:{kappa:>6}")

        summary_rows.append({
            "Model"          : model,
            "Story ID"       : story_id,
            "Total Spans"    : total,
            "Exact Match"    : exact,
            "Partial Match"  : partial,
            "Only Me"        : only_me,
            "Only Annotator" : only_an,
            "Agreement %"    : round(exact / total * 100, 1) if total > 0 else 0,
            "Cohen's Kappa"  : kappa,
            "Interpretation" : interpret_kappa(kappa),
        })

# ─────────────────────────────────────────
# KAPPA SUMMARY — per model + overall
# ─────────────────────────────────────────
df_summary = pd.DataFrame(summary_rows)

print(f"\n{'='*60}")
print("COHEN'S KAPPA SUMMARY")
print(f"{'='*60}")
print(f"{'Model':<12} {'Stories':>8} {'Avg Kappa':>12} {'Interpretation':>20}")
print(f"{'-'*60}")

for model in ["GPT", "DeepSeek", "QWEN"]:
    sub   = df_summary[df_summary["Model"] == model]
    avg_k = round(sub["Cohen's Kappa"].mean(), 4)
    print(f"{model:<12} {len(sub):>8} {avg_k:>12} {interpret_kappa(avg_k):>20}")

overall_k = round(df_summary["Cohen's Kappa"].mean(), 4)
print(f"{'-'*60}")
print(f"{'Overall':<12} {len(df_summary):>8} {overall_k:>12} "
      f"{interpret_kappa(overall_k):>20}")

# ─────────────────────────────────────────
# SAVE EXCEL — Summary + one sheet per label
# ─────────────────────────────────────────
with pd.ExcelWriter(OUT_EXCEL, engine="openpyxl") as writer:

    # Sheet 1 — Summary with Kappa
    df_summary.to_excel(writer, sheet_name="Summary", index=False)

    # One sheet per label
    for label in sorted(label_rows.keys()):
        df_label = pd.DataFrame(label_rows[label])[[
            "Model", "Story ID", "My Text", "Ann Text", "Match Status"
        ]]
        sheet_name = label[:31] if label else "Unknown"
        df_label.to_excel(writer, sheet_name=sheet_name, index=False)

print(f"\n✅ Sheets : Summary + {sorted(label_rows.keys())}")
print(f"✅ Saved  : {OUT_EXCEL}")
```

from sklearn.metrics import cohen_kappa_score
