```
# CONFIGURATION
# ─────────────────────────────────────────
MY_FILES = {
    "GPT"      : "./stories/annotated_stories/GPT_annotations.json",
    "DeepSeek" : "./stories/annotated_stories/DeepSeek_annotations.json",
    "QWEN"     : "./stories/annotated_stories/QWEN_annotations.json",
}
ANNOTATOR_FILES = {
    "GPT"      : "./stories/annotator/najm/GPT_annotations.json",
    "DeepSeek" : "./stories/annotator/najm/DeepSeek_annotations.json",
    "QWEN"     : "./stories/annotator/najm/QWEN_annotations.json",
}
OUT_EXCEL           = "inter_annotator_agreement_najm.xlsx"
EXCLUDE_LABELS      = {"English Hyphen"}
TEXT_MATCH_LABELS   = {"City", "PER"}   # text match + story level unique

# ─────────────────────────────────────────
# HELPERS
# ─────────────────────────────────────────
def clean_story_id(raw):
    return re.sub(r'^[0-9a-f]{8}-', '', raw.strip(), flags=re.IGNORECASE)

def get_spans(task):
    """Extract spans — exclude unwanted labels."""
    spans = []
    seen  = set()
    for ann in task.get("annotations", []):
        if ann.get("was_cancelled"):
            continue
        for res in ann.get("result", []):
            val   = res.get("value", {})
            label = val.get("labels", [""])[0] if val.get("labels") else ""
            if label in EXCLUDE_LABELS:
                continue
            if "start" in val and "end" in val:
                text = val.get("text", "").strip()
                key  = (text, label)
                if key in seen:
                    continue
                seen.add(key)
                spans.append({
                    "start" : val["start"],
                    "end"   : val["end"],
                    "text"  : text,
                    "label" : label,
                })
    return spans

# ─────────────────────────────────────────
# COMPARE — two strategies
# ─────────────────────────────────────────
def compare_text_match(spans_a, spans_b):
    """
    PER / City — match by text + label.
    Position ignored (story level).
    """
    results = []
    used_b  = set()

    for sa in spans_a:
        matched = False
        for i, sb in enumerate(spans_b):
            if i in used_b:
                continue
            same_text  = sa["text"]  == sb["text"]
            same_label = sa["label"] == sb["label"]
            overlap    = (sa["start"] < sb["end"]) and (sb["start"] < sa["end"])

            if same_text and same_label:
                status = "Exact Match"
            elif same_text and not same_label:
                status = "Partial Match (label differs)"
            elif overlap and same_label and not same_text:
                status = "Partial Match (span differs)"
            else:
                continue

            results.append({
                "My Text"      : sa["text"],
                "My Label"     : sa["label"],
                "Ann Text"     : sb["text"],
                "Ann Label"    : sb["label"],
                "Match Status" : status,
            })
            used_b.add(i)
            matched = True
            break

        if not matched:
            results.append({
                "My Text"      : sa["text"],
                "My Label"     : sa["label"],
                "Ann Text"     : "",
                "Ann Label"    : "",
                "Match Status" : "Only Me",
            })

    for i, sb in enumerate(spans_b):
        if i not in used_b:
            results.append({
                "My Text"      : "",
                "My Label"     : "",
                "Ann Text"     : sb["text"],
                "Ann Label"    : sb["label"],
                "Match Status" : "Only Annotator",
            })

    return results


def compare_label_match(spans_a, spans_b):
    """
    Other labels — same label in same paragraph = Exact Match.
    Text match not required.
    """
    results = []
    used_b  = set()

    for sa in spans_a:
        matched = False
        for i, sb in enumerate(spans_b):
            if i in used_b:
                continue
            if sa["label"] == sb["label"]:
                results.append({
                    "My Text"      : sa["text"],
                    "My Label"     : sa["label"],
                    "Ann Text"     : sb["text"],
                    "Ann Label"    : sb["label"],
                    "Match Status" : "Exact Match",
                })
                used_b.add(i)
                matched = True
                break

        if not matched:
            results.append({
                "My Text"      : sa["text"],
                "My Label"     : sa["label"],
                "Ann Text"     : "",
                "Ann Label"    : "",
                "Match Status" : "Only Me",
            })

    for i, sb in enumerate(spans_b):
        if i not in used_b:
            results.append({
                "My Text"      : "",
                "My Label"     : "",
                "Ann Text"     : sb["text"],
                "Ann Label"    : sb["label"],
                "Match Status" : "Only Annotator",
            })

    return results


def build_lookup(json_file):
    """Build lookup: (story_id, paragraph_text) → task"""
    with open(json_file, encoding="utf-8") as f:
        data = json.load(f)
    lookup = {}
    for task in data:
        story_id = clean_story_id(task.get("file_upload", ""))
        text     = task.get("data", {}).get("text", "").strip()
        if story_id and text:
            lookup[(story_id, text)] = task
    return lookup


def compute_kappa(span_results):
    """
    Cohen's Kappa.
    Exact Match = both agree (1,1)
    Only Me     = I marked, ann didn't (1,0)
    Only Ann    = ann marked, I didn't (0,1)
    Partial     = both marked but differ (1,0)
    """
    y_me  = []
    y_ann = []

    for r in span_results:
        status = r["Match Status"]
        if status == "Exact Match":
            y_me.append(1);  y_ann.append(1)
        elif status == "Only Me":
            y_me.append(1);  y_ann.append(0)
        elif status == "Only Annotator":
            y_me.append(0);  y_ann.append(1)
        else:   # Partial
            y_me.append(1);  y_ann.append(0)

    if len(set(y_me + y_ann)) < 2:
        return 1.0   # perfect agreement

    return round(cohen_kappa_score(y_me, y_ann), 4)


def interpret_kappa(k):
    if   k >= 0.81: return "Almost Perfect"
    elif k >= 0.61: return "Substantial"
    elif k >= 0.41: return "Moderate"
    elif k >= 0.21: return "Fair"
    elif k >= 0.01: return "Slight"
    else:           return "Poor"

# ─────────────────────────────────────────
# MAIN PROCESSING
# ─────────────────────────────────────────
label_rows   = defaultdict(list)
summary_rows = []

for model in ["GPT", "DeepSeek", "QWEN"]:
    print(f"\n{'='*55}")
    print(f"Processing: {model}")
    print(f"{'='*55}")

    my_lookup  = build_lookup(MY_FILES[model])
    ann_lookup = build_lookup(ANNOTATOR_FILES[model])
    common     = set(my_lookup.keys()) & set(ann_lookup.keys())

    print(f"My tasks     : {len(my_lookup)}")
    print(f"Ann tasks    : {len(ann_lookup)}")
    print(f"Common tasks : {len(common)}")

    # ── Story level: PER + City (unique, text match) ─────
    story_text_spans = defaultdict(lambda: {"my": [], "ann": []})
    story_seen       = defaultdict(lambda: {"my": set(), "ann": set()})

    # ── Task level: other labels (label match per paragraph)
    task_other_results = []   # list of (story_id, results)

    for key in common:
        story_id, _ = key
        my_task     = my_lookup[key]
        ann_task    = ann_lookup[key]

        my_spans  = get_spans(my_task)
        ann_spans = get_spans(ann_task)

        # Split by strategy
        my_text   = [s for s in my_spans  if s["label"] in TEXT_MATCH_LABELS]
        ann_text  = [s for s in ann_spans if s["label"] in TEXT_MATCH_LABELS]
        my_other  = [s for s in my_spans  if s["label"] not in TEXT_MATCH_LABELS]
        ann_other = [s for s in ann_spans if s["label"] not in TEXT_MATCH_LABELS]

        # PER/City → story level unique
        for span in my_text:
            uk = (span["text"], span["label"])
            if uk not in story_seen[story_id]["my"]:
                story_seen[story_id]["my"].add(uk)
                story_text_spans[story_id]["my"].append(span)

        for span in ann_text:
            uk = (span["text"], span["label"])
            if uk not in story_seen[story_id]["ann"]:
                story_seen[story_id]["ann"].add(uk)
                story_text_spans[story_id]["ann"].append(span)

        # Other labels → paragraph level
        if my_other or ann_other:
            results = compare_label_match(my_other, ann_other)
            task_other_results.append((story_id, results))

    # ── Compare PER/City at story level ──────────────────
    story_all_results = defaultdict(list)

    for story_id, spans in story_text_spans.items():
        results = compare_text_match(spans["my"], spans["ann"])
        story_all_results[story_id].extend(results)

    # ── Merge paragraph-level results ────────────────────
    for story_id, results in task_other_results:
        story_all_results[story_id].extend(results)

    # ── Build label rows + summary ────────────────────────
    for story_id, span_results in story_all_results.items():

        for r in span_results:
            label = r["My Label"] or r["Ann Label"]
            label_rows[label].append({
                "Model"        : model,
                "Story ID"     : story_id,
                "My Text"      : r["My Text"],
                "Ann Text"     : r["Ann Text"],
                "Match Status" : r["Match Status"],
            })

        total   = len(span_results)
        exact   = sum(1 for r in span_results if r["Match Status"] == "Exact Match")
        partial = sum(1 for r in span_results if "Partial" in r["Match Status"])
        only_me = sum(1 for r in span_results if r["Match Status"] == "Only Me")
        only_an = sum(1 for r in span_results if r["Match Status"] == "Only Annotator")
        kappa   = compute_kappa(span_results)

        print(f"  {story_id:<25} Total:{total:>3} | "
              f"Exact:{exact:>3} | Partial:{partial:>3} | "
              f"OnlyMe:{only_me:>3} | OnlyAnn:{only_an:>3} | "
              f"Kappa:{kappa:>6}")

        summary_rows.append({
            "Model"          : model,
            "Story ID"       : story_id,
            "Total Spans"    : total,
            "Exact Match"    : exact,
            "Partial Match"  : partial,
            "Only Me"        : only_me,
            "Only Annotator" : only_an,
            "Agreement %"    : round(exact / total * 100, 1) if total > 0 else 0,
            "Cohen's Kappa"  : kappa,
            "Interpretation" : interpret_kappa(kappa),
        })

# ─────────────────────────────────────────
# KAPPA SUMMARY — per model + overall
# ─────────────────────────────────────────
df_summary = pd.DataFrame(summary_rows)

print(f"\n{'='*60}")
print("COHEN'S KAPPA SUMMARY")
print(f"{'='*60}")
print(f"{'Model':<12} {'Stories':>8} {'Avg Kappa':>12} {'Interpretation':>20}")
print(f"{'-'*60}")

for model in ["GPT", "DeepSeek", "QWEN"]:
    sub   = df_summary[df_summary["Model"] == model]
    avg_k = round(sub["Cohen's Kappa"].mean(), 4)
    print(f"{model:<12} {len(sub):>8} {avg_k:>12} {interpret_kappa(avg_k):>20}")

overall_k = round(df_summary["Cohen's Kappa"].mean(), 4)
print(f"{'-'*60}")
print(f"{'Overall':<12} {len(df_summary):>8} {overall_k:>12} "
      f"{interpret_kappa(overall_k):>20}")

# ─────────────────────────────────────────
# SAVE EXCEL — Summary + one sheet per label
# ─────────────────────────────────────────
with pd.ExcelWriter(OUT_EXCEL, engine="openpyxl") as writer:

    # Sheet 1 — Summary with Kappa
    df_summary.to_excel(writer, sheet_name="Summary", index=False)

    # One sheet per label
    for label in sorted(label_rows.keys()):
        df_label = pd.DataFrame(label_rows[label])[[
            "Model", "Story ID", "My Text", "Ann Text", "Match Status"
        ]]
        sheet_name = label[:31] if label else "Unknown"
        df_label.to_excel(writer, sheet_name=sheet_name, index=False)

print(f"\n✅ Sheets : Summary + {sorted(label_rows.keys())}")
print(f"✅ Saved  : {OUT_EXCEL}")
```

Here's the fix — add a **substring-match strategy** for protagonist/hero labels (and any others where one annotator marks just the name and the other marks the surrounding phrase/sentence).

## 🔧 Changes

### 1. Add config at top


### 2. Add new compare function

```python name=compare_substring.py
def compare_substring_match(spans_a, spans_b):
    """
    Story Hero / Protagonist — flexible text matching.
    Exact Match if:
      - same label AND
      - one text is contained in the other (case-insensitive, whitespace-normalised)
    """
    def norm(s):
        return re.sub(r"\s+", " ", s.strip()).lower()

    results = []
    used_b  = set()

    for sa in spans_a:
        matched = False
        ta = norm(sa["text"])
        for i, sb in enumerate(spans_b):
            if i in used_b:
                continue
            if sa["label"] != sb["label"]:
                continue
            tb = norm(sb["text"])
            if not ta or not tb:
                continue

            if ta == tb:
                status = "Exact Match"
            elif ta in tb or tb in ta:
                status = "Exact Match (substring)"
            else:
                continue

            results.append({
                "My Text"      : sa["text"],
                "My Label"     : sa["label"],
                "Ann Text"     : sb["text"],
                "Ann Label"    : sb["label"],
                "Match Status" : status,
            })
            used_b.add(i)
            matched = True
            break

        if not matched:
            results.append({
                "My Text"      : sa["text"],
                "My Label"     : sa["label"],
                "Ann Text"     : "",
                "Ann Label"    : "",
                "Match Status" : "Only Me",
            })

    for i, sb in enumerate(spans_b):
        if i not in used_b:
            results.append({
                "My Text"      : "",
                "My Label"     : "",
                "Ann Text"     : sb["text"],
                "Ann Label"    : sb["label"],
                "Match Status" : "Only Annotator",
            })

    return results
```

### 3. Update the per-task split + dispatch in MAIN

```python name=main_loop_update.py
    ann_other = [s for s in ann_spans
```

### 4. Update `compute_kappa` to treat substring match as agreement

In `compute_kappa`, the `else` branch currently counts "Partial" as disagreement (1,0). Add an explicit clause so `"Exact Match (substring)"` counts as agreement:

```python name=compute_kappa_update.py
def compute_kappa(span_results):
    y_me, y_ann = [], []
    for r in span_results:
        status = r["Match Status"]
        if status in ("Exact Match", "Exact Match (substring)"):
            y_me.append(1); y_ann.append(1)
        elif status == "Only Me":
            y_me.append(1); y_ann.append(0)
        elif status == "Only Annotator":
            y_me.append(0); y_ann.append(1)
        else:   # Partial
            y_me.append(1); y_ann.append(0)
    if len(set(y_me + y_ann)) < 2:
        return 1.0
    return round(cohen_kappa_score(y_me, y_ann), 4)
```

| Source | Text |
|---|---|
| **Me** | `حمزہ` |
| **Annotator** | `ایک دبلا پتلا، بیس بائیس سالہ لڑکا اندر آیا۔ چہرہ تھکن سے بھرا ہوا، آنکھوں میں نیند اور کپڑوں پر سفر کی گرد۔ اس کا نام حمزہ تھا۔` |

Before → "Only Me" / "Only Annotator" → **disagreement, hurts κ**
After → `"Exact Match (substring)"` because `حمزہ` ∈ annotator's text → **agreement, contributes to κ**

```python name=inter_annotator_agreement.py
import json
import re
import pandas as pd
from collections import defaultdict
from sklearn.metrics import cohen_kappa_score

# ─────────────────────────────────────────
# CONFIGURATION
# ─────────────────────────────────────────
MY_FILES = {
    "GPT"      : "./stories/annotated_stories/GPT_annotations.json",
    "DeepSeek" : "./stories/annotated_stories/DeepSeek_annotations.json",
    "QWEN"     : "./stories/annotated_stories/QWEN_annotations.json",
}
ANNOTATOR_FILES = {
    "GPT"      : "./stories/annotator/najm/GPT_annotations.json",
    "DeepSeek" : "./stories/annotator/najm/DeepSeek_annotations.json",
    "QWEN"     : "./stories/annotator/najm/QWEN_annotations.json",
}
OUT_EXCEL = "inter_annotator_agreement_najm.xlsx"

EXCLUDE_LABELS         = {"English Hyphen"}
TEXT_MATCH_LABELS      = {"City", "PER"}           # story-level unique, exact text match
SUBSTRING_MATCH_LABELS = {"Story Hero", "Protagonist"}  # substring-tolerant text match


# ─────────────────────────────────────────
# HELPERS
# ─────────────────────────────────────────
def clean_story_id(raw):
    return re.sub(r'^[0-9a-f]{8}-', '', raw.strip(), flags=re.IGNORECASE)


def get_spans(task):
    """Extract spans — exclude unwanted labels, dedupe by (text, label)."""
    spans = []
    seen  = set()
    for ann in task.get("annotations", []):
        if ann.get("was_cancelled"):
            continue
        for res in ann.get("result", []):
            val   = res.get("value", {})
            label = val.get("labels", [""])[0] if val.get("labels") else ""
            if label in EXCLUDE_LABELS:
                continue
            if "start" in val and "end" in val:
                text = val.get("text", "").strip()
                key  = (text, label)
                if key in seen:
                    continue
                seen.add(key)
                spans.append({
                    "start" : val["start"],
                    "end"   : val["end"],
                    "text"  : text,
                    "label" : label,
                })
    return spans


def _norm_text(s: str) -> str:
    return re.sub(r"\s+", " ", s.strip()).lower()


# ─────────────────────────────────────────
# COMPARE — three strategies
# ─────────────────────────────────────────
def compare_text_match(spans_a, spans_b):
    """
    PER / City — match by text + label (position ignored, story level).
    """
    results = []
    used_b  = set()

    for sa in spans_a:
        matched = False
        for i, sb in enumerate(spans_b):
            if i in used_b:
                continue
            same_text  = sa["text"]  == sb["text"]
            same_label = sa["label"] == sb["label"]
            overlap    = (sa["start"] < sb["end"]) and (sb["start"] < sa["end"])

            if same_text and same_label:
                status = "Exact Match"
            elif same_text and not same_label:
                status = "Partial Match (label differs)"
            elif overlap and same_label and not same_text:
                status = "Partial Match (span differs)"
            else:
                continue

            results.append({
                "My Text"      : sa["text"],
                "My Label"     : sa["label"],
                "Ann Text"     : sb["text"],
                "Ann Label"    : sb["label"],
                "Match Status" : status,
            })
            used_b.add(i)
            matched = True
            break

        if not matched:
            results.append({
                "My Text"      : sa["text"],
                "My Label"     : sa["label"],
                "Ann Text"     : "",
                "Ann Label"    : "",
                "Match Status" : "Only Me",
            })

    for i, sb in enumerate(spans_b):
        if i not in used_b:
            results.append({
                "My Text"      : "",
                "My Label"     : "",
                "Ann Text"     : sb["text"],
                "Ann Label"    : sb["label"],
                "Match Status" : "Only Annotator",
            })

    return results


def compare_substring_match(spans_a, spans_b):
    """
    Story Hero / Protagonist — flexible text matching.
    Exact Match if same label AND one text is contained in the other
    (case-insensitive, whitespace-normalised).
    """
    results = []
    used_b  = set()

    for sa in spans_a:
        matched = False
        ta = _norm_text(sa["text"])
        for i, sb in enumerate(spans_b):
            if i in used_b:
                continue
            if sa["label"] != sb["label"]:
                continue
            tb = _norm_text(sb["text"])
            if not ta or not tb:
                continue

            if ta == tb:
                status = "Exact Match"
            elif ta in tb or tb in ta:
                status = "Exact Match (substring)"
            else:
                continue

            results.append({
                "My Text"      : sa["text"],
                "My Label"     : sa["label"],
                "Ann Text"     : sb["text"],
                "Ann Label"    : sb["label"],
                "Match Status" : status,
            })
            used_b.add(i)
            matched = True
            break

        if not matched:
            results.append({
                "My Text"      : sa["text"],
                "My Label"     : sa["label"],
                "Ann Text"     : "",
                "Ann Label"    : "",
                "Match Status" : "Only Me",
            })

    for i, sb in enumerate(spans_b):
        if i not in used_b:
            results.append({
                "My Text"      : "",
                "My Label"     : "",
                "Ann Text"     : sb["text"],
                "Ann Label"    : sb["label"],
                "Match Status" : "Only Annotator",
            })

    return results


def compare_label_match(spans_a, spans_b):
    """
    Other labels — same label in same paragraph = Exact Match.
    Text match not required.
    """
    results = []
    used_b  = set()

    for sa in spans_a:
        matched = False
        for i, sb in enumerate(spans_b):
            if i in used_b:
                continue
            if sa["label"] == sb["label"]:
                results.append({
                    "My Text"      : sa["text"],
                    "My Label"     : sa["label"],
                    "Ann Text"     : sb["text"],
                    "Ann Label"    : sb["label"],
                    "Match Status" : "Exact Match",
                })
                used_b.add(i)
                matched = True
                break

        if not matched:
            results.append({
                "My Text"      : sa["text"],
                "My Label"     : sa["label"],
                "Ann Text"     : "",
                "Ann Label"    : "",
                "Match Status" : "Only Me",
            })

    for i, sb in enumerate(spans_b):
        if i not in used_b:
            results.append({
                "My Text"      : "",
                "My Label"     : "",
                "Ann Text"     : sb["text"],
                "Ann Label"    : sb["label"],
                "Match Status" : "Only Annotator",
            })

    return results


# ─────────────────────────────────────────
# LOOKUP + METRICS
# ─────────────────────────────────────────
def build_lookup(json_file):
    """Build lookup: (story_id, paragraph_text) → task"""
    with open(json_file, encoding="utf-8") as f:
        data = json.load(f)
    lookup = {}
    for task in data:
        story_id = clean_story_id(task.get("file_upload", ""))
        text     = task.get("data", {}).get("text", "").strip()
        if story_id and text:
            lookup[(story_id, text)] = task
    return lookup


AGREEMENT_STATUSES = {"Exact Match", "Exact Match (substring)"}


def compute_kappa(span_results):
    """
    Cohen's Kappa.
      Exact Match / Exact Match (substring) -> (1,1) agreement
      Only Me                                -> (1,0)
      Only Annotator                         -> (0,1)
      Partial Match (*)                      -> (1,0) treated as disagreement
    """
    y_me, y_ann = [], []
    for r in span_results:
        status = r["Match Status"]
        if status in AGREEMENT_STATUSES:
            y_me.append(1); y_ann.append(1)
        elif status == "Only Me":
            y_me.append(1); y_ann.append(0)
        elif status == "Only Annotator":
            y_me.append(0); y_ann.append(1)
        else:   # Partial Match (label differs / span differs)
            y_me.append(1); y_ann.append(0)

    if len(set(y_me + y_ann)) < 2:
        return 1.0   # perfect agreement (only one class present)
    return round(cohen_kappa_score(y_me, y_ann), 4)


def interpret_kappa(k):
    if   k >= 0.81: return "Almost Perfect"
    elif k >= 0.61: return "Substantial"
    elif k >= 0.41: return "Moderate"
    elif k >= 0.21: return "Fair"
    elif k >= 0.01: return "Slight"
    else:           return "Poor"


# ─────────────────────────────────────────
# MAIN PROCESSING
# ─────────────────────────────────────────
label_rows   = defaultdict(list)
summary_rows = []

for model in ["GPT", "DeepSeek", "QWEN"]:
    print(f"\n{'='*55}")
    print(f"Processing: {model}")
    print(f"{'='*55}")

    my_lookup  = build_lookup(MY_FILES[model])
    ann_lookup = build_lookup(ANNOTATOR_FILES[model])
    common     = set(my_lookup.keys()) & set(ann_lookup.keys())

    print(f"My tasks     : {len(my_lookup)}")
    print(f"Ann tasks    : {len(ann_lookup)}")
    print(f"Common tasks : {len(common)}")

    # ── Story-level: PER + City (unique, exact text match) ─────
    story_text_spans = defaultdict(lambda: {"my": [], "ann": []})
    story_seen       = defaultdict(lambda: {"my": set(), "ann": set()})

    # ── Task-level: substring labels + other labels ─────
    task_other_results = []   # list of (story_id, results)

    for key in common:
        story_id, _ = key
        my_task     = my_lookup[key]
        ann_task    = ann_lookup[key]

        my_spans  = get_spans(my_task)
        ann_spans = get_spans(ann_task)

        # Three buckets
        my_text  = [s for s in my_spans  if s["label"] in TEXT_MATCH_LABELS]
        ann_text = [s for s in ann_spans if s["label"] in TEXT_MATCH_LABELS]

        my_sub   = [s for s in my_spans  if s["label"] in SUBSTRING_MATCH_LABELS]
        ann_sub  = [s for s in ann_spans if s["label"] in SUBSTRING_MATCH_LABELS]

        my_other  = [s for s in my_spans
                     if s["label"] not in TEXT_MATCH_LABELS
                     and s["label"] not in SUBSTRING_MATCH_LABELS]
        ann_other = [s for s in ann_spans
                     if s["label"] not in TEXT_MATCH_LABELS
                     and s["label"] not in SUBSTRING_MATCH_LABELS]

        # PER/City → story-level unique
        for span in my_text:
            uk = (span["text"], span["label"])
            if uk not in story_seen[story_id]["my"]:
                story_seen[story_id]["my"].add(uk)
                story_text_spans[story_id]["my"].append(span)
        for span in ann_text:
            uk = (span["text"], span["label"])
            if uk not in story_seen[story_id]["ann"]:
                story_seen[story_id]["ann"].add(uk)
                story_text_spans[story_id]["ann"].append(span)

        # Story Hero / Protagonist → substring-tolerant match
        if my_sub or ann_sub:
            results = compare_substring_match(my_sub, ann_sub)
            task_other_results.append((story_id, results))

        # Other labels → same-label-in-same-paragraph = match
        if my_other or ann_other:
            results = compare_label_match(my_other, ann_other)
            task_other_results.append((story_id, results))

    # ── Compare PER/City at story level ──────────────────
    story_all_results = defaultdict(list)
    for story_id, spans in story_text_spans.items():
        results = compare_text_match(spans["my"], spans["ann"])
        story_all_results[story_id].extend(results)

    # ── Merge paragraph-level results ────────────────────
    for story_id, results in task_other_results:
        story_all_results[story_id].extend(results)

    # ── Build label rows + per-story summary ─────────────
    for story_id, span_results in story_all_results.items():

        for r in span_results:
            label = r["My Label"] or r["Ann Label"]
            label_rows[label].append({
                "Model"        : model,
                "Story ID"     : story_id,
                "My Text"      : r["My Text"],
                "Ann Text"     : r["Ann Text"],
                "Match Status" : r["Match Status"],
            })

        total   = len(span_results)
        exact   = sum(1 for r in span_results
                      if r["Match Status"] in AGREEMENT_STATUSES)
        partial = sum(1 for r in span_results if "Partial" in r["Match Status"])
        only_me = sum(1 for r in span_results if r["Match Status"] == "Only Me")
        only_an = sum(1 for r in span_results if r["Match Status"] == "Only Annotator")
        kappa   = compute_kappa(span_results)

        print(f"  {story_id:<25} Total:{total:>3} | "
              f"Exact:{exact:>3} | Partial:{partial:>3} | "
              f"OnlyMe:{only_me:>3} | OnlyAnn:{only_an:>3} | "
              f"Kappa:{kappa:>6}")

        summary_rows.append({
            "Model"          : model,
            "Story ID"       : story_id,
            "Total Spans"    : total,
            "Exact Match"    : exact,
            "Partial Match"  : partial,
            "Only Me"        : only_me,
            "Only Annotator" : only_an,
            "Agreement %"    : round(exact / total * 100, 1) if total > 0 else 0,
            "Cohen's Kappa"  : kappa,
            "Interpretation" : interpret_kappa(kappa),
        })

# ─────────────────────────────────────────
# KAPPA SUMMARY — per model + overall
# ─────────────────────────────────────────
df_summary = pd.DataFrame(summary_rows)

print(f"\n{'='*60}")
print("COHEN'S KAPPA SUMMARY")
print(f"{'='*60}")
print(f"{'Model':<12} {'Stories':>8} {'Avg Kappa':>12} {'Interpretation':>20}")
print(f"{'-'*60}")

for model in ["GPT", "DeepSeek", "QWEN"]:
    sub   = df_summary[df_summary["Model"] == model]
    avg_k = round(sub["Cohen's Kappa"].mean(), 4) if len(sub) else 0.0
    print(f"{model:<12} {len(sub):>8} {avg_k:>12} {interpret_kappa(avg_k):>20}")

overall_k = round(df_summary["Cohen's Kappa"].mean(), 4) if len(df_summary) else 0.0
print(f"{'-'*60}")
print(f"{'Overall':<12} {len(df_summary):>8} {overall_k:>12} "
      f"{interpret_kappa(overall_k):>20}")

# ─────────────────────────────────────────
# SAVE EXCEL — Summary + one sheet per label
# ─────────────────────────────────────────
with pd.ExcelWriter(OUT_EXCEL, engine="openpyxl") as writer:
    df_summary.to_excel(writer, sheet_name="Summary", index=False)

    for label in sorted(label_rows.keys()):
        df_label = pd.DataFrame(label_rows[label])[[
            "Model", "Story ID", "My Text", "Ann Text", "Match Status"
        ]]
        sheet_name = (label[:31] if label else "Unknown") or "Unknown"
        df_label.to_excel(writer, sheet_name=sheet_name, index=False)

print(f"\n✅ Sheets : Summary + {sorted(label_rows.keys())}")
print(f"✅ Saved  : {OUT_EXCEL}")
```

```latex name=section_annotation_process.tex
\subsection{Annotation Process}
\label{subsec:annotation}

A native Urdu speaker manually annotated all 93 generated
stories using Label Studio. Each story was segmented into
paragraphs; within each paragraph, the annotator marked
spans corresponding to nine labels grouped into four
categories: \emph{linguistic}, \emph{semantic},
\emph{cultural/script}, and \emph{narrative entities}.
A second native annotator independently re-annotated a
10\% subset, on which inter-annotator agreement was
computed (Cohen's $\kappa$, Section~\ref{subsec:iaa}).

\paragraph{Annotation granularity.}
Most labels are applied at the \emph{paragraph} level
(one span per occurrence). Four labels are applied at the
\emph{story} level, where each unique entity is marked
only once per story regardless of how many times it
recurs in the text:
\textbf{PER} (person name),
\textbf{City},
\textbf{MaleProtagonist}, and
\textbf{FemaleProtagonist}.

\paragraph{Linguistic vs.\ semantic distinction.}
We initially separated \emph{Grammar Error} from
\emph{Obliqueness} (gender / number / oblique-case
agreement errors such as
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{میرا/میری},
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{سا/سی},
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{اتنا/اتنی}). Because obliqueness is
itself a sub-class of Urdu grammar, we report obliqueness
cases as a sub-type of \textbf{Grammar Error} with
examples (Table~\ref{tab:label_examples}). The
\emph{Context Mismatch} label captures factual
\emph{contradictions} within a story (e.g.\ a character
described as childless in one paragraph and as a mother in
the next). The \emph{Confuse} label captures sentences
that are grammatically well-formed but
\emph{semantically empty or incoherent} --- i.e.\ syntax
is correct but meaning cannot be recovered. \emph{Foreign
Word} captures any token containing characters from a
non-Urdu script (Latin, Devanagari, CJK, Cyrillic), either
as an entire word or as a partial substitution within an
otherwise Urdu word.
```

```latex name=section_annotation_process.tex
\subsection{Annotation Process}
\label{subsec:annotation}

\paragraph{Annotators.}
Five native Urdu speakers participated as annotators.
All annotators held at least an undergraduate degree, were
fluent readers of Urdu literary prose, and were familiar
with Pakistani cultural context. Annotators received a
written guideline document defining each label
(Table~\ref{tab:label_examples}) together with
illustrative examples, and completed a short calibration
round on three pilot stories before production annotation
began. Annotation was performed in Label Studio.

\paragraph{Annotation granularity.}
Each story was segmented into paragraphs. Within each
paragraph, annotators marked text spans for the nine
labels grouped into four categories: \emph{linguistic},
\emph{semantic}, \emph{cultural / script}, and
\emph{narrative entities}
(Table~\ref{tab:label_examples}). Five labels are applied
at the \emph{paragraph} level (one span per occurrence),
and four labels --- \textbf{PER}, \textbf{City},
\textbf{MaleProtagonist}, \textbf{FemaleProtagonist} ---
at the \emph{story} level, where each unique entity is
marked once per story regardless of how many times it
recurs.

\paragraph{Linguistic vs.\ semantic distinctions.}
We initially separated \emph{Grammar Error} from
\emph{Obliqueness} (gender / number / oblique-case
agreement errors such as
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{میرا/میری},
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{سا/سی},
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{اتنا/اتنی}). Because obliqueness is
formally a sub-class of Urdu grammatical agreement, we
report obliqueness as a sub-type of \textbf{Grammar Error}
in Table~\ref{tab:label_examples}. The \emph{Context
Mismatch} label captures factual \emph{contradictions}
within a story (e.g.\ a character described as childless
in one paragraph and as a mother in the next). The
\emph{Confuse} label captures sentences that are
grammatically well-formed but \emph{semantically empty or
incoherent} --- i.e.\ syntax is correct but meaning
cannot be recovered. \emph{Foreign Word} captures any
token containing characters from a non-Urdu script (Latin,
Devanagari, CJK, Cyrillic), either as a complete word or
as a partial substitution within an otherwise Urdu word.

\paragraph{Inter-annotator agreement.}
To assess annotation reliability, a stratified subset of
\textbf{60 stories} (20 per model, spanning all four
title categories) was independently annotated by two of
the five annotators. Agreement was computed using Cohen's
$\kappa$, with span matching adapted to label semantics:
exact text + label match for entity labels (PER, City),
substring-tolerant text match for protagonist labels
(MaleProtagonist, FemaleProtagonist), and same-label
co-occurrence within the same paragraph for all other
labels. Per-label and per-model $\kappa$ scores are
reported in Section~\ref{subsec:iaa}. Disagreements on
the IAA subset were retained in the original primary
annotation (no adjudication-based relabelling); the IAA
score is reported solely as a reliability measure of the
single-annotator primary pass.

definition and a representative example in Urdu,
ALA-LC transliteration, and English gloss.

\foreignlanguage{arabic}{مسئیبت} $\rightarrow$
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{مصیبت} &
\textit{muṣībat} & ``calamity'' \\

Space Deletion & P &
Two adjacent Urdu words written without the space
between them (word-segmentation failure). &
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{چاہتیہوں} $\rightarrow$
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{چاہتی ہوں} &
\textit{chāhtī hūṅ} & ``(I) want (fem.)'' \\

Space Insertion & P &
A single Urdu word incorrectly split by an internal
space. &
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہم سفر} $\rightarrow$
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہمسفر} &
\textit{hamsafar} & ``life-companion'' \\

\midrule
\multicolumn{6}{l}{\textit{Semantic}} \\
\midrule
Context Mismatch & P &
Direct contradiction of a fact established earlier in
the story (events, attributes, or relationships). &
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{اس کی کوئی اولاد نہ تھی … اس کا بیٹا اسکول گیا} &
\textit{us kī kōʾī aulād nah thī \ldots us kā bēṭā iskūl gayā} &
``She had no children \ldots her son went to school'' \\

Confuse & P &
Grammatically well-formed but semantically empty or
incoherent --- meaning cannot be recovered from the
text. &
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{آواز میں ایک عجیب سی کانپ تھی} $\rightarrow$
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{آواز میں ایک عجیب سی کپکپاہٹ تھی} &
\textit{āwāz mēṅ ēk ʿajīb sī kapkapāhaṭ thī} &
``there was a strange tremor in (her) voice'' \\

\midrule
\multicolumn{6}{l}{\textit{Cultural / Script}} \\
\midrule
Cultural Inconsistency & P &
Reference inconsistent with a Pakistani cultural
setting (non-Pakistani names, places, customs, or
religiously inappropriate content). &
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{جان نے کرسمس ٹری سجایا} &
\textit{Jān nē Krismas Ṭrī sajāyā} &
``John decorated the Christmas tree'' \\

Foreign Word & P &
Any token containing characters from a non-Urdu script
(Latin, Devanagari, CJK, Cyrillic), whether the entire
word or only a portion of an otherwise Urdu token. &
\textit{hospital};
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ح}aj\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ی};
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{رسمی}ities &
--- & ``hospital''; partial-Latin
\emph{ḥājī}; \emph{rasmī}+\emph{-ities} \\

\midrule
\multicolumn{6}{l}{\textit{Narrative Entities}} \\
\midrule
PER & S &
Person name appearing in the story. Each unique name
annotated once per story. &
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{حمزہ} & \textit{Ḥamzah} & ``Hamza'' \\

City & S &
City or place name. Each unique location annotated
once per story. &
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{لاہور} & \textit{Lāhaur} & ``Lahore'' \\

MaleProtagonist & S &
The main male character. Annotated once per story;
either the name token or the introducing phrase may
be selected. &
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{حمزہ} & \textit{Ḥamzah} & ``Hamza'' \\

FemaleProtagonist & S &
The main female character. Annotated once per story. &
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ثنا} & \textit{S̱anā} & ``Sana'' \\

\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table*}
```

```latex name=section_iaa.tex
\subsection{Inter-Annotator Agreement}
\label{subsec:iaa}

To quantify annotation reliability, two of the five
annotators independently re-annotated the 60-story IAA
subset described in Section~\ref{subsec:annotation}.
Agreement is reported using Cohen's $\kappa$ with three
matching strategies tailored to label semantics:
(i)~exact text~+~label match for entity labels
(\textbf{PER}, \textbf{City}); (ii)~substring-tolerant
text match for protagonist labels
(\textbf{MaleProtagonist}, \textbf{FemaleProtagonist}),
which compensates for annotators selecting either the
name token alone or the surrounding introducing phrase;
and (iii)~same-label co-occurrence within the same
paragraph for all remaining (linguistic, semantic, and
cultural / script) labels.

aggregated across all three models. Following the
benchmarks of \citet{landis1977measurement},

agreement and $\kappa \geq 0.81$ as \emph{almost
perfect}.
```

## 🐍 Quick add-on: per-label κ

Your current script computes κ per **story**; to fill `tab:iaa_per_label` you also need κ per **label**. Add this snippet at the end of your script (before `pd.ExcelWriter`):

```python name=per_label_kappa.py
print(f"\n{'='*60}")
print("PER-LABEL COHEN'S KAPPA")
print(f"{'='*60}")

per_label_kappa = {}
for label, rows in label_rows.items():
    # Each row is one comparison record
    y_me, y_ann = [], []
    for r in rows:
        status = r["Match Status"]
        if status in ("Exact Match", "Exact Match (substring)"):
            y_me.append(1); y_ann.append(1)
        elif status == "Only Me":
            y_me.append(1); y_ann.append(0)
        elif status == "Only Annotator":
            y_me.append(0); y_ann.append(1)
        else:
            y_me.append(1); y_ann.append(0)

    if len(set(y_me + y_ann)) < 2:
        k = 1.0
    else:
        k = round(cohen_kappa_score(y_me, y_ann), 4)
    per_label_kappa[label] = k
    print(f"  {label:<25} N={len(rows):>4}   κ = {k:>6}   ({interpret_kappa(k)})")
```

```latex name=section_annotation_summary.tex
\subsection{Annotation and Inter-Annotator Agreement}
\label{subsec:annotation}

Five native Urdu speakers annotated all 93 stories in
Label Studio using nine labels in four categories
(Table~\ref{tab:labels_compact}). Most labels are applied
at the \emph{paragraph} level; entity labels (\textbf{PER},
\textbf{City}, \textbf{MaleProtagonist},
\textbf{FemaleProtagonist}) are applied once per
\emph{story}. \emph{Obliqueness} (gender / number /
oblique-case agreement, e.g.\ \foreignlanguage{arabic}{سا/سی},
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{اتنا/اتنی}) is treated as a sub-type of
\emph{Grammar Error}; \emph{Confuse} marks sentences that
are syntactically well-formed but semantically empty;
\emph{Context Mismatch} marks factual contradictions; and
\emph{Foreign Word} marks any token (whole or partial)
containing non-Urdu script characters.

A stratified subset of \textbf{60 stories} (20 per model,
balanced across title categories) was independently
re-annotated by a second annotator. Cohen's $\kappa$ was
computed using exact text+label match for PER/City,
substring-tolerant match for protagonist labels (to
accommodate annotators selecting the name token vs.\ the
introducing phrase), and same-label-in-same-paragraph
match for all other labels. Overall agreement was
$\kappa$~=~\textbf{xxx} (\textbf{xxx} reliability); per-model
and per-label scores are reported in
Appendix~\ref{app:iaa}.
```

For your example (`آگ کے چراغ سے اُبلتی چائے پی رہا تھا` — "he was drinking tea boiling from a lamp of fire"), the phenomenon is **semantic nonsense / surreal imagery despite valid grammar**. Standard terms in linguistics and NLP literature for this exact issue:

---

## 🏆 Recommended: **Semantic Anomaly**

This is the **established term** in linguistics (Chomsky's *"colorless green ideas sleep furiously"* is the canonical example) and is widely used in NLP/LLM evaluation papers.

| Why it fits | |
|---|---|
| ✅ Standard academic term — reviewers will recognise it immediately | |
| ✅ Precisely means "grammatical but semantically ill-formed / impossible" | |
| ✅ Distinct from *Context Mismatch* (contradiction) and *Grammar Error* (syntax) | |
| ✅ Short, neutral, paper-ready | |

---

## 🥈 Alternatives (in order of preference)

| Name | When to prefer |
|---|---|
| **Semantic Incoherence** | If you want to emphasise meaning breakdown rather than impossibility |
| **Nonsensical Sentence** | More plain-English, slightly less formal |
| **Meaningless Statement** | Casual; weaker for academic writing |
| **Hallucinated Imagery** | Trendy LLM-evaluation term; good if you want to align with hallucination literature |

---

## ✅ My recommendation

Use **`Semantic Anomaly`** throughout the paper.

It cleanly slots into your taxonomy:

| Category | Label | What it captures |
|---|---|---|
| Linguistic | Grammar Error | Syntax / agreement / obliqueness |
| Linguistic | Spelling Error | Misspelled Urdu word |
| **Semantic** | **Context Mismatch** | **Contradiction across sentences** |
| **Semantic** | **Semantic Anomaly** | **Grammatical but meaningless within a sentence** |

The two semantic labels now read as a clean pair: **between-sentence vs. within-sentence** semantic failures.

```
\section{Introduction}

Although Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated strong performance in text generation tasks, it remains unclear whether these models generate genuinely novel narratives or merely reproduce memorized storytelling patterns. This challenge becomes even more significant in low-resource languages such as Urdu, where literary style, semantic coherence, and cultural nuances play a critical role in narrative quality.

This study investigates the capability of modern LLMs to generate Urdu stories that are not only linguistically correct but also semantically meaningful and culturally grounded within the Pakistani context. In particular, we analyze whether generated stories preserve contextual relevance, avoid repetitive narrative structures, and appropriately adapt culturally sensitive themes originating from both Urdu and Western literary traditions.
\section{Research Questions}

This study aims to answer the following research questions:

\begin{itemize}
    \item Do LLMs generate original Urdu narratives or tend to reproduce existing storytelling patterns?
    
    \item How linguistically accurate are LLM-generated Urdu stories in terms of grammar, spelling, and sentence structure?
    
    \item Are the generated stories semantically coherent and contextually meaningful?
    
    \item To what extent do LLM-generated stories preserve Pakistani cultural and social contexts?
    
    \item Do LLM-generated Urdu stories exhibit gender bias in character representation and narrative focus?
\end{itemize}
\section{Methodology}
\subsection{Story Title Corpus}
\label{subsec:title_corpus}

We constructed a corpus of \textbf{31 story titles} drawn
from four different sources
(Table~\ref{tab:title_distribution}): \emph{Translated Short
Story Titles} (TSST, N~=~11) from canonical English short
fiction (e.g.\ Jackson, Chekhov, Hemingway, Maupassant);
\emph{Urdu Short Story Titles} (UST, N~=~11) from canonical
twentieth-century Urdu writers used \emph{verbatim}
(e.g.\ Manto, Chughtai, Ghulam Abbas, Bedi, Hyder);
\emph{Fables} (N~=~3), short Indo-Pakistani moral tales used
as a memorisation control; and \emph{Neutral Urdu Titles}
(NUT, N~=~6), author-original Urdu titles composed by the
research team as an unseen-prompt control. The complete
title list with authors, sources, and ALA-LC transliterations
is given in Appendix~\ref{app:titles}
(Table~\ref{tab:title_full_list}).

For TSST, we obtained an initial Urdu rendering of each
English title via the GPT-5.1 translation API and then
manually verified every translation with a native Urdu
speaker; published Urdu renderings (e.g.\
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{لاٹری}, \foreignlanguage{arabic}{بندر کا پنجہ})
were retained verbatim where they exist.

\begin{table}[h]
\centering\small
\begin{tabular}{lcc}
\toprule
\textbf{Source} & \textbf{Abbrev.} & \textbf{\#} \\
\midrule
Translated Short Story Titles & TSST  & 11 \\
Urdu Short Story Titles       & UST   & 11 \\
Fables                        & Fable & 3  \\
Neutral Urdu Titles           & NUT   & 6  \\
\midrule
\textbf{Total}                &       & \textbf{31} \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\caption{Distribution of story titles across the four
source categories.}
\label{tab:title_distribution}
\end{table}

The four-way split is intentional: TSST tests cross-cultural
transfer, UST tests reproduction vs.\ reinterpretation of
canonical Urdu literature, Fable controls for plot
memorisation, and NUT controls for memorisation in absence
of any prior text. Corpus size (N~=~31, yielding 93 generations
across three models) balances stylistic breadth with the
depth required for sentence-level human annotation.

\subsection{Generation Protocol}
\label{subsec:generation}

Three LLMs were evaluated: \textbf{GPT}
(\texttt{5.1}), \textbf{QWEN} (\texttt{3-Max}),
and \textbf{DeepSeek} (\texttt{3.1}), all accessed
in \texttt{<11-2025>}. Each title was issued to every model
exactly once using a fixed zero-shot prompt:

\begin{quote}\itshape
``Write a 2000-word Urdu story in a Pakistani cultural
context. The title is \foreignlanguage{arabic}{دوسری عورت}.''
\end{quote}

Few-shot exemplars and chain-of-thought scaffolding were
deliberately omitted so that observed differences reflect
model capacity rather than prompt engineering.
\subsection{Corpus Statistics}
\label{subsec:corpus_stats}

The protocol produced \textbf{93 stories} (31 titles
$\times$ 3 models) totalling \textbf{151{,}965 words}
and \textbf{9{,}460 sentences}
(Table~\ref{tab:corpus_stats}). Length compliance with
the 2{,}000-word target ($\pm$20\%) varied sharply across
models: DeepSeek met it in 80.6\% of stories, GPT in
58.1\%, and QWEN in only 12.9\% --- with no QWEN story
exceeding 1{,}900 words, indicating an internal length
ceiling rather than random variance. We therefore treat
length compliance as a finding rather than a filter, and
report normalised error rates per 1{,}000 words
(Section~\ref{subsec:linguistic}) to enable fair
cross-model comparison.

\begin{table}[ht]
\label{tab:corpus_stats}
\centering\small
\caption{Per-model corpus statistics on the normalised
text. Compliance = \% of stories within $\pm$20\% of the
2{,}000-word target.}
\scalebox{0.9}{

\subsection{Annotation and Inter-Annotator Agreement}
\label{subsec:annotation}

Five native Urdu speakers annotated all 93 stories in
Label Studio using nine labels in four categories
(Table~\ref{tab:labels_compact}). Most labels are applied
at the \emph{paragraph} level; entity labels (\textbf{PER},
\textbf{City}, \textbf{MaleProtagonist},
\textbf{FemaleProtagonist}) are applied once per
\emph{story}. \emph{Obliqueness} (gender / number /
oblique-case agreement, e.g.\ \foreignlanguage{arabic}{سا/سی},
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{اتنا/اتنی}) is treated as a sub-type of
\emph{Grammar Error}; \emph{Semantic Anomaly} marks sentences that
are syntactically well-formed but semantically empty;
\emph{Context Mismatch} marks factual contradictions; and
\emph{Foreign Word} marks any token (whole or partial)
containing non-Urdu script characters or non-Urdu words.

A stratified subset of \textbf{60 stories} (20 per model,
balanced across title categories) was independently
re-annotated by a second annotator. Cohen's $\kappa$ was
computed using exact text+label match for PER/City,
substring-tolerant match for protagonist labels (to
accommodate annotators selecting the name token vs.\ the
introducing phrase), and same-label-in-same-paragraph
match for all other labels. Overall agreement was
$\kappa$~=~\textbf{xxx} (\textbf{xxx} reliability); per-model
and per-label scores are reported in
Appendix~\ref{app:iaa}.

\section{Qualitative Analysis}
Several models generated grammatically correct Urdu but failed to preserve culturally authentic Pakistani dialogues and naming conventions.

\subsection{Creativity vs Memorization}The generated stories demonstrated varying levels of originality across different models. While some narratives introduced new characters and alternative plot developments, several outputs appeared to follow repetitive storytelling structures. In certain cases, the generated stories closely resembled existing literary themes associated with the provided titles, suggesting partial reliance on memorized narrative patterns.
\subsection{Linguistic and Semantic Quality Analysis}

Manual annotation of all 93 generated stories 
(31 titles $\times$ 3 models) identified 
\textbf{762 quality issues} across three dimensions: 
linguistic errors (N~=~477, 62.6\%), semantic 
coherence failures (N~=~277, 36.4\%), and cultural 
mismatches (N~=~8, 1.0\%). Two automatically 
detected error types --- typographic dash misuse 
and foreign word intrusion --- are reported 
separately in Section~\ref{subsec:auto_errors}. 
Table~\ref{tab:error_summary} (Appendix) presents 
the complete breakdown.

%─────────────────────────────────────────
\subsubsection{Linguistic Errors}
%─────────────────────────────────────────

Linguistic errors constituted the largest share 
of identified issues (N~=~477). Four subtypes 
were annotated: grammatical mistakes, spelling 
errors, space deletion, and space insertion.

\textbf{Grammatical errors} were the most 
frequent category overall (N~=~321, avg~=~3.45 
per story), representing 67.3\% of all linguistic 
errors. QWEN exhibited the highest rate 
(N~=~149), followed by GPT (N~=~76) and 
DeepSeek (N~=~96). Recurring patterns included 
incorrect postposition usage (\foreignlanguage{arabic}{نے}، \foreignlanguage{arabic}{کو}،  
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{سے}), broken verb-subject agreement, and 
malformed compound sentences --- errors that 
significantly impair readability in Urdu prose.

\textbf{Spelling errors} (N~=~77, avg~=~0.83 
per story) were distributed across all models 
(DeepSeek: 22, GPT: 16, QWEN: 39). Their 
relatively uniform distribution suggests that 
spelling degradation is model-agnostic and 
may reflect tokenization artifacts in Urdu 
Nastaliq script processing rather than 
model-specific weaknesses.

\textbf{Space deletion}: where two distinct 
words are merged without a space, was the 
third most frequent linguistic error (N~=~78). 
GPT accounted for the majority of these errors 
(N~=~54), compared to DeepSeek (N~=~19) and 
QWEN (N~=~5). \textbf{Space insertion} was rare 
(N~=~1), confirming that over-merging is a more 
prevalent orthographic failure mode than 
over-splitting. Both error types reflect the 
well-documented challenges of whitespace 
normalization in Urdu NLP \cite{[CITE]}, arising 
from the cursive nature of Nastaliq script and 
inconsistent Unicode zero-width non-joiner 
(ZWNJ) handling.

\textbf{Obliqueness} --- vague or ambiguous 
phrasing from which meaning cannot be recovered 
--- was the dominant semantic error (N~=~144, 
51.9\% of semantic issues). QWEN showed 
markedly higher obliqueness (N~=~86) compared 
to GPT (N~=~36) and DeepSeek (N~=~22), 
suggesting that QWEN's Urdu generation tends 
toward surface fluency at the expense of 
semantic clarity. Common manifestations 
included broken anaphora chains, underspecified 
referents, and syntactically complete but 
semantically empty sentences.
%─────────────────────────────────────────
\subsubsection{Semantic Coherence}
%─────────────────────────────────────────

Semantic coherence failures (N~=~277, 
avg~=~2.98 per story) constituted the second 
largest error category. Two labels were used: 
 \textit{Context Mismatch}, 
and \textit{Semantic Anamoly}.



\textbf{Context mismatch} (N~=~75) --- where 
a sentence is inconsistent with the surrounding 
narrative --- was distributed relatively evenly 
across models (DeepSeek: 20, GPT: 18, QWEN: 37). 
This error type was particularly prominent in 
stories exceeding 1,500 words, suggesting 
degradation of long-range narrative coherence 
during extended generation.
\textbf{Semantic Anamoly}
\textbf{Contradictory content} (N~=~58) --- 
where characters, events, or facts established 
earlier are violated later in the story --- was 
least frequent but most disruptive to narrative 
integrity. GPT showed the highest rate (N~=~28), 
followed by QWEN (N~=~25) and DeepSeek (N~=~5).

\textbf{Language Register Violations: }A qualitatively distinct failure mode was observed 
exclusively in DeepSeek: the substitution of Urdu 
with Punjabi at the phrase and sentence level, where 
characters switch to Punjabi dialogue within an 
otherwise Urdu narrative. Unlike the orthographic 
errors discussed above, this operates at the 
discourse level and constitutes a direct prompt 
non-compliance, as the generation prompts explicitly 
specified Urdu. The pattern suggests that DeepSeek 
conflates Pakistani linguistic identity with Punjabi, 
the majority regional language of Pakistan, rather 
than maintaining the prompted language boundary.
%─────────────────────────────────────────
\subsubsection{Cultural Fidelity}
%─────────────────────────────────────────

Manually annotated cultural errors were 
infrequent (N~=~8), comprising Western cultural 
references (N~=~6) and religiously inappropriate 
content (N~=~2). No instances of full English 
sentences within the Urdu narrative were manually 
flagged; however, automated lexical analysis 
revealed extensive foreign word intrusion at 
the sub-sentence level, reported in 
Section~\ref{subsec:auto_errors}.

\textbf{Western cultural references} (N~=~6) 
appeared in GPT (N~=~3) and DeepSeek (N~=~3) 
stories, including non-Pakistani character 
names and geographically incongruous settings. 
These intrusions were particularly dissonant 
in Urdu Literature category stories, where 
Pakistani cultural authenticity is central 
to narrative meaning. QWEN produced no 
Western cultural references, which may 
reflect stronger regional cultural alignment 
in its training data.



\subsubsection{Em-dash} \label{subsec:auto_errors} Urdu prose 
conventionally does not employ em-dashes (---) 
as structural markers; their 
presence indicates stylistic transfer from 
English writing conventions. Automatic 
detection across all 93 stories identified 
a total of \texttt{601} dash occurrences 
(avg~=~\texttt{6.5} per story), with 
\texttt{21} showing the highest frequency.
\subsubsection{Script Contamination in LLM-Generated Urdu Text}

Beyond lexical foreign word intrusion, automated 
Unicode-level analysis revealed a more fundamental 
failure mode: the embedding of non-Urdu script 
characters, including Latin, Devanagari (Hindi), 
Chinese (CJK), and Cyrillic  within generated 
Urdu text. A word was flagged as contaminated if 
any character in it fell outside the Urdu Unicode 
range (U+0600--U+06FF) and its standard presentation 
forms, excluding digits and punctuation. Table 
\ref{tab:script_contamination} summarises findings 
across all 93 stories.

DeepSeek exhibited the most severe contamination 
(61 affected word instances across 15 of 31 stories), 
followed by QWEN (14 instances, 3 stories) and GPT 
(11 instances, 6 stories). Contamination manifested 
in two structural patterns: \textit{pure intrusion}, 
where an entire word belongs to a foreign script, 
and \textit{partial contamination}, where a single 
word contains characters from both Urdu and a foreign 
script, corrupting an otherwise valid Urdu token.

\textbf{Latin Script Intrusion:} Latin script was the most frequent contaminant 
across all models (GPT: 7, QWEN: 13, DeepSeek: 47 
word instances). Pure English word substitution 
included everyday vocabulary for which standard 
Urdu equivalents exist --- \textit{hospital}, 
\textit{career}, \textit{water}, \textit{warmth}, 
\textit{tight}.

Two partial Latin contaminations merit particular 
attention. First, QWEN substituted the pilgrimage 
honorific \foreignlanguage{arabic}{حاجی} with \foreignlanguage{arabic}{ح}aj\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ی} --- replacing 
the vowel graphemes \foreignlanguage{arabic}{ا} and \foreignlanguage{arabic}{ی} with Latin 
\textit{a} and \textit{j} , and repeated this 
malformed token 10 times within a single 
1,343-word story ً, suggesting a 
systematic tokenizer failure rather than an incidental 
error. Second, DeepSeek produced \foreignlanguage{arabic}{کہ}a, where the 
common Urdu verb \foreignlanguage{arabic}{کہا} (\textit{said}) had its 
final character replaced by Latin \textit{a}, a 
substitution that is phonetically equivalent but 
orthographically invalid, indicating that DeepSeek's 
decoder resolved an ambiguous token boundary via 
Latin fallback. Furthermore, \foreignlanguage{arabic}{رسمی}ities, 
an Urdu adjective \foreignlanguage{arabic}{رسمی} (\textit{formal}) 
suffixed with the English plural morpheme 
\textit{-ities}, represents a novel form of 
\textit{cross-lingual morphological contamination} 
not previously documented for Urdu LLM outputs, in 
which English derivational morphology is applied 
to an Urdu stem.

\textbf{Devanagari (Hindi) Intrusion:} Devanagari intrusion was most prevalent in DeepSeek 
 and even present in GPT  and 
QWEN. Given that Urdu and Hindi share 
extensive phonological overlap, this proximity appears to produce 
character-level cross-script leakage, where phonetically 
equivalent Devanagari diacritics substitute for their 
Urdu counterparts e.g.\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ٹھ}ِِ\foreignlanguage{hindi}{ं} \foreignlanguage{arabic}{ڈی}(\textit{cold}) carries the Devanagari 
. More severe is 
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ک} \foreignlanguage{hindi}{ुत्त} \foreignlanguage{arabic}{وں}, where the Urdu word for 
\textit{dogs} is partially rendered in Devanagari 
mid-token, a morpheme-level script switch rather 
than a diacritic substitution.

\textbf{Chinese and Cyrillic Intrusion:} The most striking contamination instances 
involve Chinese (CJK) and Cyrillic characters 
embedded within Urdu tokens. These  scripts have low/no phonological or typological relationship 
to Urdu, making their presence entirely 
anomalous. 

In both Chinese and Cyrillic instances, the foreign 
token is semantically appropriate in context --- 
\textit{yánsù} (\textit{serious}), 
\textit{de shēnghuó} (\textit{of life}), 
\textit{huàtí} (\textit{topic}); 
\textit{iskrennost'} (\textit{sincerity}) --- 
however, a missing word boundary caused each token 
to attach directly to the preceding Urdu word, 
producing undelimited bilingual compounds. The 
failure is thus orthographic rather than semantic: 
DeepSeek selected a valid lexical item from its 
Chinese or Cyrillic vocabulary where an Urdu 
equivalent was required, and omitted the 
delimiting space. The exception is 
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{پراج} \foreignlanguage{russian}{ек} \foreignlanguage{arabic}{ٹ}, where  
Cyrillic characters are substituted for 
similar Urdu phonemes within a single word.



%─────────────────────────────────────────
\subsubsection{Named Entity Usage}
%─────────────────────────────────────────

Annotation of named entity labels recorded 
how models populate narratives with characters 
and locations. Person names (PER: N~=~240) 
were the most frequent entity type, followed 
by city references (N~=~90) and imaginary 
places (N~=~7). GPT produced the highest 
number of named person references (N~=~94), 
followed by QWEN (N~=~80) and DeepSeek 
(N~=~66), suggesting variation in how 
explicitly models anchor narratives to 
named individuals. Gender representation 
in protagonist roles is discussed separately 
in Section~\ref{subsec:gender_bias}.

%─────────────────────────────────────────
\subsubsection{Gender Representation in 
              Protagonist Roles}
\label{subsec:gender_bias}
%─────────────────────────────────────────

% ── آپ کے لیے خالی چھوڑا ہے ──
% ── نیچے structure دی ہے، آپ اپنا analysis لکھیں ──

Annotation of protagonist roles revealed a 
pronounced gender imbalance in LLM-generated 
Urdu narratives. Across all 93 stories, male 
protagonists (N~=~40) outnumbered female 
protagonists (N~=~13) by a ratio of 
\textbf{3.1:1}, with no model approaching 
gender parity.

Table~\ref{tab:gender} presents the 
distribution by model.

This disparity is particularly noteworthy 
given that the generation prompts were 
gender-neutral, containing no explicit 
instruction regarding protagonist gender.

The consistent male-default pattern across 
all three models, suggests that 
gender bias is encoded in the underlying 
generative behavior of these models rather 
than being prompt-induced.

This finding aligns with\textbf{ prior work }
documenting gender bias in neural text 
generation.

\subsection{Title Repetition Analysis}



To quantify the unnatural repetition of story titles in 
generated narratives, we introduce \textbf{Title Repetition 
Rate (TRR)} --- the number of exact title phrase occurrences 
per 100 words in the story body, excluding the opening line.

\begin{equation}
    TRR = \frac{\text{count}(title\_phrase \in story\_body)}
               {\text{total\_words}} \times 100
\end{equation}

Both title and story text were normalized prior to matching 
through removal of diacritics, character variant normalization 
(Alef, Yeh, Kaf), punctuation removal, and whitespace 
normalization.

%─────────────────────────────────────────
\subsubsection{TRR Across Models and Categories}
%─────────────────────────────────────────

Table~\ref{tab:trr_overall} presents average TRR by model, 
and Table~\ref{tab:trr_category} breaks this down by story 
category. Stories from the \textbf{Urdu Literature category} 
--- featuring metaphorical titles such as \foreignlanguage{arabic}{لحاف}،  
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{اوور کوٹ}، and \foreignlanguage{arabic}{گرم کوٹ} --- consistently 
exhibited the highest TRR across all models. Conversely, 
\textbf{Fable category} stories showed near-zero TRR despite 
reproducing canonical plots, revealing two distinct failure 
modes: plot memorization (low TRR, no creativity) versus 
literal title anchoring (high TRR, no metaphorical understanding).

\subsubsection{Title Lexicalization Effect}
%─────────────────────────────────────────

We identify a phenomenon we term the \textbf{Title 
Lexicalization Effect}: when a title carries literary or 
metaphorical significance, LLMs interpret it in its direct 
lexical meaning rather than engaging with its cultural depth. 
This manifests as: (i)~\textit{literal narrative construction} 
--- \foreignlanguage{arabic}{لحاف} (Ismat Chughtai's metaphor for hidden desires) 
became a story about physical warmth; \foreignlanguage{arabic}{اوور کوٹ} and 
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{گرم کوٹ} became stories about garments; and \foreignlanguage{arabic}{ٹھنڈا گوشت }  about cold meat.


%─────────────────────────────────────────
\subsubsection{Comparison with Original Authors}
%─────────────────────────────────────────

To validate this finding, we obtained original Urdu texts 
of \foreignlanguage{arabic}{لحاف} \citep{chughtai1942}, \foreignlanguage{arabic}{اوور کوٹ}, and 
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{گرم کوٹ} from Rekhta.org and computed their TRR as 
a baseline. 
As shown in Table~\ref{tab:trr_original}, original authors 
used title words sparingly (TRR: 0.33--0.52), employing 
them as thematic anchors rather than literal subjects. 
LLM-generated stories exceeded original TRR in 
\textbf{8 of 9 comparisons (88.9\%)}, with ratios of 
1.4$\times$--5.3$\times$. The most extreme case was 
QWEN's \foreignlanguage{arabic}{لحاف} (TRR~=~2.79, count~=~35) --- 
5.3$\times$ more than Ismat Chughtai's original 
(TRR~=~0.52, count~=~20).

\section{Limitations}
\label{subsec:trr_limit}
%─────────────────────────────────────────

Exact-phrase TRR may underestimate title anchoring when 
models decompose multi-word titles. GPT's \foreignlanguage{arabic}{گرم کوٹ} 
(TRR~=~0.16) illustrates this: the exact phrase appeared 
only 3 times, yet \foreignlanguage{arabic}{کوٹ} alone was used extensively 
throughout --- depicting a character with a \foreignlanguage{arabic}{شال} 
aspiring to own a \foreignlanguage{arabic}{گرم کوٹ}. Partial-word matching 
and synonym detection represent directions for future 
refinement of the TRR metric.

%─────────────────────────────────────────
% FIGURES
%─────────────────────────────────────────

\begin{figure}[h]
    \centering
    \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]
        {figures/plot2_category_comparison.png}
    \caption{Average TRR by story category and model. 
    Urdu Literature titles show consistently highest TRR, 
    reflecting systematic literal interpretation of 
    metaphorical titles.}
    \label{fig:trr_category}
\end{figure}



\section{Conclusion}
This study demonstrates that while modern LLMs can generate fluent Urdu narratives, significant challenges remain in cultural grounding and linguistic precision.
```

## 1️⃣ New Abstract

```latex name=abstract.tex
\begin{abstract}
We present the first systematic evaluation of
Large Language Model (LLM) storytelling in Urdu within
a Pakistani cultural context. Using a curated corpus of
\textbf{31 story titles} spanning translated English
short fiction, canonical Urdu literature, fables, and
neutral Urdu titles, we generated \textbf{93 stories
across three LLMs} (GPT-5.1, Qwen-3-Max,
DeepSeek-3.1), totalling \textbf{151{,}965 words} and
\textbf{xxx sentences}. All stories were manually
annotated by five native Urdu speakers using a
nine-label taxonomy covering linguistic, semantic,
cultural / script, and narrative-entity dimensions; a
60-story subset was double-annotated, yielding an
overall inter-annotator agreement of $\kappa$~=~\textbf{xxx}.
We further introduce the \textbf{Title Repetition Rate
(TRR)} metric and document a recurring failure mode we
term the \textbf{Title Lexicalization Effect}, in which
metaphorical Urdu titles are reinterpreted literally:
LLM-generated stories exceed original-author TRR in 8
of 9 comparisons (up to 5.3$\times$). Additional
findings include: (i)~systematic length non-compliance
in QWEN (only 12.9\% of stories within $\pm$20\% of the
2{,}000-word target); (ii)~pervasive cross-script
contamination in DeepSeek (Latin, Devanagari, CJK,
Cyrillic embeddings, including novel cross-lingual
morphological forms such as
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{رسمی}\textit{ities});
(iii)~Punjabi register switching unique to DeepSeek;
and (iv)~a 3.7:1 male-to-female protagonist bias under
gender-neutral prompts. We release the corpus,
annotations, and evaluation scripts to support
reproducibility of Urdu LLM evaluation research.
\end{abstract}
```

## 2️⃣ Introduction — add contributions list

```latex name=intro_contributions.tex
\paragraph{Contributions.} Our work makes the
following contributions: (i)~a curated corpus of 31
Urdu story titles balanced across four sources
(translated English, canonical Urdu, fables, neutral)
with full author and ALA-LC metadata
(Appendix~\ref{app:titles}); (ii)~93 LLM-generated
Urdu stories with sentence-level manual annotation by
five native speakers and a 60-story IAA subset;
(iii)~a nine-label evaluation taxonomy with
normalised per-1{,}000-word error rates that enables
fair comparison across models with different output
verbosities; (iv)~the introduction of the
\emph{Title Repetition Rate} (TRR) metric and the
identification of the \emph{Title Lexicalization
Effect} as a systematic Urdu-LLM failure mode;
(v)~the first documented account of cross-script
contamination (Latin, Devanagari, CJK, Cyrillic) and
Punjabi register switching in Urdu LLM output;
(vi)~quantitative evidence of male-default gender
bias in Urdu LLM storytelling under gender-neutral
prompts.
```

## 3️⃣ New Related Work section (insert after §Research Questions)

```latex name=related_work.tex
\section{Related Work}
\label{sec:related}

\paragraph{Urdu NLP and low-resource generation.}
Urdu NLP has historically focused on foundational
tasks such as tokenisation, word-segmentation, and
POS tagging \citep{xxx, xxx}, with comparatively
little work on long-form generation evaluation.
Existing Urdu corpora such as the CLE Urdu Digest
\citep{xxx} and UrduHack toolkits \citep{xxx}
target sentence-level or document classification
rather than narrative quality.

\paragraph{LLM evaluation for non-English creative
generation.} Recent multilingual evaluations
\citep{bang2023multitask, ahuja2023mega, xxx}
report that frontier LLMs degrade substantially
outside English, particularly in low-resource South
Asian languages. However, these benchmarks emphasise
classification and short-form QA rather than
extended narrative generation, leaving Urdu story
quality largely unmeasured.

\paragraph{Memorisation and creativity in LLM
storytelling.} Prior work has documented verbatim
training-data memorisation in LLMs
\citep{carlini2021extracting, xxx} and template
reuse in generated fiction
\citep{lucy2021gender, xxx}. We extend this line by
introducing the Title Repetition Rate and showing
that LLMs systematically over-anchor on title
tokens relative to original human authors.

\paragraph{Gender bias in generated text.}
Lucy~and~Bamman~\citep{lucy2021gender},
Kotek~et~al.~\citep{xxx}, and
Sheng~et~al.~\citep{sheng2019woman} document
male-default character generation in English LLM
output. We provide the first such measurement for
Urdu, and find a comparable 3.7:1 male-to-female
protagonist ratio.
```

## 5️⃣ Rewritten §4.2 Linguistic and Semantic Quality Analysis

```latex name=section_4_2_quality.tex
\subsection{Linguistic and Semantic Quality}
\label{subsec:linguistic}

Manual annotation across 93 stories identified
\textbf{762 quality issues} grouped into linguistic
(N~=~621, 81.5\%), semantic (N~=~133, 17.5\%), and
cultural / script (N~=~8, 1.0\%) categories
(Table~\ref{tab:error_summary}). Automatically detected
phenomena --- em-dash overuse
(Section~\ref{subsec:em_dash}) and Unicode-level
script contamination
(Section~\ref{subsec:script_contamination}) --- are
reported separately. Because models differ in output
length (\textit{cf.}\ Section~\ref{subsec:corpus_stats}),
we report all rates per 1{,}000 words alongside raw
counts.

\paragraph{Normalisation reverses the apparent
ranking.} While DeepSeek shows higher raw counts in
several subtypes, this partly reflects its larger
output (59{,}939 words vs.\ QWEN's 42{,}827). After
normalisation, \textbf{QWEN emerges as the most
error-prone model overall} (7.99 errors / 1k~words)
--- 1.7$\times$ GPT (4.74) and 2.6$\times$ DeepSeek
(3.12). The ranking inversion is sharpest for
grammatical and semantic errors.

\subsubsection{Linguistic Errors}

Linguistic errors dominated the issue distribution
(N~=~621). \textbf{Grammar errors} (N~=~321 general
+ N~=~144 obliqueness sub-cases) were the most
frequent, with QWEN exhibiting the highest rate
(3.48 / 1k~words), followed by DeepSeek (1.60) and
GPT (1.54). Recurring patterns included incorrect
postposition usage
(\foreignlanguage{arabic}{نے},
 \foreignlanguage{arabic}{کو},
 \foreignlanguage{arabic}{سے}), broken verb--subject
agreement, malformed compound sentences, and
oblique-case agreement failures
(\foreignlanguage{arabic}{چھوٹی سی لنگر خانہ}
$\rightarrow$
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{چھوٹا سا لنگر خانہ}).

\textbf{Spelling errors} (N~=~77) were comparatively
uniform across models in absolute count (GPT~16,
QWEN~39, DeepSeek~22) but show QWEN at almost
3$\times$ the per-1{,}000-word rate of the other
models (0.91 vs.\ $\approx$0.35), suggesting that
spelling degradation in QWEN is not merely a function
of output length.

\textbf{Space Deletion} (N~=~78), where two distinct
words are merged without intervening whitespace, was
the third most frequent linguistic error, dominated
by \textbf{GPT} (54 occurrences; 1.10 / 1k~words
--- 9$\times$ QWEN's rate). \textbf{Space Insertion}
was rare (N~=~1), confirming that over-merging is the
prevalent failure mode rather than over-splitting.
Both reflect well-documented challenges of whitespace
normalisation in Urdu NLP \citep{xxx}, arising from
the cursive nature of Nastaliq script and
inconsistent zero-width non-joiner (ZWNJ) handling.

\subsubsection{Semantic Coherence}

Semantic failures (N~=~133) split into two labels:

\textbf{Context Mismatch} (N~=~75) --- direct
factual contradiction of a fact established earlier
in the story --- was distributed unevenly, with QWEN
producing nearly twice the absolute count (37) of
GPT (18) and DeepSeek (20), and 2.6$\times$ GPT's
per-word rate. Mismatches were particularly prominent
in stories exceeding 1{,}500 words, suggesting
degradation of long-range coherence during extended
generation.

\textbf{Semantic Anomaly} (N~=~58) --- sentences
that are syntactically well-formed but semantically
impossible or meaningless --- was highest in GPT
(28) and almost absent in DeepSeek (5). A typical
case:
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{آگ کے چراغ سے اُبلتی چائے پی رہا تھا}
(``he was drinking tea boiling from a lamp of
fire''), where every lexical item is well-formed but
their composition violates real-world plausibility.

\textbf{Language register violations.} A qualitatively
distinct failure mode was observed \emph{exclusively
in DeepSeek}: substitution of Urdu with Punjabi at
the phrase or sentence level within an otherwise Urdu
narrative. Because the prompt explicitly specifies
Urdu, this constitutes a discourse-level
prompt-compliance failure, suggesting that DeepSeek
conflates Pakistani linguistic identity with Punjabi
(the majority regional language) rather than
maintaining the prompted language boundary.

\subsubsection{Cultural Fidelity}

Manual cultural errors were rare (N~=~8): Western
references (N~=~6, e.g.\
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{جان نے کرسمس ٹری سجایا})
in GPT and DeepSeek, and religiously inappropriate
content (N~=~2). QWEN produced no Western references,
possibly reflecting stronger regional alignment in
its training data. However, the low manual count
substantially under-represents the cultural-failure
signal: automated analyses
(Sections~\ref{subsec:em_dash}--\ref{subsec:script_contamination})
reveal pervasive sub-sentential interference.
```

## 9️⃣ Expanded Limitations (top-level section, replaces current)

```latex name=section_limitations.tex
\section{Limitations}
\label{sec:limitations}

\paragraph{Corpus size.} We evaluate 31 titles
($\times$ 3 models = 93 stories). This size was
selected to enable exhaustive sentence-level manual
annotation by five native speakers; a larger corpus
would necessitate sampling-based evaluation.

\paragraph{Single generation per (model, title).}
We use one generation per prompt to isolate model
capacity from sampling variance. Repeated sampling
with $k$~=~3--5 generations would permit
variance-aware claims but was outside our annotation
budget.

\paragraph{Annotation reliability.} While IAA on the
60-story subset was $\kappa$~=~\textbf{xxx}, the
remaining 33 stories rely on a single annotator's
judgement.

\paragraph{Exact-phrase TRR.} TRR may underestimate
title anchoring when models decompose multi-word
titles. GPT's \foreignlanguage{arabic}{گرم کوٹ}
(TRR~=~0.16) illustrates this: the exact phrase
appeared only 3 times, yet
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{کوٹ} alone was used
extensively. Partial-word matching and synonym
detection are directions for future refinement.

\paragraph{Title translation confound.} For TSST
items, the chosen Urdu rendering may influence
model behaviour. We mitigated this by using
published Urdu titles where available and by
GPT-5.1 + native-speaker verification otherwise,
but a sensitivity analysis comparing alternative
renderings is left for future work.

\paragraph{Closed-source model dynamics.} The three
evaluated LLMs are closed-source APIs whose
behaviour may change between provider snapshots; we
record exact access dates and versions to support
re-evaluation.

\paragraph{ALA-LC vs.\ phonemic transcription.}
Transliterations follow ALA-LC for consistency; this
may differ from phonemic Urdu pronunciation that
native readers expect.
```
