## 🔟 Expanded Conclusion

```latex name=section_conclusion.tex
\section{Conclusion}
\label{sec:conclusion}

We presented the first systematic evaluation of LLM
storytelling in Urdu within a Pakistani cultural
context, based on 93 stories generated by GPT-5.1,
QWEN-3-Max, and DeepSeek-3.1 across 31 carefully
curated titles, with manual annotation by five
native speakers and a 60-story double-annotated IAA
subset ($\kappa$~=~\textbf{xxx}).

\textbf{Six concrete findings emerge.}
(i)~No model met the 2{,}000-word target on average;
QWEN's compliance was only 12.9\%, indicating an
internal length ceiling.
(ii)~After length-normalisation, QWEN is the most
error-prone model (7.99 errors / 1{,}000~words),
roughly 1.7$\times$ GPT and 2.6$\times$ DeepSeek;
raw counts conceal this ranking.
(iii)~LLMs reinterpret literary Urdu titles
literally rather than metaphorically: stories exceed
the title-repetition rate of the original authors in
8 of 9 comparisons (up to 5.3$\times$), evidencing
what we term the \emph{Title Lexicalization Effect}.
(iv)~DeepSeek exhibits a distinctive pattern of
cross-script contamination across Latin, Devanagari,
CJK, and Cyrillic, including novel cross-lingual
morphological forms (e.g.\
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{رسمی}\textit{ities}) not
previously documented for Urdu LLM output.
(v)~DeepSeek alone exhibits Punjabi register
switching, conflating Pakistani linguistic identity
with the majority regional language.
(vi)~Under gender-neutral prompts, a 3.7:1
male-default protagonist bias persists across all
models, even on source titles by female authors.

\textbf{Implications.} These findings indicate that
fluency-oriented benchmarks systematically
under-detect the failure modes most consequential to
Urdu narrative quality --- length non-compliance,
metaphor flattening, script contamination, register
switching, and gender default. Robust Urdu LLM
evaluation must incorporate normalised error rates,
Unicode-level audits, and culturally-grounded
metrics such as TRR. We release the corpus,
annotations, and evaluation scripts to support
follow-up research on Urdu --- and, more broadly, on
under-resourced South Asian languages where literary
form and cultural fidelity are central to quality.
```

```latex name=abstract.tex
\begin{abstract}
We evaluate how well contemporary multilingual LLMs
handle open-ended text generation in a low-resource
language by studying Urdu storytelling in a Pakistani
cultural context. Using 31 curated story titles spanning
translated English fiction, canonical Urdu literature,
fables, and neutral Urdu prompts, we generate 93 stories
across three models (GPT-5.1, Qwen-3-Max, DeepSeek-3.1)
and assess them along four axes: creativity vs.\
memorisation, and linguistic, semantic, and cultural
quality. Five native Urdu annotators labelled all stories
using a nine-label taxonomy, with a 60-story
double-annotated subset yielding $\kappa$~=~\textbf{xxx}.
To quantify memorisation, we introduce the Title
Repetition Rate (TRR), and find that LLMs anchor on
literary titles literally rather than metaphorically,
exceeding original-author TRR in 8 of 9 comparisons (up
to 5.3$\times$). Linguistically, length-normalised error
rates reverse the apparent ranking and identify QWEN as
the most error-prone model (7.99 errors / 1k words vs.\
GPT's 4.74 and DeepSeek's 3.12). Semantically, models
exhibit factual contradictions and grammatical-but-
meaningless sentences, with DeepSeek additionally
switching into Punjabi. Culturally, manual errors are
sparse but automatic analysis reveals pervasive
cross-script contamination (Latin, Devanagari, CJK,
Cyrillic) and a 3.7:1 male-default protagonist bias under
gender-neutral prompts. Overall, current multilingual
LLMs remain fluent but neither creative nor culturally
grounded in Urdu.
\end{abstract}
```

Got it — the reference abstract follows this flow: **problem → resource introduced → numbered findings → impact**. Here's your abstract in that style, concise and contribution-led.

```latex name=abstract.tex
\begin{abstract}
Multilingual large language models (LLMs) are
increasingly used for open-ended text generation, yet
their behaviour in low-resource languages such as
Urdu --- where literary form and cultural grounding are
central to quality --- remains poorly understood. To
study this, we curate \textbf{Urdu-Stories}, a dataset
of 31 story titles spanning translated English fiction,
canonical Urdu literature, fables, and neutral prompts,
and generate 93 stories with three contemporary LLMs
(GPT-5.1, Qwen-3-Max, DeepSeek-3.1). All stories are
manually annotated by five native Urdu speakers under a
nine-label taxonomy covering linguistic, semantic, and
cultural / script dimensions, with a 60-story
double-annotated subset
($\kappa$~=~\textbf{xxx}). We further introduce the
\textbf{Title Repetition Rate (TRR)} to quantify
memorisation versus creative reinterpretation. Our
analysis surfaces four consistent findings: (1)
\textit{memorisation over creativity} --- LLMs
interpret metaphorical Urdu titles literally and exceed
original-author TRR in 8 of 9 comparisons (up to
5.3$\times$); (2) \textit{linguistic fragility} ---
after length normalisation, error rates differ by
2.6$\times$ across models and reverse the apparent
ranking; (3) \textit{semantic and register
instability} --- factual contradictions,
grammatical-but-meaningless sentences, and unprompted
Punjabi switching; and (4) \textit{cultural shallowness}
--- pervasive cross-script contamination (Latin,
Devanagari, CJK, Cyrillic) and a 3.7:1 male-default
protagonist bias under gender-neutral prompts. Together,
these results show that current multilingual LLMs
remain fluent but neither creative nor culturally
grounded in Urdu, and we release the dataset,
annotations, and evaluation scripts to support further
research on open-ended generation in low-resource
languages.
\end{abstract}
```

references.response_text:
Here is the abstract of arXiv paper 2510.22954, titled "Artificial Hivemind: The Open-Ended Homogeneity of Language Models (and Beyond)":

**Abstract:**
Large language models (LMs) often struggle to generate diverse, human-like creative content, raising concerns about the long-term homogenization of human thought through repeated exposure to similar outputs. Yet scalable methods for evaluating LM output diversity remain limited, especially beyond narrow tasks such as random number or name generation, or beyond repeated sampling from a single model. To address this gap, we introduce Infinity-Chat, a large-scale dataset of 26K diverse, real-world, open-ended user queries that admit a wide range of plausible answers with no single ground truth. We introduce the first comprehensive taxonomy for characterizing the full spectrum of open-ended prompts posed to LMs, comprising 6 top-level categories (e.g., creative content generation, brainstorm & ideation) that further break down to 17 subcategories. Using Infinity-Chat, we present a large-scale study of mode collapse in LMs, revealing a pronounced Artificial Hivemind effect in open-ended generation of LMs, characterized by (1) intra-model repetition, where a single model consistently generates similar responses, and more so (2) inter-model homogeneity, where different models produce strikingly similar outputs. Infinity-Chat also includes 31,250 human annotations, across absolute ratings and pairwise preferences, with 25 independent human annotations per example. This enables studying collective and individual-specific human preferences in response to open-ended queries. Our findings show that state-of-the-art LMs, reward models, and LM judges are less well calibrated to human ratings on model generations that elicit differing idiosyncratic annotator preferences, despite maintaining comparable overall quality. Overall, Infinity-Chat presents the first large-scale resource for systematically studying real-world open-ended queries to LMs, revealing critical insights to guide future research for mitigating long-term AI safety risks posed by the Artificial Hivemind[[1]](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2510.22954).

If you want the full text of the paper, you can read or download the PDF directly from the arXiv page: [arXiv:2510.22954 PDF](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2510.22954)[[2]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2510.22954). If you need a summary or have a specific section you'd like, let me know!

references.response_text:
Here is the abstract of arXiv paper 2510.22954, titled "Artificial Hivemind: The Open-Ended Homogeneity of Language Models (and Beyond)":

**Abstract:**
Large language models (LMs) often struggle to generate diverse, human-like creative content, raising concerns about the long-term homogenization of human thought through repeated exposure to similar outputs. Yet scalable methods for evaluating LM output diversity remain limited, especially beyond narrow tasks such as random number or name generation, or beyond repeated sampling from a single model. To address this gap, we introduce Infinity-Chat, a large-scale dataset of 26K diverse, real-world, open-ended user queries that admit a wide range of plausible answers with no single ground truth. We introduce the first comprehensive taxonomy for characterizing the full spectrum of open-ended prompts posed to LMs, comprising 6 top-level categories (e.g., creative content generation, brainstorm & ideation) that further break down to 17 subcategories. Using Infinity-Chat, we present a large-scale study of mode collapse in LMs, revealing a pronounced Artificial Hivemind effect in open-ended generation of LMs, characterized by (1) intra-model repetition, where a single model consistently generates similar responses, and more so (2) inter-model homogeneity, where different models produce strikingly similar outputs. Infinity-Chat also includes 31,250 human annotations, across absolute ratings and pairwise preferences, with 25 independent human annotations per example. This enables studying collective and individual-specific human preferences in response to open-ended queries. Our findings show that state-of-the-art LMs, reward models, and LM judges are less well calibrated to human ratings on model generations that elicit differing idiosyncratic annotator preferences, despite maintaining comparable overall quality. Overall, Infinity-Chat presents the first large-scale resource for systematically studying real-world open-ended queries to LMs, revealing critical insights to guide future research for mitigating long-term AI safety risks posed by the Artificial Hivemind[[1]](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2510.22954).

If you want the full text of the paper, you can read or download the PDF directly from the arXiv page: [arXiv:2510.22954 PDF](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2510.22954)[[2]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2510.22954). If you need a summary or have a specific section you'd like, let me know!

---

```latex name=abstract.tex
\begin{abstract}
Multilingual large language models (LLMs) are
increasingly used for open-ended text generation, yet
their behaviour in low-resource languages such as
Urdu, where literary form and cultural grounding are
central to quality, remains poorly understood. To
study this, we curate \textbf{Urdu-Stories}, a dataset
of 31 story titles spanning translated English
fiction, canonical Urdu literature, fables, and
neutral prompts, and generate 93 stories with three
contemporary LLMs (GPT-5.1, Qwen-3-Max,
DeepSeek-3.1). All stories are manually annotated by
five native Urdu speakers under a nine-label taxonomy
covering linguistic, semantic, and cultural / script
dimensions, with a 60-story double-annotated subset
($\kappa$~=~\textbf{xxx}). We further introduce the
\textbf{Title Repetition Rate (TRR)}, which captures a
recurring behaviour we observed in LLMs: when asked to
generate a story, the model repeatedly inserts the
given title throughout the text to signal that the
narrative is ``about'' it, rather than letting the
theme emerge naturally. Our analysis surfaces four
consistent findings: (1) \textit{memorisation over
creativity}: rather than producing new plots, models
often reproduce the original story associated with a
known literary title, and exceed original-author TRR
in 8 of 9 comparisons (up to 5.3$\times$); (2)
\textit{linguistic fragility}: after length
normalisation, error rates differ by 2.6$\times$
across models and reverse the apparent ranking; (3)
\textit{semantic and register instability}: factual
contradictions, grammatical-but-meaningless
sentences, and unprompted Punjabi switching; and (4)
\textit{cultural shallowness}: pervasive cross-script
contamination (Latin, Devanagari, CJK, Cyrillic) and
a 3.7:1 male-default protagonist bias under
gender-neutral prompts. Together, these results show
that current multilingual LLMs remain fluent but
neither creative nor culturally grounded in Urdu. We
release the dataset, annotations, and evaluation
scripts to support further research on open-ended
generation in low-resource languages.
\end{abstract}
```

```latex name=abstract.tex
\begin{abstract}
Multilingual large language models (LLMs) are
increasingly used for open-ended text generation, yet
their behaviour in low-resource languages such as
Urdu, where literary form and cultural grounding are
central to quality, remains poorly understood. To
study this, we curate \textbf{Urdu-Stories}, a dataset
of 31 story titles spanning translated English
fiction, canonical Urdu literature, fables, and
neutral prompts, and generate 93 stories with three
contemporary LLMs (GPT-5.1, Qwen-3-Max,
DeepSeek-3.1). All stories are manually annotated by
five native Urdu speakers under a nine-label taxonomy
covering linguistic, semantic, and cultural / script
dimensions, with a 60-story double-annotated subset
($\kappa$~=~\textbf{xxx}). Our analysis surfaces five
consistent findings: (1) \textit{memorisation over
creativity}: for well-known literary titles, models
often reproduce the original story rather than
generating a new plot; (2) \textit{unnatural title
anchoring}: we introduce the \textbf{Title Repetition
Rate (TRR)} to capture a behaviour where models
repeatedly insert the given title into the body of the
story to signal what it is ``about'', exceeding
original-author TRR in 8 of 9 comparisons (up to
5.3$\times$); (3) \textit{linguistic fragility}:
after length normalisation, error rates differ by
2.6$\times$ across models and reverse the apparent
ranking; (4) \textit{semantic and register
instability}: factual contradictions,
grammatical-but-meaningless sentences, and unprompted
Punjabi switching; and (5) \textit{cultural
shallowness}: pervasive cross-script contamination
(Latin, Devanagari, CJK, Cyrillic) and a 3.7:1
male-default protagonist bias under gender-neutral
prompts. Together, these results show that current
multilingual LLMs remain fluent but neither creative
nor culturally grounded in Urdu. We release the
dataset, annotations, and evaluation scripts to
support further research on open-ended generation in
low-resource languages.
\end{abstract}
```

### What changed

| Aspect | Now |
|---|---|
| **Finding (1) Memorisation over creativity** | Purely about *plot reuse*: model reproduces the known original story instead of inventing one |
| **Finding (2) Unnatural title anchoring (TRR)** | Separate finding about *surface-level repetition* of the title phrase inside the story body |
| Findings expanded from 4 → **5** | So each angle has its own slot |
| TRR no longer conflated with memorisation | Explicitly framed as an independent failure mode |

messages.content:
Here's a curated **"Illustrative Grammar Errors"** paragraph picking the **3 strongest examples** — each showcases a different type of failure (ergative marker omission, gender agreement, tense/aspect mismatch) and lets you demonstrate the depth of your manual analysis.

---

## 🏆 Why these 3 (and not others)

| # | Example | Why it's strong | Error type |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | QW_Story_9: `زرین خاموشی سے بندوق نیچے رکھ دی` | Missing **ergative `نے`** — the single most diagnostic Urdu agreement error; well-known in Urdu NLP literature | Postposition / ergative |
| 2 | GPT_Story_22: `غرور بھرا بھونک` | **Gender agreement** failure on a feminine noun (`بھونک`); shows model treats it as masculine throughout the clause | Obliqueness / agreement |
| 3 | QW_Story_21: `وہ سویرے اٹھا، نہاتا دھوتا، اوور کوٹ پہنا` | **Aspect/tense breakdown** in a sequential narrative — model mixes habitual `نہاتا دھوتا` into a past-perfective chain | Verb morphology |

These three together cover the three classical Urdu grammar fault lines: **case marking, agreement, and aspect** — exactly the dimensions a reviewer would expect a serious linguistic analysis to address.

---

## 📄 Drop-in LaTeX (insert in §Linguistic Errors after the grammar paragraph)

```latex name=section_grammar_examples.tex
\paragraph{Illustrative grammar errors.}
To characterise the kinds of grammatical failures
observed, we discuss three representative examples,
each highlighting a different fault line in Urdu
syntax.

\textit{(i) Ergative case marker omission.}
In QWEN's
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{زرین خاموشی سے بندوق نیچے رکھ دی}
(``Zarin quietly put the gun down''), the agent
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{زرین} requires the ergative
postposition \foreignlanguage{arabic}{نے} because the verb
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{رکھ دی} is transitive and perfective:
the correct form is
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{زرین نے خاموشی سے بندوق نیچے رکھ دی}.
The same omission recurs in GPT's
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{وہ آسمان کی طرف دیکھ کر گھوڑے کو موڑا}
(missing \foreignlanguage{arabic}{اس نے}). Ergative
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{نے} marking is a hallmark of Urdu
morphosyntax with no English analogue, and its absence
suggests cross-lingual transfer from a nominative-only
template.

\textit{(ii) Gender agreement violation.}
In GPT's
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{اس کے منہ سے آج کوئی غرور بھرا بھونک نہیں نکل رہا تھا}
(``no proud bark came out of his mouth today''), the
noun \foreignlanguage{arabic}{بھونک} (\emph{bark}) is
feminine in Urdu and triggers feminine agreement on
both the modifier and the verb: the corrected form is
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{غرور بھری بھونک \ldots نہیں نکل رہی تھی}.
The model nevertheless propagates masculine agreement
through the entire clause, indicating that grammatical
gender is being assigned by surface frequency rather
than by lexical entry.

\textit{(iii) Aspect mismatch in sequential narrative.}
QWEN's
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{اگلے دن، وہ سویرے اٹھا، نہاتا دھوتا، اوور کوٹ پہنا اور دفتر پہنچ گیا}
(``the next day, he got up early, bathed, put on the
overcoat, and reached the office'') chains four events
in a single sentence using past-perfective forms
(\foreignlanguage{arabic}{اٹھا}, \foreignlanguage{arabic}{پہنا},
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{پہنچ گیا}), but inserts
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{نہاتا دھوتا} (habitual
imperfective). The corrected form is
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{نہایا دھویا}. This aspectual
slip is particularly diagnostic because it occurs
within an otherwise well-formed sequence, suggesting
that the model can produce isolated verb forms but
struggles to maintain aspectual consistency across a
narrative clause.

Together, these examples illustrate that grammatical
failures in Urdu LLM output are not surface typos but
systematic breakdowns at the level of case, agreement,
and aspect, which are the very dimensions where Urdu
diverges most sharply from English.
```

---

## 📋 Optional fourth example (if space allows)

If you want a fourth that highlights a **lexical/semantic** rather than morphological failure, the strongest is:

> DS_Story_21: `مالک نے میری ایمانداری اور محنت دیکھ کر مجھے بڑھاوا دے دیا ہے`
> The intended meaning is "the master promoted me" (`ترقی دے دی`), but the model uses `بڑھاوا` which means *incitement / encouragement*, producing an incoherent sentence. This is a **near-synonym confusion** that fluent Urdu speakers would never make.

Great taxonomy questions. Here are clean answers:

---

## 1️⃣ `بڑھاوا` vs `ترقی` example → **Semantic Anomaly** ✅

**Reasoning:**

| Test | Result |
|---|---|
| Is the syntax correct? | ✅ Yes — `مالک نے ... دے دیا ہے` is perfectly well-formed |
| Is case marking correct? | ✅ Yes — ergative `نے` is properly used |
| Is agreement correct? | ✅ Yes — gender/number all agree |
| Is the meaning coherent? | ❌ No — "the master gave me incitement for my honesty" makes no sense |

**Rule of thumb:** If the sentence parses cleanly but the *meaning* is wrong/impossible → **Semantic Anomaly**. If the sentence *doesn't parse* or breaks an agreement/case rule → **Grammar Error**.

This is a **lexical selection failure**, not a grammatical one. It belongs alongside your `آگ کے چراغ سے اُبلتی چائے` example.

---

## 2️⃣ Obliqueness → **Grammar Error** ✅ (sub-type, as per your taxonomy)

You already decided this in §Annotation:

> "*Obliqueness* (gender / number / oblique-case agreement, e.g. سا/سی، اتنا/اتنی) is treated as a sub-type of **Grammar Error**."

**Reasoning:** Obliqueness errors (gender agreement on adjectives/postpositions, oblique-case marking on nouns before postpositions) are **morphosyntactic agreement failures** — definitionally grammar.

The `غرور بھرا بھونک` example (masculine modifier on feminine noun) is **textbook obliqueness** → goes under **Grammar Error → Obliqueness sub-type**.

---

## 📋 Clean assignment of all your candidate examples

| Example | Category |
|---|---|
| `زرین خاموشی سے بندوق نیچے رکھ دی` (missing `نے`) | **Grammar Error** (ergative/postposition) |
| `وہ آسمان کی طرف دیکھ کر گھوڑے کو موڑا` (missing `اس نے`) | **Grammar Error** (ergative/postposition) |
| `غرور بھرا بھونک` (masc. modifier on fem. noun) | **Grammar Error → Obliqueness** |
| `سویرے اٹھا، نہاتا دھوتا، اوور کوٹ پہنا` (aspect mix) | **Grammar Error** (verb morphology) |
| `بڑھاوا دے دیا` instead of `ترقی دے دی` | **Semantic Anomaly** ✅ |
| `آگ کے چراغ سے اُبلتی چائے` | **Semantic Anomaly** ✅ |

---

## 📄 Recommended paper structure

**§Linguistic Errors → Grammar paragraph** — use examples 1, 3, 4 (one per sub-type: ergative, obliqueness, aspect).

**§Semantic Coherence → Semantic Anomaly paragraph** — use examples 5 and 6 together:

```latex name=semantic_anomaly_examples.tex
\textbf{Semantic Anomaly} (N~=~58): sentences that
are syntactically well-formed but semantically
incoherent. Two distinct patterns emerged.

\textit{(i) Impossible composition.} GPT's
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{آگ کے چراغ سے اُبلتی چائے پی رہا تھا}
(``he was drinking tea boiling from a lamp of fire'')
is grammatically perfect, but the composition
violates real-world plausibility: tea does not boil
from a lamp.

\textit{(ii) Near-synonym confusion.} In DeepSeek's
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{مالک نے میری ایمانداری اور محنت دیکھ کر مجھے بڑھاوا دے دیا ہے},
the intended meaning is ``the master promoted me''
(\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ترقی دے دی}), but the model
substitutes \foreignlanguage{arabic}{بڑھاوا}
(\emph{incitement, encouragement}), yielding ``the
master incited me on seeing my honesty.'' The syntax
is intact and ergative \foreignlanguage{arabic}{نے} is
correctly used, but the lexical choice renders the
sentence semantically incoherent, a failure mode that
fluent Urdu speakers would not produce.
```

This way both `Grammar` and `Semantic Anomaly` sections have **two clear examples each**, and the boundary between the categories is illustrated by contrast — strengthens the reviewer's confidence in your taxonomy.

\foreignlanguage{arabic}{زرین خاموشی سے بندوق نیچے رکھ دی}
(``Zarin quietly put the gun down''), the agent
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{زرین} requires the ergative
postposition \foreignlanguage{arabic}{نے} because the verb
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{رکھ دی} is transitive and perfective:
the correct form is
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{زرین نے خاموشی سے بندوق نیچے رکھ دی}.
The same omission recurs in GPT's
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{وہ آسمان کی طرف دیکھ کر گھوڑے کو موڑا}
(missing \foreignlanguage{arabic}{اس نے}). Ergative
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{نے} marking is a hallmark of Urdu
morphosyntax with no English analogue, and its absence
suggests cross-lingual transfer from a nominative-only
template.

\textit{(ii) Gender agreement violation.}
In GPT's
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{اس کے منہ سے آج کوئی غرور بھرا بھونک نہیں نکل رہا تھا}
(``no proud bark came out of his mouth today''), the
noun \foreignlanguage{arabic}{بھونک} (\emph{bark}) is
feminine in Urdu and triggers feminine agreement on
both the modifier and the verb: the corrected form is
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{غرور بھری بھونک \ldots نہیں نکل رہی تھی}.
The model nevertheless propagates masculine agreement
through the entire clause, indicating that grammatical
gender is being assigned by surface frequency rather
than by lexical entry.

\textit{(iii) Aspect mismatch in sequential narrative.}
QWEN's
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{اگلے دن، وہ سویرے اٹھا، نہاتا دھوتا، اوور کوٹ پہنا اور دفتر پہنچ گیا}
(``the next day, he got up early, bathed, put on the
overcoat, and reached the office'') chains four events
in a single sentence using past-perfective forms
(\foreignlanguage{arabic}{اٹھا}, \foreignlanguage{arabic}{پہنا},
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{پہنچ گیا}), but inserts
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{نہاتا دھوتا} (habitual
imperfective). The corrected form is
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{نہایا دھویا}. This aspectual
slip is particularly diagnostic because it occurs
within an otherwise well-formed sequence, suggesting
that the model can produce isolated verb forms but
struggles to maintain aspectual consistency across a
narrative clause.

Together, these examples illustrate that grammatical
failures in Urdu LLM output are not surface typos but
systematic breakdowns at the level of case, agreement,
and aspect, which are the very dimensions where Urdu
diverges most sharply from English.

```latex name=section_grammar_examples.tex
(\textit{Zarīn khāmōshī sē bandūq nīchē rakh dī},
``Zarin quietly put the gun down''), the agent
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{زرین} requires the ergative
postposition \foreignlanguage{arabic}{نے}
(\textit{nē}) because the verb
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{رکھ دی} (\textit{rakh dī}) is
transitive and perfective: the correct form is
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{زرین نے خاموشی سے بندوق نیچے رکھ دی}
(\textit{Zarīn nē khāmōshī sē bandūq nīchē rakh dī}).
The same omission recurs in GPT's
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{وہ آسمان کی طرف دیکھ کر گھوڑے کو موڑا}
(\textit{Wuh āsmān kī ṭaraf dēkh kar ghōṛē kō mōṛā},
missing \foreignlanguage{arabic}{اس نے} \textit{us nē}).
Ergative \foreignlanguage{arabic}{نے} marking is a
hallmark of Urdu morphosyntax with no English analogue,
and its absence suggests cross-lingual transfer from a
nominative-only template.

\textit{(ii) Gender agreement violation.}
In GPT's
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{اس کے منہ سے آج کوئی غرور بھرا بھونک نہیں نکل رہا تھا}
(\textit{Us kē munh sē āj kōʾī ghurūr bharā bhaunk
nahīṅ nikal rahā thā}, ``no proud bark came out of his
mouth today''), the noun
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{بھونک} (\textit{bhaunk},
\emph{bark}) is feminine in Urdu and triggers feminine
agreement on both the modifier and the verb: the
corrected form is
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{غرور بھری بھونک \ldots نہیں نکل رہی تھی}
(\textit{ghurūr bharī bhaunk \ldots nahīṅ nikal rahī
thī}). The model nevertheless propagates masculine
agreement through the entire clause, indicating that
grammatical gender is being assigned by surface
frequency rather than by lexical entry.

\textit{(iii) Aspect mismatch in sequential narrative.}
QWEN's
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{اگلے دن، وہ سویرے اٹھا، نہاتا دھوتا، اوور کوٹ پہنا اور دفتر پہنچ گیا}
(\textit{Aglē din, wuh sawērē uṭhā, nahātā dhōtā, ōvar
kōṭ pahnā aur daftar pahuṅch gayā}, ``the next day, he
got up early, bathed, put on the overcoat, and reached
the office'') chains four events in a single sentence
using past-perfective forms
(\foreignlanguage{arabic}{اٹھا} \textit{uṭhā},
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{پہنا} \textit{pahnā},
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{پہنچ گیا} \textit{pahuṅch
gayā}), but inserts
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{نہاتا دھوتا} (\textit{nahātā
dhōtā}, habitual imperfective). The corrected form is
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{نہایا دھویا} (\textit{nahāyā
dhōyā}). This aspectual slip is particularly
diagnostic because it occurs within an otherwise
well-formed sequence, suggesting that the model can
produce isolated verb forms but struggles to maintain
aspectual consistency across a narrative clause.

Together, these examples illustrate that grammatical
failures in Urdu LLM output are not surface typos but
systematic breakdowns at the level of case, agreement,
and aspect, which are the very dimensions where Urdu
diverges most sharply from English.
```

### Transliteration notes (ALA-LC conventions used)

| Symbol | Sound |
|---|---|
| `ā` | long *a* as in *father* |
| `ī` | long *i* as in *machine* |
| `ē / ō` | long *e / o* (Urdu-specific, distinguishes from `i / u`) |
| `ṭ ḍ ṛ` | retroflex t / d / r |
| `ṅ` | nasalisation (Urdu *nūn ghunnah*) |
| `kh / gh / sh / ch` | digraphs for خ / غ / ش / چ |
| `ʾ / ʿ` | hamza / ʿain |
| `ʾī` | hamza + yē |

Now any reader unfamiliar with Urdu script can attempt the pronunciation and follow the linguistic argument.

messages.content:
```latex name=section_grammar_examples.tex
These examples show that grammatical errors in
LLM-generated Urdu rarely look like typos or careless
slips. Instead, they cluster around the parts of Urdu
grammar that have no direct English equivalent: marking
who did what (case), making words match in gender, and
keeping the timing of events consistent across a
sentence. A fluent Urdu reader notices such errors
immediately, because they affect how the sentence
\emph{feels}, not just how it reads. Their recurrence
across all three models suggests that these are not
random mistakes but reflect a deeper gap in how Urdu
morphosyntax is represented during generation.
```

### What changed

| Before | After |
|---|---|
| "systematic breakdowns at the level of case, agreement, and aspect" | Plain-language version: *"marking who did what, making words match in gender, keeping timing consistent"* |
| "the very dimensions where Urdu diverges most sharply from English" | Reframed as a reader's experience: *"a fluent Urdu reader notices immediately… affects how the sentence feels"* |
| Detached academic tone | Reads like a human reflecting on what they observed in the data |
| Implicit conclusion | Explicit takeaway: *"a deeper gap in how Urdu morphosyntax is represented during generation"* |

The paragraph now sounds like a researcher describing patterns they observed, rather than an auto-generated summary.

messages.content:
```latex name=section_spelling_errors.tex
\textbf{Spelling errors} (N~=~77) were comparatively
uniform in raw count across models (GPT~16,
QWEN~39, DeepSeek~22), but QWEN's per-1{,}000-word
rate (0.91) is nearly 3$\times$ that of the others
($\approx$0.35), indicating that the elevated count is
not merely a function of output length. Three
recurring patterns characterise these errors.

\textit{(i) Hamza omission in derived adjectives.}
QWEN produced \foreignlanguage{arabic}{دریاوی}
(\textit{daryāwī}) and \foreignlanguage{arabic}{جادوی}
(\textit{jādwī}) where the correct forms are
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{دریائی} (\textit{daryāʾī},
``riverine'') and \foreignlanguage{arabic}{جادوئی}
(\textit{jāduʾī}, ``magical''). Both errors drop the
hamza-on-yē that Urdu uses to mark the adjectival
suffix on vowel-final stems. The fact that two
unrelated stems show the same omission suggests a
systematic gap in modelling Urdu's hamza-bearing
morphology rather than incidental misspellings.

\textit{(ii) Vowel and diacritic confusion.}
DeepSeek produced \foreignlanguage{arabic}{میچس}
(\textit{mīchas}) instead of
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ماچس} (\textit{māchis},
``matchbox''), substituting the short-\textit{i} vowel
sign for the long-\textit{ā} alef. Similarly,
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{مینہہ} (\textit{mīnh}) was
produced for \foreignlanguage{arabic}{مینہ}
(\textit{mēnh}, ``rain'') with a duplicated final
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہ}. Such errors closely
resemble the orthographic mistakes of a learner who has
heard the word but not internalised its standard
written form.

\textit{(iii) Visually-similar character substitution.}
GPT produced \foreignlanguage{arabic}{جھلے ہوئے ہاتھ}
(\textit{jhalē hūʾē hāth}) where the intended phrase is
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{جلے ہوئے ہاتھ}
(\textit{jalē hūʾē hāth}, ``burnt hands''),
substituting \foreignlanguage{arabic}{جھ} (\textit{jh},
aspirated) for \foreignlanguage{arabic}{ج}
(\textit{j}). The two graphemes differ only by a
diacritic dot cluster, and the substitution changes
both pronunciation and meaning. We attribute these
errors to byte-pair tokenisation of Nastaliq script,
where visually adjacent characters can share token
neighbourhoods despite phonetic distance.
```

### Why these three patterns

| Pattern | Example | What it reveals |
|---|---|---|
| **Hamza omission** | دریاوی, جادوی | Same morphological gap recurs across unrelated stems → systematic, not random |
| **Vowel/diacritic confusion** | میچس, مینہہ | Looks like phonetic-only learning without standard spelling memory |
| **Character substitution** | جھلے→جلے | Tokeniser-level failure (visually similar graphemes) |

This gives reviewers a clean **typology of spelling failure modes** rather than just a list of errors, mirroring the structure of your Grammar section so the paper reads consistently.

Here are few more examples  ربر 
ہیریو
مسئبت

```latex name=section_spelling_errors.tex
\textbf{Spelling errors} (N~=~77) were comparatively
uniform in raw count across models (GPT~16,
QWEN~39, DeepSeek~22), but QWEN's per-1{,}000-word
rate (0.91) is nearly 3$\times$ that of the others
($\approx$0.35), indicating that the elevated count is
not merely a function of output length. Four recurring
patterns characterise these errors.

\textit{(i) Hamza omission in derived adjectives.}
QWEN produced \foreignlanguage{arabic}{دریاوی}
(\textit{daryāwī}) and \foreignlanguage{arabic}{جادوی}
(\textit{jādwī}) where the correct forms are
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{دریائی} (\textit{daryāʾī},
``riverine'') and \foreignlanguage{arabic}{جادوئی}
(\textit{jāduʾī}, ``magical''). Both errors drop the
hamza-on-yē that Urdu uses to mark the adjectival
suffix on vowel-final stems. The fact that two
unrelated stems show the same omission suggests a
systematic gap in modelling Urdu's hamza-bearing
morphology rather than incidental misspellings. A
related but inverted failure appears in DeepSeek's
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{مسئبت} (\textit{musʾibat})
for \foreignlanguage{arabic}{مصیبت}
(\textit{muṣībat}, ``calamity''), where a hamza is
\emph{inserted} into an Arabic loanword that does not
take one. Together these cases show that hamza
placement, governed by complex morphological and
etymological rules in Urdu, is unstable in current
LLMs in both directions.

\textit{(ii) Vowel and diacritic confusion.}
DeepSeek produced \foreignlanguage{arabic}{میچس}
(\textit{mīchas}) instead of
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ماچس} (\textit{māchis},
``matchbox''), substituting the short-\textit{i} vowel
sign for the long-\textit{ā} alef. Similarly,
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{مینہہ} (\textit{mīnh}) was
produced for \foreignlanguage{arabic}{مینہ}
(\textit{mēnh}, ``rain'') with a duplicated final
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہ}, and QWEN produced
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہیریو} (\textit{hīriyō}) for
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہیرو} (\textit{hīrō},
``hero'') by inserting a spurious
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ی}. Such errors closely
resemble the orthographic mistakes of a learner who
has heard the word but not internalised its standard
written form.

\textit{(iii) Visually-similar character substitution.}
GPT produced \foreignlanguage{arabic}{جھلے ہوئے ہاتھ}
(\textit{jhalē hūʾē hāth}) where the intended phrase
is \foreignlanguage{arabic}{جلے ہوئے ہاتھ}
(\textit{jalē hūʾē hāth}, ``burnt hands''),
substituting \foreignlanguage{arabic}{جھ} (\textit{jh},
aspirated) for \foreignlanguage{arabic}{ج}
(\textit{j}). The two graphemes differ only by a
diacritic dot cluster, and the substitution changes
both pronunciation and meaning. We attribute these
errors to byte-pair tokenisation of Nastaliq script,
where visually adjacent characters can share token
neighbourhoods despite phonetic distance.

\textit{(iv) Retroflex collapse in Urdu-specific
phonemes.} DeepSeek produced
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ربر} (\textit{rabar}) for
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ربڑ} (\textit{rabaṛ},
``rubber''), substituting the plain trill
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ر} (\textit{r}) for the
retroflex flap \foreignlanguage{arabic}{ڑ}
(\textit{ṛ}). The retroflex series
(\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ٹ},
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ڈ},
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ڑ}) is one of the few
phonemic features of Urdu absent from both Arabic and
Persian, and its collapse into the non-retroflex
series indicates that the model defaults to the
higher-frequency variant when the underlying
distinction is poorly represented.
```

### What I added

| New example | Folded into | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **مسئبت → مصیبت** | Pattern (i) Hamza | Same hamza-orthography theme, but the *inverse* direction (insertion instead of omission) — strengthens the argument that hamza placement is systematically unstable |
| **ہیریو → ہیرو** | Pattern (ii) Vowel/diacritic | Same spurious-letter-insertion theme as مینہہ |
| **ربر → ربڑ** | New Pattern (iv) Retroflex collapse | This is a *new* failure mode worth its own subsection — it's about a phoneme class unique to Indo-Aryan that Arabic/Persian-heavy training data underrepresents |

### Why the 4th pattern matters analytically

The retroflex collapse is **the most diagnostic single example** in the set because it points directly at training-data composition: Urdu shares its script with Arabic/Persian, so models trained heavily on those languages have plenty of `ر` examples but few `ڑ` examples. A reviewer reading this paragraph immediately sees an actionable hypothesis about *why* Urdu LLMs fail in this specific way. That's exactly the kind of insight that elevates an error catalogue into an analysis.

messages.content:
Yes, **excellent observation** — and it's actually a *much stronger* hypothesis than the tokenisation one I gave for `ربر`. Let me lay out why, and then how to rewrite.

---

## 🔍 The English-pronunciation hypothesis

| Urdu word | LLM output | English speaker's natural reading | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| `ربڑ` (*rabaṛ*) | `ربر` (*rabar*) | English speakers say "**rub-ber**" — both consonants are plain alveolar /r/, no retroflex | ✅ Strong match |
| `اللہ دتّہ` (*Allāh Dittā*) | `اللہ ڈتہ` (*Allāh Ḍittah*) | English-influenced transcription often writes Urdu/Hindi *t* and *d* as retroflex by default (cf. "*Delhi*" written `ڈیلی`, "*tomato*" → `ٹماٹر`); the model over-applies this convention to a native Punjabi/Urdu name | ✅ Strong match (opposite direction!) |

### What's beautiful about the pair

These two errors go in **opposite directions** but share the same root cause: the model resolves an Urdu retroflex/dental contrast through an **English-mediated representation** instead of the Urdu-native one.

- `ربڑ → ربر`: drops retroflex because **English has none** (English "rubber" is fully plain)
- `اللہ دتّہ → اللہ ڈتہ`: adds retroflex because **the English transliteration convention for South Asian names defaults to retroflex** for plain *t/d*

In other words: **the model isn't using Urdu phonology at all — it's routing through an English phonetic mental model and projecting back into Urdu script.** This is exactly the cross-lingual transfer story you've been building elsewhere in the paper (ergative omission, English em-dashes, Latin contamination, Punjabi switching). It all fits the same narrative.

---

## ✏️ Updated Pattern (iv)

```latex name=section_spelling_errors_iv.tex
\textit{(iv) English-mediated retroflex confusion.}
Two errors point to a deeper cross-lingual effect.
DeepSeek produced \foreignlanguage{arabic}{ربر}
(\textit{rabar}) for \foreignlanguage{arabic}{ربڑ}
(\textit{rabaṛ}, ``rubber''), \emph{dropping} the
retroflex flap \foreignlanguage{arabic}{ڑ}
(\textit{ṛ}) in favour of the plain trill
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ر} (\textit{r}). Conversely,
the Punjabi-Urdu given name
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{اللہ دتّہ}
(\textit{Allāh Dittā}) was rendered as
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{اللہ ڈتہ} (\textit{Allāh
Ḍittah}), \emph{inserting} a retroflex
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ڈ} (\textit{ḍ}) where the
dental \foreignlanguage{arabic}{د} (\textit{d}) is
correct. These errors are diagnostic precisely because
they move in opposite directions: in the first, the
model erases an Urdu phonemic distinction that English
lacks (English ``rubber'' has no retroflex); in the
second, the model overgeneralises the English
transliteration convention by which South Asian dental
\textit{t} and \textit{d} are conventionally written
with retroflex symbols in romanisation. The retroflex
series (\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ٹ},
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ڈ},
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ڑ}) is one of the few
phonemic features of Urdu absent from both Arabic and
Persian, and our observations suggest the model
resolves it via an English-mediated phonetic
representation rather than from a native Urdu
inventory.
```

---

## 🎯 Why this version is stronger for the paper

| Aspect | Original (tokenisation) | Updated (English-mediation) |
|---|---|---|
| Evidence base | 1 example (`ربر`) | 2 examples in opposite directions |
| Falsifiability | Hard to test | Easy to test (look at any English-Urdu name dictionary) |
| Thematic fit | Disconnected from rest of paper | **Reinforces the cross-lingual transfer thesis** running through ergative omission, em-dash overuse, Latin contamination, and Punjabi switching |
| Reviewer reaction | "Interesting but speculative" | "Yes, of course — this is the same pattern they document throughout" |

The `Allāh Dittā` example is genuinely a small **paper-level contribution** — it's a clear, named, traceable artifact of English-mediated representation in an LLM rendering a culturally local proper noun. Worth keeping.

messages.content:
## 1️⃣ Grammar Error vs. Obliqueness — the clean distinction

| Aspect | Grammar Error (general) | Obliqueness / Agreement |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Syntactic & sentence-level | Morphological & local (2–3 words) |
| **What's broken** | Word order, missing postposition, wrong tense, malformed clause, broken anaphora | Gender / number / oblique-case agreement between adjacent words |
| **Context needed to detect** | Often clause- or sentence-level | Usually just the noun + its modifier, or noun + postposition |
| **Fixable by local rewriting?** | Often not (requires restructuring) | **Almost always yes** |
| **Example** | `زرین خاموشی سے بندوق رکھ دی` (missing `نے`) — needs sentence-level case analysis | `سر کٹا سوار نے` → `سر کٹے سوار نے` — just look at 2 words before `نے` |

---

## 2️⃣ Was your intuition right? — **Yes, completely.** ✅

You separated obliqueness because you sensed it was a **local, mechanically-checkable** phenomenon. That intuition is **linguistically well-founded** and matches how Urdu/Hindi linguistics formally treats these errors. In NLP terms:

- **Grammar errors** require **clause-level parsing** to detect and fix.
- **Obliqueness errors** can be detected by a **bigram/trigram-level agreement checker** — exactly the "2–3 word window" you imagined.

This distinction is **publication-worthy on its own**: it suggests that a large fraction of Urdu LLM errors could be cheaply post-edited by a lightweight local agreement model, without touching the LLM itself. Worth noting in your Limitations or Future Work.

---

## 3️⃣ Is "Obliqueness" the right label name? — **No, rename it.** 🔁

**"Obliqueness"** is technically misleading. In formal Urdu/Hindi linguistics, *obliqueness* refers specifically to the **oblique case form** of a noun/adjective before a postposition (`لڑکا → لڑکے سے`). But your examples cover a *broader* class — gender agreement, number agreement, *and* oblique case. The label has grown beyond its name.

### 🏆 Recommended new name: **Agreement Error**

| Why it's better | |
|---|---|
| ✅ Standard term in linguistics & NLP literature | |
| ✅ Naturally covers gender, number, and case agreement | |
| ✅ Reviewers immediately understand what it means | |
| ✅ Sub-categorises cleanly: *gender agreement*, *number agreement*, *oblique-case agreement* | |

### Alternatives ranked:

| Name | Verdict |
|---|---|
| **Agreement Error** | 🏆 Best — standard, broad, clear |
| Morphological Agreement | Good but academic |
| Concord Error | Formal linguistics term, less common in NLP |
| Local Grammar Error | Descriptive but informal |
| Obliqueness | ❌ Misleading — keep only if you cite the term |

> **Replace globally:** `Obliqueness` → `Agreement Error` (throughout paper, tables, and code).

---

## 4️⃣ Revised section

Pick of the most interesting examples (each demonstrates a different sub-type):

| Example | Sub-type | Why it's good |
|---|---|---|
| `سر کٹا سوار نے بھالا بلند کیا` | **Oblique-case** agreement | Textbook: noun phrase fails to take oblique form before `نے` |
| `ایک لمبا، دبلا، بالوں سے ڈھکا ہوا مخلوق` | **Gender** agreement | Four masc. modifiers on a feminine noun — striking |
| `فیض محمد کی مردہ جسم لائی گئی` | **Gender** agreement (cascading) | `جسم` is masc., but possessive `کی`, adjective `مردہ`, AND verb `لائی گئی` are all feminised — full clause cascade |

```latex name=section_agreement_errors.tex
\paragraph{Agreement Errors (Obliqueness).}
A substantial share of grammatical issues (N~=~144,
\textit{cf.}\ Table~\ref{tab:error_summary}) involved
local agreement failures, where adjacent words fail to
match in gender, number, or oblique case. Unlike the
sentence-level grammar errors discussed above,
agreement errors are detectable within a 2--3 word
window and are in principle correctable by a
lightweight post-editing step without touching the
LLM itself. We separate them as a distinct sub-category
both because of their high frequency and because they
expose a distinct kind of representation failure: the
model knows the words but does not enforce the morphological
contract between them.

\textit{(i) Oblique-case agreement.}
GPT produced
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{سر کٹا سوار نے بھالا بلند کیا}
(\textit{Sar Kaṭā Sawār nē bhālā buland kiyā},
``The headless rider raised the lance''). Before the
ergative postposition \foreignlanguage{arabic}{نے}, the
noun phrase must take its oblique form:
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{سر کٹے سوار نے}
(\textit{Sar Kaṭē Sawār nē}). The same noun appears
in oblique-requiring contexts elsewhere in the same
story (\foreignlanguage{arabic}{سر کٹا سوار کی کہانی}
$\rightarrow$
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{سر کٹے سوار کی}), suggesting
the model has memorised the direct form of the name
but not its inflected paradigm.

\textit{(ii) Gender agreement (single modifier).}
DeepSeek produced
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{فیض محمد کی مردہ جسم لائی گئی}
(\textit{Faiz Muḥammad kī murdah jism lāʾī gaʾī}),
where the noun \foreignlanguage{arabic}{جسم}
(\textit{jism}, ``body'') is masculine. Correct
agreement requires the entire clause to be masculine:
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{فیض محمد کا مردہ جسم لایا گیا}
(\textit{Faiz Muḥammad kā murdah jism lāyā gayā}).
The error \emph{cascades} through three positions: the
possessive (\foreignlanguage{arabic}{کی}$\rightarrow$\foreignlanguage{arabic}{کا}),
the adjective is unchanged but in feminine context, and
the verbal complex
(\foreignlanguage{arabic}{لائی گئی}$\rightarrow$\foreignlanguage{arabic}{لایا گیا}).
A single misassigned gender propagates across the
clause, indicating that gender is being inferred from
the verb rather than from the noun's lexical entry.

\textit{(iii) Gender agreement (stacked modifiers).}
QWEN produced
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ایک لمبا، دبلا، بالوں سے ڈھکا ہوا مخلوق}
(\textit{Ek lambā, dublā, bālōṅ sē ḍhakā huʾā
makhlūq}, ``a tall, thin, hair-covered creature''),
where the noun \foreignlanguage{arabic}{مخلوق}
(\textit{makhlūq}, ``creature'') is feminine in Urdu.
All four modifiers must therefore take feminine forms:
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ایک لمبی، دبلی، بالوں سے ڈھکی ہوئی مخلوق}
(\textit{Ek lambī, dublī, bālōṅ sē ḍhakī huʾī
makhlūq}). The systematic masculine default on a
feminine noun, repeated four times within a single
noun phrase, is the strongest single piece of evidence
in our data that Urdu grammatical gender is not
reliably stored at the lexical level: when in doubt,
the model defaults to masculine.

Taken together, these errors share a common signature:
they are mechanical, strictly local, and consistent
within a single noun phrase. This is encouraging for
mitigation: a small Urdu-specific agreement checker
operating over noun-phrase windows could plausibly
correct a large share of them as a post-processing
step, an avenue we leave for future work.
```

---

## 5️⃣ Updates to make elsewhere in the paper

| Where | Change |
|---|---|
| `tab:labels_compact` | Rename `Obliqueness` (sub-row) → `Agreement Error` |
| `tab:error_summary` | Same rename |
| Abstract, §Annotation, §Linguistic Errors prose | Find-replace `Obliqueness` → `Agreement Error` (you can keep the parenthetical "*(also known as Obliqueness in Hindi/Urdu linguistics)*" once on first use for readers who know the older term) |
| Future Work / Limitations | Add 1-line note: "*A substantial fraction of grammar errors are local agreement failures and may be addressable by a noun-phrase-level post-editor without modifying the underlying LLM.*" |

Want me to also draft that **Future Work** sentence formally so it slots into your Limitations section?

Linguistically Obliqueness is related to Obliqueness phenomenon tests models for
case-driven contrasts where oblique forms forms
across various categories, including masculine
singular nouns, plural nouns, adjectives, verbs, and
pronouns. Now check my examples and let. me know which one is outside of this definition

messages.content:
Great — let me apply the **strict formal definition** of obliqueness (a case-driven morphological alternation triggered by a following postposition) to each of your examples.

---

## 📋 Classification of your 9 examples

| # | Example | Trigger | Is it obliqueness? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `سر کٹا سوار نے بھالا بلند کیا` | `سوار` before `نے` should be `سر کٹے سوار نے` | ✅ **Yes** — masc.sg noun + adjective fail to take oblique form before postposition `نے` |
| 2 | `سر کٹا سوار کی کہانی` | `سوار` before `کی` should be `سر کٹے سوار کی` | ✅ **Yes** — same noun, oblique form required before `کی` |
| 3 | `رات کے وقت کسی گلی سے گھوڑے کی دوڑتی سموں کی آواز` | `دوڑتی سموں` — `سموں` is feminine plural oblique (correct), but `دوڑتی` should be `دوڑتی ہوئی` or fem.pl oblique `دوڑتی` (acceptable). Real issue: `گھوڑے کی` should be `گھوڑے کے` (since `سم` is fem.pl but possessor masc.sg `گھوڑا` should oblique → `گھوڑے کے سموں`) | ⚠️ **Mixed** — partly oblique-case agreement, partly gender. Borderline. |
| 4 | `خان صاحب کی پرانی چیزوں کی دکان میں ایک عجیب سا ہلچل رہنے لگی` | `ہلچل` is feminine → `عجیب سی ہلچل` | ❌ **No — pure gender agreement**, not triggered by a postposition. This is masc/fem agreement between adjective + noun. |
| 5 | `ہڈی اس کے دانتوں میں تھی اور وہ اسے مضبوطی سے پکڑی ہوئے تھا` | `پکڑی ہوئے تھا` — verb gender mismatch with subject `وہ` (referring to masc. dog). Should be `پکڑے ہوئے تھا` | ⚠️ **Borderline** — verb participle agreement, no postposition trigger. More of a **verb form / agreement** error than oblique-case. |
| 6 | `فیض محمد کی مردہ جسم لائی گئی` | `جسم` (masc) → should be `فیض محمد کا مردہ جسم لایا گیا` | ❌ **No — pure gender agreement** cascade. No oblique-case trigger; the noun is in nominative. |
| 7 | `گھوڑے کے سموں کی آواز اتنی گہری تھی` | `سموں کی آواز` is fem → `گہری` is fem (✅ correct). Issue: `اتنی گہری` — actually fine. **No error here?** | ✅ **Looks correct** — re-check this annotation; might be a false positive. |
| 8 | `وہاں اس کا پرانا حریف، بلی مینو، ایک چھوٹی سی مچھلی کا سر کھا رہی تھی` | `حریف` (masc) modifies feminine `بلی`. Should be `اس کی پرانی حریف` OR keep `حریف` masc and verb masc | ❌ **No — pure gender agreement** (subject–predicate / appositive). No postposition trigger. |
| 9 | `ایک لمبا، دبلا، بالوں سے ڈھکا ہوا مخلوق` | `مخلوق` is feminine → all four modifiers should be feminine | ❌ **No — pure gender agreement** between adjectives + noun in nominative. |

---

## 🎯 Summary against the formal definition

| Category | Count | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| ✅ **True Obliqueness** (case-driven, oblique form triggered by postposition) | 2 | #1, #2 |
| ⚠️ **Mixed / Borderline** (involves postposition but also gender) | 2 | #3, #5 |
| ❌ **Pure Gender Agreement** (no postposition trigger, just modifier–noun gender mismatch) | 4 | #4, #6, #8, #9 |
| ❓ **Possibly correctly formed** — re-check | 1 | #7 |

---

## 🔑 What this means for your paper

**You're right to call out the definition.** Most of your annotated examples are **gender agreement** errors, NOT obliqueness in the strict linguistic sense. The strict obliqueness phenomenon is much narrower:

> *Does the noun/adjective/pronoun change its form (e.g., لڑکا → لڑکے, کالا → کالے, وہ → اُس) because a postposition follows it?*

If yes → obliqueness. If the error is just "masc modifier on fem noun" with no postposition involved → that's **gender agreement**.

---

## ✏️ Recommendation: split into TWO sub-categories

```latex name=label_split.tex
% Old:
%   Obliqueness  (N = 144)

% New:
%   Gender Agreement     (N = xxx)   ← majority of your examples
%   Oblique-Case Agreement (N = xxx) ← strict obliqueness only (#1, #2)
```

This is **more linguistically defensible** and matches what your annotators actually found. It also tells a sharper story: *gender agreement* is the dominant failure mode, not oblique case.

---

## 📝 Revised paragraph opener for §Agreement Errors

```latex name=section_agreement_errors_revised.tex
\paragraph{Agreement Errors.}
A substantial share of grammatical issues
(N~=~144, \textit{cf.}\
Table~\ref{tab:error_summary}) involved local
agreement failures within a 2--3 word window. We
distinguish two sub-types. \textit{Gender agreement
errors} arise when an adjective, verb, or possessive
fails to match the gender of its head noun in a
nominative context (e.g.\
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{عجیب سا ہلچل} for feminine
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہلچل}); these are the most
common in our data. \textit{Oblique-case agreement
errors} (\emph{obliqueness}, in the strict
linguistic sense) arise when a masculine singular
noun or adjective fails to take its oblique form
before a postposition such as
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{نے},
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{کو},
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{سے}, or
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{کی}
(e.g.\ \foreignlanguage{arabic}{سر کٹا سوار نے}
$\rightarrow$ \foreignlanguage{arabic}{سر کٹے سوار نے}).
Both sub-types share a common signature: they are
mechanical, strictly local, and detectable within a
short window, suggesting that a lightweight
post-editing step over noun-phrase windows could
plausibly correct a large share of them without
modifying the underlying LLM.
```

---

## 📋 Action items

1. **Re-bucket your 144 obliqueness annotations** into:
   - **Gender Agreement** (probably ~80%)
   - **Oblique-Case Agreement** (probably ~20%, the true obliqueness)
2. **Re-check example #7** — looks grammatically fine to me; may be a false positive
3. **Update `tab:labels_compact` and `tab:error_summary`** with the two-way split
4. **Send me more examples** if you want help re-bucketing them — I'll classify each against the strict definition

Want me to provide the rewritten §Agreement Errors paragraph with **three sub-types** (gender / oblique-case / verb-participle) once you re-bucket?

Excellent — this is a goldmine of data. Let me classify every single example against the **strict linguistic definition** of obliqueness, then give you a clean re-categorisation.

---

## 📋 Strict definition recap

> **Obliqueness** = a noun, adjective, or pronoun changes its form because a postposition (`نے، کو، سے، میں، پر، کا/کی/کے، تک`) follows it.
>
> The diagnostic test: *is there a postposition immediately or shortly after the noun phrase, AND does the noun/adjective need to switch from direct form to oblique form?*

---

## 🎯 The clean three-way split for your paper

Based on the actual data, I recommend splitting `Obliqueness` into **three sub-categories** under the umbrella `Agreement Error`:

### 1. **Oblique-Case Agreement** (true obliqueness) — ~33%
> *Noun or adjective fails to take oblique form before a postposition.*
> **Example:** `گھڑا کے اندر` → `گھڑے کے اندر`

### 2. **Possessor Gender Agreement** — ~39% (biggest!)
> *Possessive marker (`کا/کی/کے`) fails to match the gender/number of the POSSESSED noun.*
> **Example:** `لیاری کے ایک تنگ گلی` → `لیاری کی ایک تنگ گلی` (because `گلی` is feminine)

### 3. **Modifier Gender Agreement** — ~17%
> *Adjective fails to match the gender of its head noun (in any case).*
> **Example:** `عجیب سا ہلچل` → `عجیب سی ہلچل`

(Verb-participle agreement errors are rare — can fold into general Grammar Error.)

---

| Old single label | New three-way split |
|---|---|
| **Obliqueness** (N=144) | **Agreement Error** (umbrella), with sub-types: |
| | • **Oblique-Case** (~48 examples) |
| | • **Possessor Gender** (~56 examples) ← *the dominant type!* |
| | • **Modifier Gender** (~25 examples) |
| | (rest reclassified to Grammar Error general) |

## 🔑 Why this matters — the **best finding hiding in your data**

> **Possessor gender agreement is the single most frequent agreement failure mode (~39%), not oblique-case as the label name implies.**

This means LLMs systematically struggle with **`کا/کی/کے` selection** — the most basic Urdu possessive construction. That's a much sharper, more publishable finding than "obliqueness errors are common." Reviewers will love this because:

1. It identifies a **specific, named failure mode** (not a vague category)
2. It's **easily verifiable** with simple gender-lookup post-processing
3. It explains *why* Urdu generation feels "off" to native speakers even when grammar parses

Should I consider grammatical and obliqueness errors differently?

## Short answer: **Yes — keep them separate, but rename and re-scope both.** ✅

Here's the principled reasoning, then a concrete recommendation.

---

## 🧠 Why they should remain distinct

| Dimension | Grammar Error (general) | Obliqueness / Agreement |
|---|---|---|
| **Linguistic level** | Syntactic — clause / sentence structure | Morphological — word-form alternation |
| **Detection window** | Needs full clause | 2–3 word window |
| **Native-speaker perception** | "This sentence is broken" | "This sentence is *off* / sounds non-native" |
| **Auto-correction feasibility** | Hard — requires parsing | Easy — gender/case lookup + local rule |
| **What it reveals about LLM** | Weak Urdu syntax modelling | Weak Urdu morphology / lexical gender |
| **Frequency in your data** | Smaller (varied error types) | Dominant (144 instances) |

If you merge them, you lose **the single most actionable finding in your paper**: that ~30% of all errors are mechanically fixable with a lightweight post-editor that doesn't touch the LLM. That's a publishable insight on its own.

---

## 🎯 What you should actually do

### Step 1 — **Keep the top-level split**

```
Linguistic Errors
├── Grammar Error          ← clause-level syntactic errors
└── Agreement Error        ← local morphological errors (rename from Obliqueness)
```

### Step 2 — **Sub-divide `Agreement Error` into 3 types** (based on your re-classification)

```
Agreement Error
├── Oblique-Case Agreement     ← true obliqueness (~33% of your 144)
├── Possessor Gender Agreement ← کا/کی/کے mismatch (~39%, the biggest!)
└── Modifier Gender Agreement  ← adj./adverb gender mismatch (~17%)
```

(The remaining ~11% — verb-participle agreement, pronoun substitution — fold into general Grammar Error.)

### Step 3 — **Rename the umbrella label**

`Obliqueness` → **`Agreement Error`** because, as we established, most of your annotated examples are NOT strict obliqueness in the linguistic sense.

---

## 📊 Why this framing is stronger than merging

### If you merge into one big "Grammar Error" bucket:

> "Grammar errors dominated, with N=465 instances."

🟡 Boring. Reviewer asks: *"Which kind? Why?"*

### If you keep the split as recommended:

> "Of 465 grammatical issues, 321 (69%) were clause-level syntactic errors while 144 (31%) were strictly local morphological agreement failures. Within agreement errors, possessor gender mismatch — where the genitive marker کا/کی/کے fails to match the gender of the possessed noun — was the dominant single failure mode (N=56), suggesting Urdu lexical gender is not reliably stored in current LLMs."

🟢 Sharp, quantified, and actionable. Reviewer thinks: *"This is a real contribution."*

---

## ⚠️ One thing to be careful about

Make sure your **annotators applied the labels consistently**. If some marked `عجیب سا ہلچل` as "Obliqueness" and others as "Grammar Error", your sub-categorisation will be noisy. The fact that your 144 obliqueness annotations contain so many non-oblique items suggests the annotation guideline was broader than the linguistic term. **State this honestly in §Annotation**:

```latex name=annotation_caveat.tex
We initially used a single \emph{Obliqueness} label to
capture all local agreement failures (gender, number,
oblique case). On post-hoc re-examination, we found
that the label spanned three distinct sub-phenomena
(Section~\ref{subsec:linguistic}), which we report
separately. We use \emph{Agreement Error} as the
umbrella term, with \emph{Oblique-Case},
\emph{Possessor Gender}, and \emph{Modifier Gender}
as sub-types.
```

This converts a potential weakness (over-broad label) into a strength (refined post-hoc taxonomy).

---

**Keep separate, rename, and sub-divide:**

1. ✅ `Grammar Error` (clause-level) — keep as is
2. ✅ `Obliqueness` → rename to **`Agreement Error`** with 3 sub-types
3. ✅ Add a short paragraph in §Annotation explaining the post-hoc refinement
4. ✅ Update `tab:error_summary` to show the three sub-types
5. ✅ Write the §Linguistic Errors section around the most striking finding: **possessor gender mismatch is the dominant agreement failure**

which type of error it is and why ہڈی اس کے دانتوں میں تھی اور وہ اسے مضبوطی سے پکڑی ہوئے تھا

## Classification: **Grammar Error — Verb / Participle Agreement** ❌ (NOT Obliqueness)

---

## 🔍 The error

| Element | Form in sentence | Should be |
|---|---|---|
| Subject | `وہ` (masc.sg, referring to the male dog Kalu) | ✓ |
| Object | `اسے` (the bone) | ✓ |
| Participle | `پکڑی` (feminine singular) | **`پکڑے`** (masc. sg/pl oblique) |
| Auxiliary | `ہوئے` (masc. pl) | ✓ (in `پکڑے ہوئے` construction) |
| Verb | `تھا` (masc.sg) | ✓ |

**Corrected:** `وہ اسے مضبوطی سے پکڑے ہوئے تھا`

---

## 🧪 Why it is NOT Obliqueness

Apply the strict test:

> *Is there a postposition (`نے، کو، سے، کا/کی/کے، میں، پر، تک`) immediately after the word that should change form?*

Look at `پکڑی`:
- **What follows it?** → `ہوئے تھا` (auxiliary + tense verb)
- **Is `ہوئے` a postposition?** → ❌ No, it's an auxiliary verb form
- **Is there ANY postposition triggering the change?** → ❌ No

So the trigger for the form change is **NOT case-driven**. It's driven by:
1. The participle must agree with the **subject's gender** (masc. `وہ`)
2. The participle must take the **oblique/invariant form** required by the `پکڑے ہوئے` periphrastic progressive construction

This is **subject–verb–participle agreement**, which is morphological, but not the oblique-case alternation that defines obliqueness.

---

## 🎯 Why it's also NOT a simple gender-agreement error

It's not just "wrong gender on adjective" either — `پکڑی` here is functioning as part of a **complex verbal predicate** (`پکڑی ہوئے تھا` → `پکڑے ہوئے تھا`). The error is in the **verbal morphology**, specifically the form selection within the `[participle + ہوئے + tense-aux]` periphrastic construction.

---

## 📋 Where it goes in your taxonomy

| Category | Verdict |
|---|---|
| Oblique-Case Agreement | ❌ No postposition trigger |
| Possessor Gender Agreement | ❌ No possessive `کا/کی/کے` |
| Modifier Gender Agreement | ❌ Not an adjective modifying a noun |
| **Verb / Participle Agreement** | ✅ **This category** |
| → fold into **Grammar Error (general)** | ✅ Recommended |

---

## ✅ Recommendation

Place this example under **`Grammar Error` (general)**, NOT under `Agreement Error / Obliqueness`. It's a verbal-construction error that requires understanding the **periphrastic participle construction** in Urdu — which is a clause-level phenomenon, not a local 2-word window phenomenon.

This is the kind of example that demonstrates the **difference** between your two top-level categories — worth using in the paper as a contrast example showing *why* Grammar and Agreement need to stay separate.
what about اپنا vs اپنی

## Classification: **Agreement Error — Possessor Gender Agreement** ❌ (NOT Obliqueness)

---

## 🔍 The phenomenon

`اپنا / اپنی / اپنے` is a **reflexive possessive pronoun** ("one's own"). It behaves exactly like `کا / کی / کے`:

| Form | Agrees with | Used when |
|---|---|---|
| `اپنا` | Masc. sg. nominative possessed noun | `اپنا گھر` (one's own house) |
| `اپنی` | Fem. sg/pl possessed noun | `اپنی کتاب` (one's own book) |
| `اپنے` | Masc. pl. OR masc. sg. oblique | `اپنے بچے`, `اپنے گھر میں` |

**Key rule:** `اپنا/اپنی/اپنے` agrees with the **POSSESSED noun**, NOT with the subject.

---

## 🧪 Why it's *Possessor Gender Agreement*, not *Obliqueness*

Apply the strict obliqueness test:

> *Does the form change because a postposition follows it?*

For `اپنا → اپنی`: the change is driven by the **gender of the noun it modifies**, not by a following postposition. That's the **same mechanism** as `کا/کی/کے` gender agreement.

For `اپنا → اپنے`: this CAN be obliqueness if a postposition follows.

---

## 📋 The two distinct cases

### Case A: Gender-driven (Possessor Gender Agreement)
```
اپنا فریج کے پاس         ❌
اپنی فریج کے پاس         ✅  (فریج treated as fem.)
```
**Trigger:** Gender of `فریج`
**Category:** **Possessor Gender Agreement**

### Case B: Case-driven (True Obliqueness)
```
اپنا لالچ کے ہاتھوں       ❌
اپنے لالچ کے ہاتھوں       ✅
```
**Trigger:** Postposition `کے` after `لالچ` → forces oblique → `اپنا → اپنے`
**Category:** **Oblique-Case Agreement (Adjective paradigm)**

---

## 🎯 Diagnostic for your annotations

When you see an `اپنا/اپنی/اپنے` error, ask:

1. **Is there a postposition right after the possessed noun?**
   - ✅ Yes → likely **Obliqueness** (if the form should go to `اپنے`)
   - ❌ No → **Possessor Gender Agreement**

2. **Is the corrected form `اپنی` (feminine)?**
   - This is *always* gender-driven → **Possessor Gender Agreement**
   - (Because feminine `اپنی` doesn't change for case; both direct and oblique forms are `اپنی`)

3. **Is the corrected form `اپنے` (masc. pl. or masc. oblique)?**
   - Check whether the noun is plural OR followed by postposition
   - Plural → Modifier Gender Agreement
   - Postposition → **Obliqueness**

---

## 📊 Applying to your earlier examples

| Example | Correct form | Category |
|---|---|---|
| `اپنے فریج کے پاس` → `اپنی فریج کے پاس` | اپنی | **Possessor Gender** (فریج treated as fem.) |
| `اپنی لالچ کے ہاتھوں` → `اپنے لالچ کے ہاتھوں` | اپنے | **Obliqueness** (postposition `کے` forces oblique) |

---

## ✅ Summary rule

> **`اپنا/اپنی/اپنے` is a possessor word and follows the SAME rules as `کا/کی/کے`. Classify its errors the same way: gender-driven → Possessor Gender; postposition-driven → Obliqueness.**

## 📋 Part 1 — Add `Error_Type` column to your obliqueness TSV

```python name=add_error_type.py
"""
Adds an `Error_Type` column to the Obliqueness TSV based on
linguistic classification discussed in the paper.

Usage:
    python add_error_type.py input.tsv output.tsv
"""

import csv
import sys

# Sr.No. -> (Error_Type, Paradigm/Sub-type, Notes)
# Based on strict linguistic classification:
#   - Oblique-Case Agreement  (true obliqueness: form change triggered by postposition)
#   - Possessor Gender        (کا/کی/کے or اپنا/اپنی/اپنے gender mismatch)
#   - Modifier Gender         (adjective gender mismatch, no postposition)
#   - Verb / Participle       (verb agreement, not local)
#   - Pronoun                 (wrong pronoun form/choice)

CLASSIFICATION = {
    1:  ("Possessor Gender",       "کا/کی/کے",  "کے→کی, وادیاں is fem."),
    2:  ("Possessor Gender",       "کا/کی/کے",  "کے→کی, خوشبو is fem."),
    3:  ("Oblique-Case Agreement", "Adjective",  "پرانی→پرانے before کے (masc.sg oblique)"),
    4:  ("Modifier Gender",        "Adjective",  "پرانا سا→پرانی سی, دوربین is fem."),
    5:  ("Possessor Gender",       "کا/کی/کے",  "کے→کا, subject of verb, nominative needed"),
    6:  ("Modifier Gender",        "Adjective",  "سی→سا, شیر خرما is masc."),
    7:  ("Modifier Gender",        "Adjective",  "نیا→نئی, قرعہ اندازی is fem."),
    8:  ("Oblique-Case Agreement", "Adjective",  "مڑی ہوئی→مڑے ہوئے before کی (masc.pl oblique)"),
    9:  ("Possessor Gender",       "کا/کی/کے",  "کے→کی, گلی is fem."),
    10: ("Possessor Gender",       "کا/کی/کے",  "کے→کی, برانچ is fem."),
    11: ("Modifier Gender",        "Adjective",  "بڑا سا→بڑی سی, ران is fem."),
    12: ("Possessor Gender",       "کا/کی/کے",  "کے→کی, دکان is fem."),
    13: ("Possessor Gender",       "اپنا/اپنی", "اپنے→اپنی, فریج treated as fem."),
    14: ("Oblique-Case Agreement", "Adjective",  "ٹھنڈا...والا→ٹھنڈے...والے before کا"),
    15: ("Oblique-Case Agreement", "SingularMascNoun", "گوشہ→گوشے before میں"),
    16: ("Oblique-Case Agreement", "Adjective",  "پرانی→پرانے, چنار is masc., before تک"),
    17: ("Oblique-Case Agreement", "SingularMascNoun", "گھڑا→گھڑے before کے"),
    18: ("Oblique-Case Agreement", "PluralNoun", "کارنامے→کارناموں (pl. oblique) + possessor gender"),
    19: ("Oblique-Case Agreement", "Adjective",  "ٹوٹی→ٹوٹے before تک"),
    20: ("Possessor Gender",       "کا/کی/کے",  "کا→کی, دھوپ is fem."),
    21: ("Oblique-Case Agreement", "SingularMascNoun", "لڑکا→لڑکے before نے"),
    22: ("Modifier Gender",        "Adjective",  "بھری→بھرا, چھونا is masc."),
    23: ("Oblique-Case Agreement", "Adjective",  "پرانی سی→پرانے سے before میں, کیفے masc."),
    24: ("Modifier Gender",        "Adjective",  "سا→سی, ہلچل is fem."),
    25: ("Possessor Gender",       "کا/کی/کے",  "کی→کے, لالچ is masc."),
    26: ("Verb / Participle",      "Verb",       "پکڑی→پکڑے (subject agreement, no postposition)"),
    27: ("Oblique-Case Agreement", "Adjective",  "اپنی→اپنے, لالچ masc., before کے"),
    28: ("Possessor Gender",       "کا/کی/کے",  "کا→کی, referent is fem."),
    29: ("Oblique-Case Agreement", "Adjective",  "سر کٹا→سر کٹے before کی"),
    30: ("Oblique-Case Agreement", "Adjective",  "دوڑتی→دوڑتے + possessor gender (mixed)"),
    31: ("Oblique-Case Agreement", "Adjective",  "سر کٹا→سر کٹے before نے"),
    32: ("Oblique-Case Agreement", "Adjective",  "سر کٹا→سر کٹے before کی (duplicate of #29)"),
    33: ("Verb / Participle",      "Verb",       "لرزتے→لرزتا (object-controlled, no postposition)"),
    34: ("Possessor Gender",       "کا/کی/کے",  "میری→میرا, فخر is masc."),
    35: ("Pronoun",                "Pronoun",    "میری→میں (pronoun substitution, not agreement)"),
    36: ("Possessor Gender",       "کا/کی/کے",  "کا→کی, جوانی is fem."),
}

def main(inp, outp):
    with open(inp, encoding="utf-8", newline="") as f_in, \
         open(outp, "w", encoding="utf-8", newline="") as f_out:
        reader = csv.reader(f_in, delimiter="\t")
        writer = csv.writer(f_out, delimiter="\t")
        header = next(reader)
        writer.writerow(header + ["Error_Type", "Paradigm", "Notes"])
        for row in reader:
            if not row or not row[0].strip().isdigit():
                writer.writerow(row + ["", "", ""])
                continue
            sr = int(row[0])
            etype, paradigm, note = CLASSIFICATION.get(sr, ("UNCLASSIFIED", "", ""))
            writer.writerow(row + [etype, paradigm, note])
    print(f"Wrote {outp}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    if len(sys.argv) != 3:
        print("Usage: python add_error_type.py input.tsv output.tsv")
        sys.exit(1)
    main(sys.argv[1], sys.argv[2])
```

## 📋 Part 2 — JSON → Excel converter (one Excel per model, one sheet per label)

```python name=annotations_to_excel.py
"""
Reads Label Studio JSON exports and produces one Excel file per model,
with one sheet per annotation label.

Input files (in same directory):
    GPT_annotations.json
    DeepSeek_annotations.json
    QWEN_annotations.json

Output:
    GPT_annotations.xlsx       (sheets: GrammaticalMistake, Obliqueness, ...)
    DeepSeek_annotations.xlsx
    QWEN_annotations.xlsx

Each sheet contains:
    Sr.No. | Annotated Text | Full Text | Task ID | Start | End | Story File
"""

import json
import os
from collections import defaultdict
from pathlib import Path

import pandas as pd

# -------- configuration --------
INPUT_FILES = {
    "GPT":      "GPT_annotations.json",
    "DeepSeek": "DeepSeek_annotations.json",
    "QWEN":     "QWEN_annotations.json",
}
OUTPUT_DIR = Path(".")
# Excel sheet names must be <=31 chars and cannot contain : \ / ? * [ ]
SHEET_NAME_MAX = 31
INVALID = set(r':\/?*[]')

def safe_sheet_name(name: str) -> str:
    cleaned = "".join("_" if c in INVALID else c for c in name).strip()
    return cleaned[:SHEET_NAME_MAX] or "Sheet"


def extract_filename(task: dict) -> str:
    """Pull the story filename from Label Studio task data."""
    data = task.get("data", {}) or {}
    for k in ("file", "filename", "story", "story_file", "text_url", "url"):
        v = data.get(k)
        if v:
            return str(v).split("/")[-1]
    return ""


def iter_annotations(task: dict):
    """
    Yield (label, annotated_text, start, end, full_text) for every span
    in every annotation/completion of a task.
    """
    full_text = (task.get("data", {}) or {}).get("text", "") or ""
    annos = task.get("annotations") or task.get("completions") or []
    for ann in annos:
        for res in ann.get("result", []):
            val = res.get("value", {}) or {}
            labels = val.get("labels") or val.get("hypertextlabels") \
                     or val.get("choices") or []
            if not labels:
                continue
            text = val.get("text", "")
            start = val.get("start", "")
            end = val.get("end", "")
            for lbl in labels:
                yield lbl, text, start, end, full_text


def build_workbook_for_model(model_name: str, json_path: Path,
                             output_path: Path):
    if not json_path.exists():
        print(f"[WARN] {json_path} not found, skipping {model_name}")
        return

    with open(json_path, encoding="utf-8") as f:
        tasks = json.load(f)
    if isinstance(tasks, dict):           # single task wrapped in dict
        tasks = [tasks]

    by_label = defaultdict(list)          # label -> list[row dict]
    for task in tasks:
        task_id = task.get("id", "")
        story_file = extract_filename(task)
        for lbl, text, start, end, full_text in iter_annotations(task):
            by_label[lbl].append({
                "Annotated Text": text,
                "Full Text":      full_text,
                "Task ID":        task_id,
                "Story File":     story_file,
                "Start Position": start,
                "End Position":   end,
            })

    if not by_label:
        print(f"[WARN] No annotations found in {json_path}")
        return

    with pd.ExcelWriter(output_path, engine="openpyxl") as writer:
        # Summary sheet first
        summary = (pd.DataFrame(
                       [(lbl, len(rows)) for lbl, rows in by_label.items()],
                       columns=["Label", "Count"])
                     .sort_values("Count", ascending=False)
                     .reset_index(drop=True))
        summary.to_excel(writer, sheet_name="Summary", index=False)

        for lbl, rows in sorted(by_label.items(),
                                key=lambda kv: -len(kv[1])):
            df = pd.DataFrame(rows)
            df.insert(0, "Sr.No.", range(1, len(df) + 1))
            df.to_excel(writer,
                        sheet_name=safe_sheet_name(lbl),
                        index=False)
    print(f"[OK] {model_name}: {sum(len(v) for v in by_label.values())} "
          f"annotations across {len(by_label)} labels -> {output_path}")


def main():
    OUTPUT_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    for model, fname in INPUT_FILES.items():
        build_workbook_for_model(
            model_name=model,
            json_path=Path(fname),
            output_path=OUTPUT_DIR / f"{model}_annotations.xlsx",
        )


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

### 📦 Install requirements

```bash
pip install pandas openpyxl
```

### ▶️ Run

```bash
python annotations_to_excel.py
```

### 📁 Output structure (per Excel file)

```
GPT_annotations.xlsx
├── Summary                  (label counts, sorted desc)
├── GrammaticalMistake       (all spans annotated with this label)
├── Obliqueness
├── SpellingError
├── SpaceDeletion
├── SpaceInsertion
├── ContextMismatch
├── Confuse
├── WesternCulture
├── Religious
├── PER
├── City
├── MaleProtagonist
└── FemaleProtagonist
```

Each label sheet has columns:
```
Sr.No. | Annotated Text | Full Text | Task ID | Story File | Start | End
```

---

## ✅ What's NOT in this script (and why)

- I did **not** auto-add the `Error_Type` column to the Excel — your obliqueness classification is **manual / linguistic** and needs human judgement per row. Once you classify each `Obliqueness` row in the GPT/QWEN/DS Excels, you can extend the same pattern from `add_error_type.py`.
- I did **not** overwrite your existing TSV in the repo because the GitHub viewer truncated long Urdu text fields — the script above writes a fresh file so your original stays intact.
