unit. Across our 93-story corpus
(\textit{approx.}~XX{,}XXX Urdu words) we identified
57 such errors: GPT~31, DeepSeek~22, QWEN~4.
Normalised by output length, the rates are
GPT~$\approx$~1.8 per 1{,}000 words, DeepSeek~$\approx$~1.0
per 1{,}000 words, and QWEN~$\approx$~0.25 per
1{,}000 words --- collectively \textit{below 0.2\% of
all tokens}. The single largest concentration appears
in DeepSeek's \textit{DS\_Story\_18} (12~deletions in
one story), followed by GPT's \textit{GPT\_Story\_18}
(6~deletions).

\textit{Compound verbs dominate the error inventory.}
Across all three models, the most frequent deletions
involve light-verb constructions where a participle
or adverb fuses with an auxiliary verb:
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہو گیا} (\textit{ho gayā}),
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہو گا} (\textit{ho gā}),
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{آ گیا} (\textit{ā gayā}),
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{آ رہی} (\textit{ā rahī}),
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{کر کے} (\textit{kar ke}).
In GPT, \foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہوگیا} alone
accounts for 7 of 31 errors (23\%); together with
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہوگا},
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہوگئی},
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{آگئی}, and
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{آرہا/آرہی}, the family of
\textsc{ہو-} and \textsc{آ-}auxiliary fusions covers
over 70\% of the model's space-deletion errors.
DeepSeek (\foreignlanguage{arabic}{آگیا},
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{آرہی},
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{آکر},
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{آگئے},
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہوگا}) and QWEN
(\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہوگا},
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہوگی},
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{مرجاتا}) show the same
profile in miniature. The convergence across
architectures strongly suggests that Urdu light-verb
constructions are being represented as single
sub-word tokens during training, and the fused form
subsequently leaks into generation.

\textit{Low surface salience, high downstream impact.}
At well under 0.2\% of total tokens, space-deletion
errors are unlikely to disrupt a human reader's
comprehension; a native speaker scanning
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہوگیا} typically restores
the intended segmentation
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہو گیا} without conscious
effort. The risk these errors pose is therefore not
to readability but to \emph{downstream consumers of
the text}. First, virtually all standard Urdu NLP
pipelines---POS taggers, dependency parsers, named
entity recognisers, search indices, and BM25 / TF-IDF
retrievers---depend on whitespace as their primary
tokenisation cue, and a fused
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہوگیا} is a different token
from \foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہو گیا}, breaking
lookup and degrading parser accuracy without any
visible signal in the text. Second, and more
seriously, LLM-generated text is increasingly being
used as \emph{synthetic training data} for Urdu and
other low-resource languages where naturally
occurring corpora are scarce. If such fused forms
are silently absorbed into the training set of a
next-generation model, the error compounds: the
fused token gains additional statistical support,
the segmented token loses relative frequency, and
the orthographic distinction may eventually collapse
in the model's internal representation. What appears
as a minor cosmetic slip in a single story thus has
the potential to become a self-reinforcing
contamination of the language's digital record. The
errors are also trivially correctable by a small
lookup table of attested light-verb pairs, making
this a strong candidate for cheap post-processing
before LLM output is reused for any training or
indexing pipeline.

section_space_deletion.tex.name: section_space_deletion.tex
section_space_deletion.tex.value:
\paragraph{Space Deletion.}
Space-deletion errors arise when two adjacent Urdu
tokens that should be written with an intervening
space are concatenated into a single orthographic
unit. Across our 93-story corpus
(152{,}137 Urdu words in total) we identified 57
such errors: GPT~31, DeepSeek~22, QWEN~4.
Normalised by output length, the rates are
GPT~0.63, DeepSeek~0.37, and QWEN~0.09 errors per
1{,}000 words --- collectively only 0.037\% of all
tokens generated. The single largest concentration
appears in DeepSeek's \textit{DS\_Story\_18}
(12~deletions in one story), followed by GPT's
\textit{GPT\_Story\_18} (6~deletions). The
$\approx$7$\times$ spread between GPT and QWEN
indicates that this is not a uniform property of
LLMs generating Urdu but a model-specific
tokenisation artefact.

\textit{Compound verbs dominate the error inventory.}
Across all three models, the most frequent
deletions involve light-verb constructions where a
participle or adverb fuses with an auxiliary verb:
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہو گیا} (\textit{ho gayā}),
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہو گا} (\textit{ho gā}),
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{آ گیا} (\textit{ā gayā}),
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{آ رہی} (\textit{ā rahī}),
and \foreignlanguage{arabic}{کر کے} (\textit{kar ke}).
In GPT, \foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہوگیا} alone
accounts for 7 of 31 errors (23\%); together with
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہوگا},
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہوگئی},
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{آگئی}, and
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{آرہا/آرہی}, the family of
\textsc{ہو-} and \textsc{آ-}auxiliary fusions
covers over 70\% of the model's space-deletion
errors. DeepSeek
(\foreignlanguage{arabic}{آگیا},
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{آرہی},
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{آکر},
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{آگئے},
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہوگا}) and QWEN
(\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہوگا},
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہوگی},
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{مرجاتا}) show the same
profile in miniature. The convergence across
architectures strongly suggests that Urdu
light-verb constructions are being represented as
single sub-word tokens during training, and the
fused form subsequently leaks into generation.

\textit{Low surface salience, high downstream
impact.} At 0.037\% of total tokens, space-deletion
errors are unlikely to disrupt a human reader's
comprehension; a native speaker scanning
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہوگیا} typically restores
the intended segmentation
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہو گیا} without conscious
effort. The risk these errors pose is therefore not
to readability but to \emph{downstream consumers of
the text}. First, virtually all standard Urdu NLP
pipelines---POS taggers, dependency parsers, named
entity recognisers, search indices, and BM25 /
TF-IDF retrievers---depend on whitespace as their
primary tokenisation cue, and a fused
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہوگیا} is a different
token from \foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہو گیا},
breaking lookup and silently degrading parser
accuracy. Second, and more seriously, LLM-generated
text is increasingly being used as \emph{synthetic
training data} for Urdu and other low-resource
languages where naturally occurring corpora are
scarce. If such fused forms are absorbed into the
training set of a next-generation model, the error
compounds: the fused token gains additional
statistical support, the correctly segmented form
loses relative frequency, and the orthographic
distinction may eventually collapse in the model's
internal representation. What appears as a minor
cosmetic slip in a single story thus has the
potential to become a self-reinforcing
contamination of the language's digital record.
Encouragingly, the errors are concentrated in a
small, closed set of light-verb pairs and are
therefore trivially correctable by a lookup-based
post-processing step before LLM output is reused
for any training or indexing pipeline.

section_space_deletion.tex.name: section_space_deletion.tex
section_space_deletion.tex.value:
\paragraph{Space Deletion.}
Space-deletion errors --- where two adjacent Urdu
tokens are concatenated into a single orthographic
unit --- account for only 57 instances across our
152{,}137-word corpus (0.037\% of tokens), but show
a $\approx$7$\times$ spread between models: GPT~0.63,
DeepSeek~0.37, and QWEN~0.09 per~1{,}000~words.
Over 70\% involve light-verb fusions with the
auxiliaries \foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہونا} and
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{آنا} (e.g.\
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہوگیا},
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہوگا},
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{آگیا},
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{آرہی}), with
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہوگیا} alone responsible
for 23\% of GPT's cases. The convergence across
architectures on the same closed set of compound
verbs points to a tokenisation artefact rather than
a generation slip. Although such fusions are largely
invisible to human readers, they are not benign for
downstream use: whitespace-based Urdu NLP pipelines
(POS taggers, parsers, retrievers) treat
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہوگیا} and
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہو گیا} as different
tokens, and if LLM output is reused as synthetic
training data --- a common practice for
low-resource languages --- these fused forms gain
additional statistical support and risk
self-reinforcing contamination of the language's
digital record. The errors are confined to a small,
closed inventory and are therefore easily corrected
by a lookup-based post-processing step.

section_space_deletion.tex.name: section_space_deletion.tex
section_space_deletion.tex.value:
\paragraph{Space Deletion.}
Space-deletion errors --- where two adjacent Urdu
tokens are concatenated into a single orthographic
unit --- account for 57 instances across our
152{,}137-word corpus (0.037\% of tokens). Although
the absolute rate is low, comparing each fused form
against its correctly-spaced counterpart reveals a
sharp \emph{model-specific orthographic signature}
rather than random noise. GPT fuses
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہو-}auxiliary constructions
at substantial rates
(\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہوگیا} 41\%,
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہوجاتا} 40\%,
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہوگا} 67\%) while DeepSeek
and QWEN produce these forms with the correct space
in essentially 100\% of cases (e.g.\ DeepSeek
writes \foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہو گیا} 27/27
times, QWEN 14/14). The picture inverts for
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{آ-}auxiliary fusions:
DeepSeek fuses \foreignlanguage{arabic}{آگیا} 44\%
of the time and \foreignlanguage{arabic}{آگئے}
67\%, where GPT and QWEN remain near zero. QWEN
shows almost no fusion of either family. Because
each model handles \emph{a different} closed
inventory of compounds poorly while writing the
rest correctly, the errors are not consistent with
a single shared tokenisation merge of these
sequences; they instead reflect sub-word vocabulary
choices specific to each model's tokeniser, where
certain auxiliary-verb combinations have been
absorbed as single high-frequency units. Although
largely invisible to human readers, this matters
downstream: whitespace-based Urdu NLP pipelines
treat \foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہوگیا} and
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہو گیا} as different
tokens, and if LLM output is reused as synthetic
training data --- a common practice for
low-resource languages --- a model's own fusion
preferences gain additional statistical support and
risk self-reinforcing contamination of the
language's digital record. The errors are confined
to a small, model-identifiable inventory and are
therefore easily corrected by a lookup-based
post-processing step.

word_count_rates.py.name: word_count_rates.py
word_count_rates.py.value:
"""
Compute total words per model and the space-deletion rate per 1,000 words.

Usage:
    python word_count_rates.py \
        --models GPT=stories/GPT DeepSeek=stories/DeepSeek QWEN=stories/QWEN \
        --errors GPT=31 DeepSeek=22 QWEN=4
"""

import argparse
import re
from pathlib import Path

# Urdu word = run of Urdu/Arabic letters (digits & punctuation excluded)
URDU_WORD = re.compile(
    r"[\u0600-\u06FF\u0750-\u077F\uFB50-\uFDFF\uFE70-\uFEFF]+"
)

def count_words(folder: Path) -> tuple[int, int]:
    total_words, n_files = 0, 0
    for fp in sorted(folder.glob("*.txt")):
        text = fp.read_text(encoding="utf-8", errors="replace")
        total_words += len(URDU_WORD.findall(text))
        n_files += 1
    return total_words, n_files

def parse_kv(items):
    return {x.split("=", 1)[0]: x.split("=", 1)[1] for x in items}

def main():
    ap = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    ap.add_argument("--models", required=True, nargs="+")
    ap.add_argument("--errors", required=True, nargs="+",
                    help="NAME=COUNT, e.g. GPT=31")
    args = ap.parse_args()

    model_dirs = {k: Path(v) for k, v in parse_kv(args.models).items()}
    errors     = {k: int(v)  for k, v in parse_kv(args.errors).items()}

    print(f"{'Model':<10}{'Stories':>9}{'Words':>10}"
          f"{'Avg/story':>11}{'Errors':>9}{'Per1k':>9}{'% of words':>12}")
    print("-" * 72)
    total_w, total_e = 0, 0
    for m, folder in model_dirs.items():
        words, n = count_words(folder)
        e = errors.get(m, 0)
        per1k = (e / words * 1000) if words else 0
        pct   = (e / words * 100) if words else 0
        total_w += words
        total_e += e
        print(f"{m:<10}{n:>9}{words:>10}{words/n:>11.0f}"
              f"{e:>9}{per1k:>9.2f}{pct:>11.3f}%")
    print("-" * 72)
    print(f"{'ALL':<10}{'':>9}{total_w:>10}{'':>11}"
          f"{total_e:>9}{total_e/total_w*1000:>9.2f}"
          f"{total_e/total_w*100:>11.3f}%")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

space_deletion_consistency.py.name: space_deletion_consistency.py
space_deletion_consistency.py.value:
"""
For each space-deletion example, measure how OFTEN the model fuses the pair
vs. writes it with the correct space.

For an example like "ہوگیا" (the fused/incorrect form), we also search for
its correctly-spaced counterpart "ہو گیا" and compute:

    fusion_rate = fused_count / (fused_count + spaced_count)

Interpretation:
    1.00 -> model ALWAYS fuses these two words (systematic)
    0.50 -> model is inconsistent
    0.00 -> model never fuses (the error is rare/accidental)

Input:
    space_deletion_examples.txt  -- lines of either:
        ہوگیا                       (script auto-splits at the boundary you specify)
        ہوگیا = ہو گیا              (explicit fused = spaced mapping; preferred)

Usage:
    python space_deletion_consistency.py \
        --examples space_deletion_examples.txt \
        --models GPT=stories/GPT DeepSeek=stories/DeepSeek QWEN=stories/QWEN \
        --out space_deletion_consistency.xlsx
"""

import argparse
import re
from collections import defaultdict
from pathlib import Path

import pandas as pd

URDU_LETTER = r"[\u0600-\u06FF\u0750-\u077F\uFB50-\uFDFF\uFE70-\uFEFF]"


# ---------- Examples file loader ----------
def load_pairs(path: Path) -> list[tuple[str, str]]:
    """
    Each line is either:
        fused                       (just the joined form; spaced form must be
                                     supplied manually -- script will skip these
                                     with a warning)
        fused = spaced              (explicit mapping, recommended)
    Returns list of (fused, spaced) tuples.
    """
    pairs = []
    for raw in path.read_text(encoding="utf-8").splitlines():
        s = raw.strip()
        if not s or s.startswith("#"):
            continue
        if "=" in s:
            fused, spaced = [p.strip() for p in s.split("=", 1)]
            pairs.append((fused, spaced))
        else:
            print(f"[WARN] No spaced form given for {s!r}; skipping. "
                  f"Use 'fused = spaced' syntax.")
    # de-dup, preserve order
    seen, uniq = set(), []
    for p in pairs:
        if p not in seen:
            seen.add(p)
            uniq.append(p)
    return uniq


# ---------- Pattern compilation ----------
def boundary_pattern(token: str) -> re.Pattern:
    """Match `token` only when not flanked by Urdu letters on either side."""
    return re.compile(
        rf"(?<!{URDU_LETTER}){re.escape(token)}(?!{URDU_LETTER})"
    )


# ---------- Scanning ----------
def scan_model(folder: Path, pairs: list[tuple[str, str]]):
    """Return per-pair {fused_count, spaced_count, stories_with_fused} dict."""
    patterns = [(f, s, boundary_pattern(f), boundary_pattern(s))
                for f, s in pairs]

    stats = {f: {"fused": 0, "spaced": 0,
                 "stories_with_fused": 0, "stories_with_spaced": 0}
             for f, _ in pairs}

    files = sorted(folder.glob("*.txt"))
    for fp in files:
        text = fp.read_text(encoding="utf-8", errors="replace")
        for f, s, rxf, rxs in patterns:
            fc = len(rxf.findall(text))
            sc = len(rxs.findall(text))
            stats[f]["fused"] += fc
            stats[f]["spaced"] += sc
            if fc > 0:
                stats[f]["stories_with_fused"] += 1
            if sc > 0:
                stats[f]["stories_with_spaced"] += 1

    return stats, len(files)


# ---------- Reporting ----------
def build_dataframe(model: str, stats: dict, n_stories: int,
                    pairs: list[tuple[str, str]]) -> pd.DataFrame:
    rows = []
    spaced_lookup = dict(pairs)
    for fused, s in stats.items():
        total = s["fused"] + s["spaced"]
        rate = (s["fused"] / total) if total else 0.0
        if s["fused"] > 0:
            if rate >= 0.80:
                verdict = "Systematic (always fuses)"
            elif rate >= 0.40:
                verdict = "Inconsistent"
            elif rate > 0:
                verdict = "Rare/accidental"
            else:
                verdict = "Never fuses"
        else:
            verdict = "Never fuses"
        rows.append({
            "Fused form":       fused,
            "Spaced form":      spaced_lookup[fused],
            "Fused count":      s["fused"],
            "Spaced count":     s["spaced"],
            "Total occurrences": total,
            "Fusion rate":      round(rate, 3),
            "Stories w/ fused": s["stories_with_fused"],
            "Stories w/ spaced": s["stories_with_spaced"],
            "Stories scanned":  n_stories,
            "Verdict":          verdict,
        })
    df = pd.DataFrame(rows).sort_values(
        ["Fusion rate", "Fused count"], ascending=False
    ).reset_index(drop=True)
    return df


def parse_kv(items):
    return {x.split("=", 1)[0]: x.split("=", 1)[1] for x in items}


def main():
    ap = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    ap.add_argument("--examples", required=True, type=Path)
    ap.add_argument("--models", required=True, nargs="+")
    ap.add_argument("--out", default="space_deletion_consistency.xlsx",
                    type=Path)
    args = ap.parse_args()

    pairs = load_pairs(args.examples)
    if not pairs:
        raise SystemExit("No (fused = spaced) pairs loaded; nothing to do.")
    print(f"Loaded {len(pairs)} fused/spaced pairs.\n")

    model_dirs = {k: Path(v) for k, v in parse_kv(args.models).items()}

    # Cross-model headline: per-pair fusion rate side by side
    headline_rows = {f: {"Fused form": f, "Spaced form": s}
                     for f, s in pairs}

    with pd.ExcelWriter(args.out, engine="openpyxl") as writer:
        for model, folder in model_dirs.items():
            stats, n = scan_model(folder, pairs)
            df = build_dataframe(model, stats, n, pairs)
            df.to_excel(writer, sheet_name=model, index=False)

            print(f"=== {model} ({n} stories) ===")
            print(df.to_string(index=False))
            print()

            for f, s in pairs:
                headline_rows[f][f"{model} fused"]   = stats[f]["fused"]
                headline_rows[f][f"{model} spaced"]  = stats[f]["spaced"]
                tot = stats[f]["fused"] + stats[f]["spaced"]
                headline_rows[f][f"{model} rate"] = (
                    round(stats[f]["fused"]/tot, 3) if tot else 0.0
                )

        headline = pd.DataFrame(list(headline_rows.values()))
        headline.to_excel(writer, sheet_name="Cross-Model Comparison",
                          index=False)

    print(f"[OK] Wrote {args.out}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

"""

as a percentage of total sentences in that model's stories.

Inputs
------
./stories/manually_verified_annotated_errors/GPT_annotations_verified.xlsx
./stories/manually_verified_annotated_errors/DeepSeek_annotations_verified.xlsx
./stories/manually_verified_annotated_errors/QWEN_annotations_verified.xlsx

    Each workbook has one sheet per label (plus a Summary sheet which is
    ignored). Every row in a label sheet = one verified annotation of
    that label.

./stories/GPT/*.txt          (raw story files used to count sentences)
./stories/DeepSeek/*.txt
./stories/QWEN/*.txt

Outputs
-------
./stories/manually_verified_annotated_errors/label_stats_summary.xlsx
    Sheets:
      - Counts        (rows = labels, cols = models, values = raw counts)
      - Per100Sent    (rows = labels, cols = models, values = % of sentences)
      - SentenceTotals
      - LongFormat    (one row per model x label, for plotting)

Usage
-----
    python verified_label_stats.py
"""

import re
from pathlib import Path

import pandas as pd

# ---------- configuration ----------
VERIFIED_DIR = Path("./stories/manually_verified_annotated_errors")
STORY_DIRS = {
    "GPT":      Path("./stories/GPT"),
    "DeepSeek": Path("./stories/DeepSeek"),
    "QWEN":     Path("./stories/QWEN"),
}
VERIFIED_FILES = {
    "GPT":      VERIFIED_DIR / "GPT_annotations_verified.xlsx",
    "DeepSeek": VERIFIED_DIR / "DeepSeek_annotations_verified.xlsx",
    "QWEN":     VERIFIED_DIR / "QWEN_annotations_verified.xlsx",
}
OUT_FILE = VERIFIED_DIR / "label_stats_summary.xlsx"

# Sheets in the verified workbooks that are NOT labels
NON_LABEL_SHEETS = {"Summary", "Cover", "Index", "Notes"}

# ---------- sentence splitter ----------
# Urdu sentence terminators: ۔ ؟ ! and ASCII . ? !
# Split on these followed by whitespace or end of line.
SENT_SPLIT = re.compile(r"[۔؟!\.\?!]+\s*")

def count_sentences(folder: Path) -> int:
    total = 0
    for fp in sorted(folder.glob("*.txt")):
        text = fp.read_text(encoding="utf-8", errors="replace")
        # remove empty fragments
        sents = [s for s in SENT_SPLIT.split(text) if s.strip()]
        total += len(sents)
    return total


# ---------- annotation counting ----------
def count_labels_per_workbook(path: Path) -> dict[str, int]:
    """Return {label: row_count} for every label sheet in the workbook."""
    if not path.exists():
        print(f"[WARN] {path} not found")
        return {}
    xl = pd.ExcelFile(path)
    counts = {}
    for sheet in xl.sheet_names:
        if sheet in NON_LABEL_SHEETS:
            continue
        df = pd.read_excel(xl, sheet_name=sheet)
        # drop fully-empty rows just in case
        df = df.dropna(how="all")
        counts[sheet] = len(df)
    return counts


# ---------- main ----------
def main():
    # 1. Sentence totals per model
    sent_totals = {}
    for model, folder in STORY_DIRS.items():
        if not folder.exists():
            print(f"[WARN] story folder missing: {folder}")
            sent_totals[model] = 0
            continue
        sent_totals[model] = count_sentences(folder)
    print("Sentence totals:", sent_totals)

    # 2. Label counts per model
    counts_per_model = {}
    for model, path in VERIFIED_FILES.items():
        counts_per_model[model] = count_labels_per_workbook(path)

    # 3. Build wide counts table (labels x models)
    all_labels = sorted({lbl for d in counts_per_model.values() for lbl in d})
    counts_df = pd.DataFrame(
        {model: [counts_per_model[model].get(lbl, 0) for lbl in all_labels]
         for model in VERIFIED_FILES},
        index=all_labels,
    )
    counts_df.index.name = "Label"
    counts_df["Total"] = counts_df.sum(axis=1)
    counts_df = counts_df.sort_values("Total", ascending=False)

    # 4. Percentage of sentences (errors per 100 sentences)
    pct_df = counts_df.drop(columns=["Total"]).copy()
    for model in VERIFIED_FILES:
        n_sents = sent_totals.get(model, 0) or 1   # avoid div/0
        pct_df[model] = (pct_df[model] / n_sents * 100).round(2)

    # 5. Long-format for plotting / further analysis
    long_rows = []
    for model in VERIFIED_FILES:
        n_sents = sent_totals.get(model, 0)
        for lbl in all_labels:
            cnt = counts_per_model[model].get(lbl, 0)
            long_rows.append({
                "Model":          model,
                "Label":          lbl,
                "Count":          cnt,
                "Sentences":      n_sents,
                "PctOfSentences": round((cnt / n_sents * 100), 2)
                                  if n_sents else 0.0,
            })
    long_df = pd.DataFrame(long_rows)

    sent_df = pd.DataFrame(
        [{"Model": m, "Total Sentences": s} for m, s in sent_totals.items()]
    )

    # 6. Write Excel
    VERIFIED_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    with pd.ExcelWriter(OUT_FILE, engine="openpyxl") as w:
        counts_df.to_excel(w, sheet_name="Counts")
        pct_df.to_excel(w,    sheet_name="PctOfSentences")
        sent_df.to_excel(w,   sheet_name="SentenceTotals", index=False)
        long_df.to_excel(w,   sheet_name="LongFormat",     index=False)

    # 7. Console print
    print("\n=== Raw counts (verified) ===")
    print(counts_df.to_string())
    print("\n=== % of sentences containing this error type ===")
    print(pct_df.to_string())
    print(f"\n[OK] Wrote {OUT_FILE}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

section_linguistic_errors.tex.name: section_linguistic_errors.tex
section_linguistic_errors.tex.value:
\subsection{Linguistic Errors}
\label{subsec:linguistic_errors}

To keep the comparison fair across models of unequal
verbosity, we report all rates as the percentage of
sentences in which at least one error of the given
type was annotated. Denominators are 3{,}835 sentences
for GPT, 4{,}720 for DeepSeek, and 3{,}319 for QWEN
(11{,}874 sentences in total across 93 stories).

\paragraph{Grammatical Mistake.}
Sentence-level grammatical errors, including
ergative-marker omission, tense-aspect mismatches,
and word-order violations, are the most pervasive
class of linguistic errors in our corpus. They occur
in 1.88\% of GPT sentences, 2.25\% of DeepSeek
sentences, and 4.16\% of QWEN sentences. QWEN's
rate is roughly 2.2 times GPT's and almost twice
DeepSeek's, making it the weakest of the three on
clause-level Urdu syntax. The ranking is
GPT~$<$~DeepSeek~$<$~QWEN, and it holds for every
sub-type we examined (Section~\ref{subsec:grammar_subtypes}),
suggesting that QWEN's underlying weakness is in
syntactic planning rather than in any single
construction.

\paragraph{Obliqueness (Agreement Errors).}
Local morphological agreement errors, where a noun,
adjective, pronoun or verb fails to take the form
required by a neighbouring postposition or
controller, occur in 1.07\% of GPT sentences,
0.83\% of DeepSeek sentences, and 2.77\% of QWEN
sentences. The cross-model spread is sharper than
for any other linguistic category: QWEN's rate is
roughly 3.3 times DeepSeek's and 2.6 times GPT's.
The most frequent sub-types are possessor-gender
mismatch on \foreignlanguage{arabic}{کا/کی/کے}
(\textit{kā/kī/ke}, ``of'') and case-driven
adjective oblique forms such as
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{بڑا بھائی}
(\textit{baṛā bhā'ī}, ``elder brother'') versus the
expected oblique \foreignlanguage{arabic}{بڑے بھائی}
(\textit{baṛe bhā'ī}, ``elder brother [obl.]'').
Because these errors are entirely local and
mechanically describable, they are recoverable by
a lightweight rule-based post-editor; the more
interesting observation is that QWEN consistently
fails the same local agreement rules that the other
two models largely respect, indicating that Urdu
lexical gender and case morphology are not reliably
encoded in QWEN's representations.

\paragraph{Semantic Anomaly.}
Semantic anomalies, where a sentence is locally
well-formed but globally incoherent (impossible
events, contradictory attributes, or referents
without antecedents), occur in 0.91\% of GPT
sentences, 1.57\% of DeepSeek sentences, and 0.75\%
of QWEN sentences. The ranking inverts relative to
the previous two categories: DeepSeek is now the
weakest, producing semantic anomalies almost twice
as often as GPT and over twice as often as QWEN.
The contrast with QWEN's profile is informative.
QWEN tops the table for sentence-level grammar and
local agreement yet has the lowest semantic-anomaly
rate, suggesting that its outputs are semantically
plausible but morphologically careless. DeepSeek
shows the opposite trade-off: cleaner morphology
but weaker global coherence.

\paragraph{Context Mismatch.}
Context-mismatch errors, where a sentence contradicts
earlier story content (a character's name, age, or
location changes mid-narrative), are the rarest of
the four categories: 0.21\% for GPT, 0.44\% for
DeepSeek, and 0.63\% for QWEN. Although the
absolute rates are low, the QWEN-worst ordering
seen in grammar and obliqueness reappears here at
roughly 3 times the GPT rate, indicating that
long-range narrative consistency is a further
weakness of QWEN in addition to its morphological
ones. We treat this category as a low-prevalence
but high-impact phenomenon: a single
context-mismatch in a short story can disrupt the
reader's mental model of the narrative more
seriously than several local agreement slips.

\paragraph{Summary.}
Two complementary profiles emerge from these four
categories. GPT is the most balanced, with the
lowest rates in three of the four categories.
QWEN dominates the surface-level error types
(grammar 4.16\%, obliqueness 2.77\%, context
mismatch 0.63\%) but performs best on semantic
plausibility. DeepSeek sits between the two on
morphology and grammar but is the weakest on
semantic coherence (1.57\%). These divergent
profiles suggest that the three models are
optimising different aspects of Urdu generation,
and that no single model is uniformly best across
linguistic levels.

section_context_mismatch.tex.name: section_context_mismatch.tex
section_context_mismatch.tex.value:
\paragraph{Context Mismatch.}
Context-mismatch errors arise when a generated
sentence is locally well-formed but conflicts with
earlier narrative content, a real-world fact, or a
culturally established script. They occur in
0.21\% of GPT sentences, 0.44\% of DeepSeek
sentences, and 0.63\% of QWEN sentences. The
absolute rates are low, but the cross-model
ordering matches the QWEN-weakest pattern observed
for grammar and obliqueness (Section
\ref{subsec:linguistic_errors}), suggesting that
narrative coherence is correlated with morphological
precision rather than independent of it. The
annotated instances fall into five recurring failure
modes, each of which reveals a different limitation
of current Urdu LLMs.

\textit{Kinship-term misuse} is the dominant
sub-category. Urdu encodes a much finer-grained
kinship lexicon than English, distinguishing
paternal from maternal relations
(\foreignlanguage{arabic}{دادی} \textit{dādī}
``father's mother'' vs \foreignlanguage{arabic}{نانی}
\textit{nānī} ``mother's mother''), nephews by
sibling gender (\foreignlanguage{arabic}{بھتیجا}
\textit{bhatījā} ``brother's son'' vs
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{بھانجا} \textit{bhānjā}
``sister's son''), and address forms by social
relation. Models routinely collapse these
distinctions: in one QWEN story, a brother writes
to his sister calling her son
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{بھانجا} (\textit{bhānjā},
``sister's son'') where
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{بھتیجا} (\textit{bhatījā},
``brother's son'') is required; a DeepSeek story
introduces a maternal-side visit but uses
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{دادی اماں} (\textit{dādī
ammā}, ``paternal grandmother'') for the
grandmother encountered there. Address forms are
similarly over-generalised: QWEN uses
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ابا جان} (\textit{abbā
jān}, ``father, respectful'') for an elderly
stranger across multiple stories, and a DeepSeek
wife addresses her husband as
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{بیٹا} (\textit{beṭā},
``son''). These errors are consistent with an
English-mediated representation in which
``uncle'', ``nephew'', ``grandmother'', and
``dear'' map onto whichever Urdu term is
statistically most frequent in the training data,
regardless of the discourse context.

\textit{Long-range gender breakdown} forms a second
cluster, mostly observed in DeepSeek. Once a female
protagonist has been established, the model
nevertheless produces verbs and adjectives in the
masculine: a female speaker uses
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{جانتا} (\textit{jāntā},
masc.\ ``know'') instead of
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{جانتی} (\textit{jāntī},
fem.); a girl on a beach bench is described as
wearing \foreignlanguage{arabic}{عمامہ}
(\textit{`imāmah}, ``turban'', a male head-dress);
two figures including a girl are referred to as
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{دو نوجوان} (\textit{do
nau-jawān}, ``two young men''). These are not local
agreement slips but long-range failures in which
the discourse-established gender is lost over
several sentences.

\textit{Cultural and religious anachronism} appears
in low numbers but high salience. A QWEN story
describes the appearance of the
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{مکمل ہلال} (\textit{mukammal
hilāl}, ``complete crescent'') on Eid, conflating
the crescent (\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہلال}
\textit{hilāl}) that marks the festival with a full
moon (\foreignlanguage{arabic}{بدر} \textit{badr});
another describes a moon-sighting in the morning. A
DeepSeek story has the remains of a Muslim character
cremated, returned as
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{راکھ} (\textit{rākh},
``ash''), and given a funeral with an empty coffin,
none of which conform to Islamic burial practice. A
GPT story promotes a chef to
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{کیپٹن} (\textit{kepṭan},
``captain''), a term reserved for restaurant
front-of-house staff in Pakistani usage. The models
have memorised Urdu vocabulary but not the cultural
scripts that constrain its use.

\textit{Intra-story contradictions} include
references to a daughter ``lost five years ago''
who is nonetheless still being educated in the same
paragraph (QWEN), a mother said to have sewn a
blanket after her son had already left (GPT), an
uncle described as the protagonist's father's
closest friend (GPT), and the recurring head of a
man ``killed last year'' (DeepSeek). These reveal a
lack of persistent state across sentences in
medium-length narratives.

\textit{Physical and commonsense impossibilities}
round out the category: a shadow with hair, the
colour of a claw being visible in mud, a chandelier
inside a bus, books packed inside a thermos, and a
wrist-watch from 1947 displaying the date 14
November 1971. The surface fluency of these
sentences masks the absence of a world model.

Although Context Mismatch contributes the smallest
percentage of any linguistic-error category, its
qualitative profile is the most diagnostic of how
Urdu LLMs fail. The kinship and cultural sub-types
in particular indicate that current models reproduce
Urdu surface forms while operating with an
English-anchored semantic representation, an
observation consistent with the cross-lingual
contamination patterns reported in our spelling
(Section \ref{subsec:spelling}) and punctuation
sections.

section_western_influence.tex.name: section_western_influence.tex
section_western_influence.tex.value:
\paragraph{Western-Influenced Context Mismatch.}
A small but qualitatively striking sub-set of
context-mismatch errors involves the substitution
of Pakistani objects, institutions, and practices
with their nearest Western analogues, while the
surrounding Urdu prose remains fluent. Although
these instances are too few to dominate any single
model's overall error rate, they form a coherent
pattern that reinforces the cross-lingual
contamination hypothesis raised in our spelling
and punctuation sections. Three sub-types recur.

\textit{Western material culture in Pakistani
settings.} A GPT story set in a Punjabi village
describes the bedroom wall as covered with
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{پیلا وال پیپر}
(\textit{pīlā wāl pepar}, ``yellow wallpaper''), an
interior-decor convention essentially absent from
rural Pakistani homes, which use lime-wash or
distemper paint. Another GPT story has a rickshaw
driver refer to a
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{گیس اسٹیشن}
(\textit{gais isṭeshan}, ``gas station''), an
American term, where Pakistani usage is uniformly
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{پٹرول پمپ}
(\textit{paṭrol pamp}, ``petrol pump''). A third
introduces a
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ریفریشمنٹ وین}
(\textit{rifreshmanṭ wain}, ``refreshment van'')
inside a Pakistan-Railways train, where the
corresponding facility is the dining car or the
chai-trolley vendor. A DeepSeek story has a
character produce an old photograph from her
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{پورٹ فولیو}
(\textit{porṭ folīo}, ``portfolio''), a term
associated with professional design or finance
work rather than a personal photo collection.

\textit{Western institutional and role labels.}
DeepSeek refers to a twelve-year-old grandson as a
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{بارہ سالہ جونیئر}
(\textit{bārah sālah jūniyar}, ``twelve-year-old
junior''), importing the American junior--senior
school-grade classification into a Pakistani village
imam's household, where such labels are not used.
The same model also uses
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{ہوٹل کے بوائے}
(\textit{hoṭal ke bo'e}, ``hotel boy'') for a
restaurant waiter, calquing the colonial
``boy''-as-servant register, and introduces a
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{والدین میٹنگ}
(\textit{wāldain mīṭing}, ``parents meeting'') as
a literal translation of the English
``parent--teacher meeting'', whereas the
established Urdu term is
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{پی ٹی ایم}
(\textit{pī ṭī em}) or
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{اساتذہ والدین اجلاس}
(\textit{asātizah wāldain ijlās}).

\textit{Western ritual practice in Muslim contexts.}
The most consequential instance occurs in a
DeepSeek story in which a son is described as
performing the funeral washing of his deceased
mother:
\foreignlanguage{arabic}{اس نے اپنی ماں کی میت کو
اپنے ہاتھوں سے غسل دیا} (\textit{us ne apnī mā\,n
kī mayyit ko apne hāth\,on se ghusl diyā}, ``he
gave his mother's body bath with his own hands''),
narrated as a moment of filial devotion. In Islamic
practice, however, the ritual washing
(\textit{ghusl-e-mayyit}) of a woman is performed
by women only, and a male relative, including a
son, is prohibited from performing it on a female
body. The Urdu sentence is grammatically and
emotionally fluent; the violation is
procedural-religious, of the same kind as the
cremation example in
Section~\ref{subsec:context_mismatch}, and is
plausibly traceable to training on English-language
narratives in which sons routinely participate in
their parents' burials.

\textit{Why this matters.} These cases are vanishing
small in count, but they share a structure that
makes them especially hazardous for downstream use:
the Urdu is correct, the Pakistani reader will
notice the slip immediately, but an automatic
quality filter will not. If such passages are
ingested into the training data of a next-generation
Urdu model, the Western-anchored referent gains
statistical support relative to its Pakistani
counterpart, and the language model's representation
of Pakistani daily life drifts incrementally toward
its English-language source. This is the same
self-reinforcing contamination mechanism we identify
for space-deletion errors
(Section~\ref{subsec:space_deletion}), now operating
at the level of cultural ontology rather than
orthography.

creativity_vs_memorization.py.name: creativity_vs_memorization.py
creativity_vs_memorization.py.value:
"""
Measure Creativity vs Memorization of LLM-generated Urdu stories
against the original stories that share the same title.

Classification labels (per generated story, judged against the original):
    PR = Plot Reproduction       (closely follows the original story plot)
    LI = Literal Interpretation  (no plot from original; story built only
                                  from the surface meaning of the title)
    OR = Original                (genuinely new plot, not derivable from
                                  either the original story or the title alone)
    PI = Partial                 (some plot elements match the original,
                                  others are new)

Pairing rule (configurable):
    For original file  "Story_Title_05.txt"  in  ./stories/original/
    the script looks for matching files in each model folder using
    --pair-by  one of:
        index   -> matches by the trailing integer in the filename
                   (default; works for GPT_Story_5.txt vs Story_5.txt)
        name    -> matches by exact basename
        title   -> matches by the leading title slug before the index

Inputs
------
./stories/original/*.txt
./stories/GPT/*.txt
./stories/DeepSeek/*.txt
./stories/QWEN/*.txt

Outputs
-------
./creativity_results/per_story_judgments.xlsx
    Sheets:
      - Raw                (one row per (model, story) judgment)
      - LabelCounts        (counts of PR/LI/OR/PI per model)
      - LabelPercent       (same, as % of stories)
      - CrossModelOverlap  (pairwise plot-similarity between models)
      - Failures           (any API/parse failures for re-runs)

Requires
--------
    pip install openai pandas openpyxl python-dotenv tqdm

Set your key in environment:
    export OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...
"""

import argparse
import json
import os
import re
import time
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Optional

import pandas as pd
from openai import OpenAI
from tqdm import tqdm

# ---------------- configuration ----------------
ORIGINAL_DIR = Path("./stories/original")
MODEL_DIRS = {
    "GPT":      Path("./stories/GPT"),
    "DeepSeek": Path("./stories/DeepSeek"),
    "QWEN":     Path("./stories/QWEN"),
}
OUT_DIR = Path("./creativity_results")
JUDGE_MODEL = "gpt-4o-mini"     # cheap, multilingual, good enough
TEMPERATURE = 0.0
MAX_RETRIES = 3
SLEEP_ON_FAIL = 4               # seconds

LABELS = ["PR", "LI", "OR", "PI"]
LABEL_DESCRIPTIONS = {
    "PR": "Plot Reproduction - closely follows the original story plot, "
          "same key events, same outcome.",
    "LI": "Literal Interpretation of the title - the generated story does "
          "NOT reuse the original plot; it builds a new story directly from "
          "the surface meaning of the title with no creative twist.",
    "OR": "Original - the plot is genuinely new, not derivable from the "
          "original story and not a literal unfolding of the title.",
    "PI": "Partial - some plot elements clearly match the original story, "
          "others are new or different. Use when the answer is between PR and OR.",
}

# ---------------- file pairing ----------------
INDEX_RX = re.compile(r"(\d+)(?=\D*$)")  # last integer in basename

def index_of(path: Path) -> Optional[int]:
    m = INDEX_RX.search(path.stem)
    return int(m.group(1)) if m else None

def title_slug(path: Path) -> str:
    """Strip leading model prefix and trailing index."""
    stem = path.stem
    stem = re.sub(r"^(GPT|DS|DeepSeek|QW|QWEN)[_-]?", "", stem,
                  flags=re.IGNORECASE)
    stem = re.sub(r"[_-]?\d+$", "", stem)
    return stem.strip("_- ").lower()

def pair_files(originals: list[Path], generated: list[Path],
               mode: str) -> list[tuple[Path, Path]]:
    pairs = []
    if mode == "index":
        gen_by_idx = {index_of(p): p for p in generated if index_of(p) is not None}
        for o in originals:
            i = index_of(o)
            if i in gen_by_idx:
                pairs.append((o, gen_by_idx[i]))
    elif mode == "name":
        gen_by_name = {p.name: p for p in generated}
        for o in originals:
            if o.name in gen_by_name:
                pairs.append((o, gen_by_name[o.name]))
    elif mode == "title":
        gen_by_slug = {title_slug(p): p for p in generated}
        for o in originals:
            s = title_slug(o)
            if s in gen_by_slug:
                pairs.append((o, gen_by_slug[s]))
    else:
        raise ValueError(f"Unknown pair-by mode: {mode}")
    return pairs


# ---------------- judge prompt ----------------
SYSTEM_PROMPT = """You are a literary analyst fluent in Urdu and English.
You will be given:
  - the ORIGINAL Urdu story for a given title,
  - a NEW Urdu story written by an LLM for the same title.

Decide which ONE label best describes the relationship between the two:

PR = Plot Reproduction. The new story closely follows the original plot:
     same main characters' roles, same key events, same resolution.

LI = Literal Interpretation of the title. The new story does NOT reuse
     the original plot. It is built only from the surface meaning of the
     title, with no creative twist beyond what the title literally implies.

OR = Original. The plot is genuinely new. It is neither a retelling of
     the original story nor a literal unfolding of the title.

PI = Partial. Some clear plot elements come from the original; others are
     new. Use when the answer is between PR and OR.

Return STRICT JSON only, with this schema:
{
  "label": "PR" | "LI" | "OR" | "PI",
  "confidence": 0.0-1.0,
  "overlap_score": 0.0-1.0,      // plot overlap with the ORIGINAL
  "shared_elements": [string],   // up to 5 short phrases
  "new_elements":    [string],   // up to 5 short phrases
  "reason": string               // <= 60 words
}
Do not include any text outside the JSON object.
"""

CROSS_SYSTEM_PROMPT = """You are a literary analyst fluent in Urdu and English.
You will be given two Urdu stories generated by DIFFERENT LLMs for the SAME title.
Estimate how similar their plots are (NOT their style or word choice).

Return STRICT JSON only, with this schema:
{
  "overlap_score": 0.0-1.0,
  "shared_elements": [string],     // up to 5
  "diverging_elements": [string],  // up to 5
  "reason": string                 // <= 60 words
}
Do not include any text outside the JSON object.
"""


def truncate(text: str, max_chars: int = 8000) -> str:
    return text if len(text) <= max_chars else text[:max_chars] + " […]"


# ---------------- LLM call ----------------
def call_judge(client: OpenAI, system_prompt: str,
               user_prompt: str) -> Optional[dict]:
    for attempt in range(MAX_RETRIES):
        try:
            resp = client.chat.completions.create(
                model=JUDGE_MODEL,
                temperature=TEMPERATURE,
                response_format={"type": "json_object"},
                messages=[
                    {"role": "system", "content": system_prompt},
                    {"role": "user",   "content": user_prompt},
                ],
            )
            return json.loads(resp.choices[0].message.content)
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"  [retry {attempt+1}/{MAX_RETRIES}] {e}")
            time.sleep(SLEEP_ON_FAIL)
    return None


def judge_original_vs_generated(client, title, orig_text, gen_text):
    user = (f"TITLE: {title}\n\n"
            f"===== ORIGINAL STORY =====\n{truncate(orig_text)}\n\n"
            f"===== NEW STORY =====\n{truncate(gen_text)}\n")
    return call_judge(client, SYSTEM_PROMPT, user)


def judge_cross_model(client, title, text_a, text_b, model_a, model_b):
    user = (f"TITLE: {title}\n\n"
            f"===== STORY BY {model_a} =====\n{truncate(text_a)}\n\n"
            f"===== STORY BY {model_b} =====\n{truncate(text_b)}\n")
    return call_judge(client, CROSS_SYSTEM_PROMPT, user)


# ---------------- main ----------------
def main():
    ap = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    ap.add_argument("--pair-by", choices=["index", "name", "title"],
                    default="index")
    ap.add_argument("--skip-cross", action="store_true",
                    help="Skip cross-model similarity pass")
    ap.add_argument("--limit", type=int, default=0,
                    help="Limit number of originals processed (for testing)")
    args = ap.parse_args()

    api_key = os.environ.get("OPENAI_API_KEY")
    if not api_key:
        raise SystemExit("Set OPENAI_API_KEY in your environment.")
    client = OpenAI(api_key=api_key)

    OUT_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

    originals = sorted(ORIGINAL_DIR.glob("*.txt"))
    if args.limit:
        originals = originals[:args.limit]
    print(f"Loaded {len(originals)} original stories.")

    # Build (model -> [(original_path, generated_path)])
    model_pairs = {}
    for model, folder in MODEL_DIRS.items():
        gen = sorted(folder.glob("*.txt"))
        pairs = pair_files(originals, gen, args.pair_by)
        model_pairs[model] = pairs
        print(f"  {model}: matched {len(pairs)}/{len(originals)} stories")

    # 1. Judge each generated story vs its original
    rows, failures = [], []
    for model, pairs in model_pairs.items():
        for orig_path, gen_path in tqdm(pairs, desc=f"Judging {model}"):
            orig_text = orig_path.read_text(encoding="utf-8", errors="replace")
            gen_text  = gen_path.read_text(encoding="utf-8", errors="replace")
            title = orig_path.stem
            result = judge_original_vs_generated(
                client, title, orig_text, gen_text)
            if not result:
                failures.append({"model": model,
                                 "original": orig_path.name,
                                 "generated": gen_path.name,
                                 "stage": "vs_original"})
                continue
            rows.append({
                "Model":           model,
                "Title":           title,
                "Original File":   orig_path.name,
                "Generated File":  gen_path.name,
                "Label":           result.get("label"),
                "Confidence":      result.get("confidence"),
                "Overlap Score":   result.get("overlap_score"),
                "Shared Elements": " | ".join(result.get("shared_elements", [])),
                "New Elements":    " | ".join(result.get("new_elements", [])),
                "Reason":          result.get("reason", ""),
            })

    df = pd.DataFrame(rows)

    # 2. Aggregate
    counts = (df.groupby(["Model", "Label"]).size().unstack(fill_value=0)
                .reindex(columns=LABELS, fill_value=0))
    totals = counts.sum(axis=1).replace(0, 1)
    percent = counts.div(totals, axis=0).mul(100).round(2)
    counts["Total"] = counts.sum(axis=1)

    # 3. Cross-model overlap (per title, between every pair of models)
    cross_rows = []
    if not args.skip_cross:
        # Build per-title text per model
        titles = sorted({r["Title"] for r in rows})
        by_title_model = {(r["Title"], r["Model"]): r["Generated File"]
                          for r in rows}
        model_list = list(MODEL_DIRS.keys())
        for t in tqdm(titles, desc="Cross-model"):
            avail = [m for m in model_list if (t, m) in by_title_model]
            for i in range(len(avail)):
                for j in range(i + 1, len(avail)):
                    m_a, m_b = avail[i], avail[j]
                    fa = MODEL_DIRS[m_a] / by_title_model[(t, m_a)]
                    fb = MODEL_DIRS[m_b] / by_title_model[(t, m_b)]
                    ta = fa.read_text(encoding="utf-8", errors="replace")
                    tb = fb.read_text(encoding="utf-8", errors="replace")
                    res = judge_cross_model(client, t, ta, tb, m_a, m_b)
                    if not res:
                        failures.append({"model": f"{m_a}/{m_b}",
                                         "original": t,
                                         "generated": "",
                                         "stage": "cross_model"})
                        continue
                    cross_rows.append({
                        "Title":   t,
                        "Model A": m_a,
                        "Model B": m_b,
                        "Overlap": res.get("overlap_score"),
                        "Shared":  " | ".join(res.get("shared_elements", [])),
                        "Diverging": " | ".join(res.get("diverging_elements", [])),
                        "Reason":  res.get("reason", ""),
                    })

    cross_df = pd.DataFrame(cross_rows)
    if not cross_df.empty:
        cross_summary = (cross_df.groupby(["Model A", "Model B"])["Overlap"]
                                  .agg(["mean", "median", "count"])
                                  .round(3).reset_index())
    else:
        cross_summary = pd.DataFrame()

    # 4. Write Excel
    out_path = OUT_DIR / "per_story_judgments.xlsx"
    with pd.ExcelWriter(out_path, engine="openpyxl") as w:
        df.to_excel(w, sheet_name="Raw", index=False)
        counts.to_excel(w, sheet_name="LabelCounts")
        percent.to_excel(w, sheet_name="LabelPercent")
        if not cross_df.empty:
            cross_df.to_excel(w, sheet_name="CrossModelOverlap_Raw",
                              index=False)
            cross_summary.to_excel(w, sheet_name="CrossModelOverlap",
                                   index=False)
        if failures:
            pd.DataFrame(failures).to_excel(w, sheet_name="Failures",
                                            index=False)

    print("\n=== Label counts ===")
    print(counts.to_string())
    print("\n=== Label % per model ===")
    print(percent.to_string())
    if not cross_summary.empty:
        print("\n=== Cross-model overlap (mean) ===")
        print(cross_summary.to_string(index=False))
    print(f"\n[OK] Wrote {out_path}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

"""
Measure Creativity vs Memorization of LLM-generated Urdu stories
against a small set of original reference stories.

Folder layout
-------------
AIStories/stories/creativityTest/   <-- a FEW originals, named *_Story_<ID>.txt
                                        e.g. Original_Story_5.txt, Ref_Story_12.txt
AIStories/stories/GPT/*.txt         <-- e.g. GPT_Story_5.txt
AIStories/stories/DeepSeek/*.txt    <-- e.g. DS_Story_5.txt
AIStories/stories/QWEN/*.txt        <-- e.g. QW_Story_5.txt

The script:
  1. Picks ONLY the originals present in creativityTest/.
  2. For each original, finds the file in GPT/DeepSeek/QWEN whose filename
     ends in the SAME _Story_<ID>.
  3. Asks GPT-4o-mini to classify each generated story vs its original:
        PR = Plot Reproduction
        LI = Literal Interpretation of the title
        OR = Original (new plot)
        PI = Partial overlap
  4. Optionally compares the same-ID outputs across models (cross-model).

Labels (per generated story, judged against the original):
    PR = Plot Reproduction       (closely follows the original story plot)
    LI = Literal Interpretation  (no plot from original; story built only
                                  from the surface meaning of the title)
    OR = Original                (genuinely new plot, not derivable from
                                  either the original story or the title alone)
    PI = Partial                 (some plot elements match the original,
                                  others are new)

Usage
-----
    # First, smoke-test on 2 originals:
    python creativity_vs_memorization.py --limit 2

    # Then full run:
    python creativity_vs_memorization.py

    # Skip the (extra) cross-model pass:
    python creativity_vs_memorization.py --skip-cross

Requires
--------
    pip install openai pandas openpyxl tqdm
    export OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...
"""

import argparse
import json
import os
import re
import time
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Optional

import pandas as pd
from openai import OpenAI
from tqdm import tqdm

# ---------------- configuration ----------------
BASE = Path("AIStories/stories")
ORIGINAL_DIR = BASE / "creativityTest"
MODEL_DIRS = {
    "GPT":      BASE / "GPT",
    "DeepSeek": BASE / "DeepSeek",
    "QWEN":     BASE / "QWEN",
}
OUT_DIR = Path("AIStories/creativity_results")
JUDGE_MODEL = "gpt-4o-mini"
TEMPERATURE = 0.0
MAX_RETRIES = 3
SLEEP_ON_FAIL = 4
MAX_CHARS = 8000

LABELS = ["PR", "LI", "OR", "PI"]

# Match the trailing "_Story_<digits>" of a stem, with anything before it.
# e.g.  "Original_Story_5"  -> id = "5"
#       "GPT_Story_12"      -> id = "12"
#       "DS_Story_3_v2"     -> id = "3"  (trailing digits before optional suffix)
STORY_ID_RX = re.compile(r"_Story_(\d+)", re.IGNORECASE)


def story_id(path: Path) -> Optional[str]:
    m = STORY_ID_RX.search(path.stem)
    return m.group(1) if m else None


# ---------------- judge prompts ----------------
SYSTEM_PROMPT = """You are a literary analyst fluent in Urdu and English.
You will be given:
  - the ORIGINAL Urdu story for a given title,
  - a NEW Urdu story written by an LLM for the same title.

Decide which ONE label best describes the relationship between the two:

PR = Plot Reproduction. The new story closely follows the original plot:
     same main characters' roles, same key events, same resolution.

LI = Literal Interpretation of the title. The new story does NOT reuse
     the original plot. It is built only from the surface meaning of the
     title, with no creative twist beyond what the title literally implies.

OR = Original. The plot is genuinely new. It is neither a retelling of
     the original story nor a literal unfolding of the title.

PI = Partial. Some clear plot elements come from the original; others are
     new. Use when the answer is between PR and OR.

Return STRICT JSON only, with this schema:
{
  "label": "PR" | "LI" | "OR" | "PI",
  "confidence": 0.0-1.0,
  "overlap_score": 0.0-1.0,
  "shared_elements": [string],
  "new_elements":    [string],
  "reason": string
}
Do not include any text outside the JSON object.
"""

CROSS_SYSTEM_PROMPT = """You are a literary analyst fluent in Urdu and English.
You will be given two Urdu stories generated by DIFFERENT LLMs for the SAME title.
Estimate how similar their plots are (NOT their style or word choice).

Return STRICT JSON only, with this schema:
{
  "overlap_score": 0.0-1.0,
  "shared_elements": [string],
  "diverging_elements": [string],
  "reason": string
}
Do not include any text outside the JSON object.
"""


def truncate(text: str, max_chars: int = MAX_CHARS) -> str:
    return text if len(text) <= max_chars else text[:max_chars] + " […]"


# ---------------- LLM call ----------------
def call_judge(client: OpenAI, system_prompt: str,
               user_prompt: str) -> Optional[dict]:
    for attempt in range(MAX_RETRIES):
        try:
            resp = client.chat.completions.create(
                model=JUDGE_MODEL,
                temperature=TEMPERATURE,
                response_format={"type": "json_object"},
                messages=[
                    {"role": "system", "content": system_prompt},
                    {"role": "user",   "content": user_prompt},
                ],
            )
            return json.loads(resp.choices[0].message.content)
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"  [retry {attempt+1}/{MAX_RETRIES}] {e}")
            time.sleep(SLEEP_ON_FAIL)
    return None


def judge_original_vs_generated(client, title, orig_text, gen_text):
    user = (f"TITLE: {title}\n\n"
            f"===== ORIGINAL STORY =====\n{truncate(orig_text)}\n\n"
            f"===== NEW STORY =====\n{truncate(gen_text)}\n")
    return call_judge(client, SYSTEM_PROMPT, user)


def judge_cross_model(client, title, text_a, text_b, model_a, model_b):
    user = (f"TITLE: {title}\n\n"
            f"===== STORY BY {model_a} =====\n{truncate(text_a)}\n\n"
            f"===== STORY BY {model_b} =====\n{truncate(text_b)}\n")
    return call_judge(client, CROSS_SYSTEM_PROMPT, user)


# ---------------- main ----------------
def main():
    ap = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    ap.add_argument("--limit", type=int, default=0,
                    help="Process only the first N originals (smoke test).")
    ap.add_argument("--skip-cross", action="store_true",
                    help="Skip the cross-model similarity pass.")
    args = ap.parse_args()

    api_key = os.environ.get("OPENAI_API_KEY")
    if not api_key:
        raise SystemExit("Set OPENAI_API_KEY in your environment.")
    client = OpenAI(api_key=api_key)

    if not ORIGINAL_DIR.exists():
        raise SystemExit(f"Missing folder: {ORIGINAL_DIR}")

    # 1. Discover originals and index them by Story ID
    originals = sorted(ORIGINAL_DIR.glob("*.txt"))
    indexed = []
    for p in originals:
        sid = story_id(p)
        if sid is None:
            print(f"[SKIP] cannot parse Story ID from {p.name}")
            continue
        indexed.append((sid, p))
    if args.limit:
        indexed = indexed[:args.limit]

    print(f"Found {len(indexed)} original stories to evaluate "
          f"(limit={args.limit or 'all'}):")
    for sid, p in indexed:
        print(f"  ID {sid:>4}  {p.name}")

    # 2. Build (model -> {id: path})
    model_index = {}
    for model, folder in MODEL_DIRS.items():
        if not folder.exists():
            print(f"[WARN] {folder} not found; skipping {model}")
            model_index[model] = {}
            continue
        m = {}
        for p in folder.glob("*.txt"):
            sid = story_id(p)
            if sid is not None:
                m[sid] = p
        model_index[model] = m
        print(f"  {model}: indexed {len(m)} files in {folder}")

    OUT_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

    # 3. Judge each generated story vs its original
    rows, failures = [], []
    for sid, orig_path in tqdm(indexed, desc="Originals"):
        orig_text = orig_path.read_text(encoding="utf-8", errors="replace")
        title = orig_path.stem
        for model, idx in model_index.items():
            gen_path = idx.get(sid)
            if gen_path is None:
                print(f"  [MISS] {model}: no file for Story ID {sid}")
                failures.append({"model": model, "story_id": sid,
                                 "original": orig_path.name,
                                 "generated": "", "stage": "pair_missing"})
                continue
            gen_text = gen_path.read_text(encoding="utf-8", errors="replace")
            result = judge_original_vs_generated(
                client, title, orig_text, gen_text)
            if not result:
                failures.append({"model": model, "story_id": sid,
                                 "original": orig_path.name,
                                 "generated": gen_path.name,
                                 "stage": "vs_original"})
                continue
            rows.append({
                "Story ID":       sid,
                "Model":          model,
                "Title":          title,
                "Original File":  orig_path.name,
                "Generated File": gen_path.name,
                "Label":          result.get("label"),
                "Confidence":     result.get("confidence"),
                "Overlap Score":  result.get("overlap_score"),
                "Shared Elements": " | ".join(result.get("shared_elements", [])),
                "New Elements":    " | ".join(result.get("new_elements", [])),
                "Reason":         result.get("reason", ""),
            })

    df = pd.DataFrame(rows)

    if df.empty:
        print("\n[ERROR] No successful judgments. Check folders / API key.")
        return

    # 4. Aggregate
    counts = (df.groupby(["Model", "Label"]).size().unstack(fill_value=0)
                .reindex(columns=LABELS, fill_value=0))
    totals = counts.sum(axis=1).replace(0, 1)
    percent = counts.div(totals, axis=0).mul(100).round(2)
    counts["Total"] = counts.sum(axis=1)

    # 5. Cross-model overlap (per Story ID, every model pair)
    cross_rows = []
    if not args.skip_cross:
        ids = sorted({r["Story ID"] for r in rows})
        per_id_model_file = {(r["Story ID"], r["Model"]): r["Generated File"]
                             for r in rows}
        model_list = list(MODEL_DIRS.keys())
        for sid in tqdm(ids, desc="Cross-model"):
            avail = [m for m in model_list
                     if (sid, m) in per_id_model_file]
            for i in range(len(avail)):
                for j in range(i + 1, len(avail)):
                    m_a, m_b = avail[i], avail[j]
                    fa = MODEL_DIRS[m_a] / per_id_model_file[(sid, m_a)]
                    fb = MODEL_DIRS[m_b] / per_id_model_file[(sid, m_b)]
                    ta = fa.read_text(encoding="utf-8", errors="replace")
                    tb = fb.read_text(encoding="utf-8", errors="replace")
                    res = judge_cross_model(client, f"Story_{sid}",
                                            ta, tb, m_a, m_b)
                    if not res:
                        failures.append({"model": f"{m_a}/{m_b}",
                                         "story_id": sid,
                                         "original": "", "generated": "",
                                         "stage": "cross_model"})
                        continue
                    cross_rows.append({
                        "Story ID": sid,
                        "Model A": m_a,
                        "Model B": m_b,
                        "Overlap": res.get("overlap_score"),
                        "Shared":  " | ".join(res.get("shared_elements", [])),
                        "Diverging": " | ".join(res.get("diverging_elements", [])),
                        "Reason":  res.get("reason", ""),
                    })

    cross_df = pd.DataFrame(cross_rows)
    cross_summary = pd.DataFrame()
    if not cross_df.empty:
        cross_summary = (cross_df.groupby(["Model A", "Model B"])["Overlap"]
                                  .agg(["mean", "median", "count"])
                                  .round(3).reset_index())

    # 6. Write Excel
    out_path = OUT_DIR / "per_story_judgments.xlsx"
    with pd.ExcelWriter(out_path, engine="openpyxl") as w:
        df.to_excel(w, sheet_name="Raw", index=False)
        counts.to_excel(w, sheet_name="LabelCounts")
        percent.to_excel(w, sheet_name="LabelPercent")
        if not cross_df.empty:
            cross_df.to_excel(w, sheet_name="CrossModel_Raw", index=False)
            cross_summary.to_excel(w, sheet_name="CrossModel_Summary",
                                   index=False)
        if failures:
            pd.DataFrame(failures).to_excel(w, sheet_name="Failures",
                                            index=False)

    # 7. Console summary
    print("\n=== Label counts ===")
    print(counts.to_string())
    print("\n=== Label % per model ===")
    print(percent.to_string())
    if not cross_summary.empty:
        print("\n=== Cross-model overlap (mean) ===")
        print(cross_summary.to_string(index=False))
    if failures:
        print(f"\n[NOTE] {len(failures)} failures logged in 'Failures' sheet.")
    print(f"\n[OK] Wrote {out_path}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
