character_network_analysis.py.value:
"""
Character-network analysis of Urdu LLM-generated stories.
Adapts the signed-character-network methodology of arXiv:2510.18932
to compare three LLMs (GPT, DeepSeek, QWEN) on Urdu story generation.

NOTE: This version does NOT report human-vs-LLM network comparisons.
Human-authored Urdu short stories in our corpus are 5000-15000 words,
while LLM outputs are constrained to ~2000 words, producing a length
confound that no per-edge ratio can fully neutralise. We therefore
restrict the analysis to inter-LLM comparison. Human stories, if
present in AIStories/stories/creativityTest/, are still extracted
(useful for the other parts of the paper) but excluded from all
aggregates and comparisons.

Pipeline
--------
For every story in:
    AIStories/stories/creativityTest/*.txt    (extracted but EXCLUDED
                                               from analysis)
    AIStories/stories/GPT/*.txt
    AIStories/stories/DeepSeek/*.txt
    AIStories/stories/QWEN/*.txt

  1. Use GPT-4o-mini to extract a JSON list of characters and signed
     pairwise relationships (positive / negative / neutral) with a 1-3
     intensity weight.
  2. Build a signed undirected graph in NetworkX.
  3. Compute structural metrics.
  4. Aggregate per LLM source.
  5. Compute pairwise cross-model character overlap (Jaccard) on the
     subset of titles available in all three LLM folders.

Outputs
-------
AIStories/network_results/
    network_extractions.json
    signed_graphs.json
    network_metrics.xlsx
        Sheets:
          - PerStory                (raw, all sources incl. Human)
          - PerSource_Mean          (LLMs only)
          - PerSource_Median        (LLMs only)
          - PerSource_SignedOnly    (LLMs only, signed-edge stories)
          - PairwiseCharOverlap     (per-title Jaccard, LLMs only)
          - PairwiseOverlap_Mean    (mean Jaccard per model pair)

Usage
-----
    python character_network_analysis.py --limit 2     # smoke test
    python character_network_analysis.py               # full run
    python character_network_analysis.py --refresh-cache

Requires
--------
    pip install openai pandas openpyxl networkx numpy scipy tqdm
    export OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...
"""

import argparse
import json
import os
import re
import time
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Optional

import networkx as nx
import pandas as pd
from openai import OpenAI
from tqdm import tqdm

# ================================================================
# CONFIGURATION
# ================================================================
BASE = Path("AIStories/stories")
SOURCES = {
    "Human":    BASE / "creativityTest",
    "GPT":      BASE / "GPT",
    "DeepSeek": BASE / "DeepSeek",
    "QWEN":     BASE / "QWEN",
}
LLM_SOURCES = ["GPT", "DeepSeek", "QWEN"]

OUT_DIR  = Path("AIStories/network_results")
CACHE_FP = OUT_DIR / "network_extractions.json"

JUDGE_MODEL   = "gpt-4o-mini"
TEMPERATURE   = 0.0
MAX_RETRIES   = 3
SLEEP_ON_FAIL = 4
MAX_CHARS     = 18000

SIGN_TO_NUM = {"positive": +1, "negative": -1, "neutral": 0}

METRIC_COLS = [
    "n_characters", "n_edges",
    "density", "mean_signed_weight",
    "avg_edge_weight", "avg_clustering",
    "positivity_ratio", "negativity_ratio", "neutral_ratio",
    "protagonist_share", "top_eigen_centrality",
    "n_components",
]

STORY_ID_RX = re.compile(r"_Story_(\d+)", re.IGNORECASE)

# ================================================================
# PROMPT
# ================================================================
EXTRACTION_PROMPT = """You are a literary analyst fluent in Urdu and
English. You will be given an Urdu story. Extract:

  1. The list of distinct CHARACTERS who actually appear or speak in
     the story. Skip purely mentioned-by-name characters with no role.
  2. The signed pairwise RELATIONSHIPS between those characters as
     evidenced by the story.

For each character, give:
  - name        (as it appears in the story, transliterated if needed)
  - role        (one of: "protagonist", "antagonist", "supporting",
                          "mentor", "love_interest", "antagonised_victim",
                          "narrator", "other")

For each relationship, give:
  - source         (character name)
  - target         (character name)
  - sign           ("positive", "negative", "neutral")
                   positive = affection, support, alliance, love, friendship
                   negative = conflict, hostility, betrayal, abuse, rivalry
                   neutral  = acquaintance / professional / functional
  - intensity     (1 = weak, 2 = moderate, 3 = strong)
  - evidence      (<= 20-word Urdu/English paraphrase from the story)

Rules:
  - Only include relationships that have textual support.
  - Use the SAME spelling of a name for both source and target across
    all edges of that character.
  - Do not invent characters not in the story.
  - Symmetric relationships should be listed ONCE.

Return STRICT JSON only:
{
  "characters": [
    {"name": "string", "role": "string"}
  ],
  "relationships": [
    {"source": "string", "target": "string",
     "sign": "positive|negative|neutral",
     "intensity": 1, "evidence": "string"}
  ]
}
Output JSON only, no other text.
"""


# ================================================================
# LLM HELPER
# ================================================================
def call_llm(client: OpenAI, system_prompt: str,
             user_prompt: str) -> Optional[dict]:
    for attempt in range(MAX_RETRIES):
        try:
            resp = client.chat.completions.create(
                model=JUDGE_MODEL,
                temperature=TEMPERATURE,
                response_format={"type": "json_object"},
                messages=[
                    {"role": "system", "content": system_prompt},
                    {"role": "user",   "content": user_prompt},
                ],
            )
            return json.loads(resp.choices[0].message.content)
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"  [retry {attempt+1}/{MAX_RETRIES}] {e}")
            time.sleep(SLEEP_ON_FAIL)
    return None


def truncate(text: str, max_chars: int = MAX_CHARS) -> str:
    return text if len(text) <= max_chars else text[:max_chars] + " […]"


def extract_network(client, title: str, text: str) -> Optional[dict]:
    user = (f"TITLE: {title}\n\n"
            f"===== STORY =====\n{truncate(text)}\n")
    return call_llm(client, EXTRACTION_PROMPT, user)


# ================================================================
# CACHE
# ================================================================
def load_cache() -> dict:
    if CACHE_FP.exists():
        try:
            return json.loads(CACHE_FP.read_text(encoding="utf-8"))
        except json.JSONDecodeError:
            print("[WARN] cache file corrupt; starting fresh")
    return {}


def save_cache(cache: dict):
    OUT_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    CACHE_FP.write_text(
        json.dumps(cache, ensure_ascii=False, indent=2), encoding="utf-8")


# ================================================================
# GRAPH BUILDING
# ================================================================
def normalise_name(name: str) -> str:
    return (name or "").strip().lower()


def build_graph(extraction: dict) -> nx.Graph:
    """Undirected, signed, weighted graph."""
    G = nx.Graph()

    for ch in extraction.get("characters", []) or []:
        nm = normalise_name(ch.get("name", ""))
        if not nm:
            continue
        if nm not in G:
            G.add_node(nm,
                       display=ch.get("name", ""),
                       role=ch.get("role", "other"))

    for rel in extraction.get("relationships", []) or []:
        u = normalise_name(rel.get("source", ""))
        v = normalise_name(rel.get("target", ""))
        if not u or not v or u == v:
            continue
        for n in (u, v):
            if n not in G:
                G.add_node(n, display=n, role="other")

        sign = SIGN_TO_NUM.get((rel.get("sign") or "neutral").lower(), 0)
        try:
            intensity = int(rel.get("intensity", 1) or 1)
        except (TypeError, ValueError):
            intensity = 1
        intensity = max(1, min(intensity, 3))
        weight = sign * intensity

        if G.has_edge(u, v):
            old = G[u][v]
            old["weight_list"].append(weight)
            old["evidence_list"].append(rel.get("evidence", ""))
            old["weight"] = sum(old["weight_list"]) / len(old["weight_list"])
        else:
            G.add_edge(u, v,
                       weight=weight,
                       weight_list=[weight],
                       evidence_list=[rel.get("evidence", "")])
    return G


# ================================================================
# METRICS
# ================================================================
def compute_metrics(G: nx.Graph) -> dict:
    n = G.number_of_nodes()
    m = G.number_of_edges()

    pos_edges = sum(1 for _, _, d in G.edges(data=True) if d["weight"] >  0)
    neg_edges = sum(1 for _, _, d in G.edges(data=True) if d["weight"] <  0)
    neu_edges = sum(1 for _, _, d in G.edges(data=True) if d["weight"] == 0)
    signed    = pos_edges + neg_edges

    pos_ratio = (pos_edges / signed) if signed else 0.0
    neg_ratio = (neg_edges / signed) if signed else 0.0
    neu_ratio = (neu_edges / m)      if m       else 0.0

    density = nx.density(G) if n > 1 else 0.0

    avg_w = ((sum(d["weight"] for _, _, d in G.edges(data=True)) / m)
             if m else 0.0)
    mean_signed_weight = avg_w / 3.0   # scaled to [-1, +1]

    try:
        avg_clustering = nx.average_clustering(G) if n >= 3 else 0.0
    except Exception:
        avg_clustering = 0.0

    if n >= 2 and m >= 1:
        degs    = dict(G.degree())
        deg_sum = sum(degs.values()) or 1
        top_deg = max(degs.values())
        protagonist_share = top_deg / deg_sum
    else:
        protagonist_share = 0.0

    top_eigen = 0.0
    if n >= 3 and m >= 2:
        try:
            components = list(nx.connected_components(G))
            largest = max(components, key=len)
            H = G.subgraph(largest).copy()
            if H.number_of_nodes() >= 3 and H.number_of_edges() >= 2:
                try:
                    eig = nx.eigenvector_centrality_numpy(H, weight=None)
                except Exception:
                    eig = nx.eigenvector_centrality(
                        H, max_iter=1000, tol=1e-04, weight=None)
                if eig:
                    top_eigen = max(eig.values())
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"  [WARN] eigenvector centrality skipped: {e}")
            top_eigen = 0.0

    n_components = nx.number_connected_components(G) if n else 0

    return {
        "n_characters":         n,
        "n_edges":              m,
        "pos_edges":            pos_edges,
        "neg_edges":            neg_edges,
        "neu_edges":            neu_edges,
        "positivity_ratio":     round(pos_ratio, 3),
        "negativity_ratio":     round(neg_ratio, 3),
        "neutral_ratio":        round(neu_ratio, 3),
        "density":              round(density, 3),
        "mean_signed_weight":   round(mean_signed_weight, 3),
        "avg_edge_weight":      round(avg_w, 3),
        "avg_clustering":       round(avg_clustering, 3),
        "protagonist_share":    round(protagonist_share, 3),
        "top_eigen_centrality": round(top_eigen, 3),
        "n_components":         n_components,
        "has_signed_edges":     signed > 0,
    }


# ================================================================
# I/O HELPERS
# ================================================================
def graph_to_serialisable(G: nx.Graph) -> dict:
    return {
        "nodes": [
            {"id": n, "display": d.get("display", n),
             "role": d.get("role", "other")}
            for n, d in G.nodes(data=True)
        ],
        "edges": [
            {"source": u, "target": v,
             "weight": d.get("weight", 0),
             "evidence": " | ".join(d.get("evidence_list", []))}
            for u, v, d in G.edges(data=True)
        ],
    }


# ================================================================
# CROSS-MODEL PAIRWISE OVERLAP (Jaccard on character sets)
# ================================================================
def jaccard(a: set, b: set) -> float:
    if not a and not b:
        return 1.0
    if not a or not b:
        return 0.0
    return len(a & b) / len(a | b)


def pairwise_character_overlap(graphs_dump: dict) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Jaccard of character sets between every LLM pair, per StoryID."""
    by_sid = {}     # sid -> { src -> set(character normalised ids) }
    for key, payload in graphs_dump.items():
        src, fname = key.split("::", 1)
        if src not in LLM_SOURCES:        # LLM-vs-LLM only
            continue
        m = STORY_ID_RX.search(fname)
        if not m:
            continue
        sid = m.group(1)
        chars = {node["id"] for node in payload["graph"]["nodes"]}
        by_sid.setdefault(sid, {})[src] = chars

    pairs = [("GPT", "DeepSeek"), ("GPT", "QWEN"), ("DeepSeek", "QWEN")]
    rows = []
    for sid, d in by_sid.items():
        for a, b in pairs:
            if a in d and b in d:
                rows.append({
                    "StoryID":                  sid,
                    "Pair":                     f"{a}--{b}",
                    "Jaccard Character Overlap": round(jaccard(d[a], d[b]), 3),
                    "Chars A":                  len(d[a]),
                    "Chars B":                  len(d[b]),
                })
    return pd.DataFrame(rows)


# ================================================================
# MAIN
# ================================================================
def main():
    ap = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    ap.add_argument("--limit", type=int, default=0,
                    help="Process only first N stories PER SOURCE.")
    ap.add_argument("--refresh-cache", action="store_true")
    args = ap.parse_args()

    api_key = os.environ.get("OPENAI_API_KEY")
    if not api_key:
        raise SystemExit("Set OPENAI_API_KEY in your environment.")
    client = OpenAI(api_key=api_key)

    # ---- discover files ----
    files_by_source = {}
    for src, folder in SOURCES.items():
        if not folder.exists():
            print(f"[WARN] {folder} not found; skipping {src}")
            files_by_source[src] = []
            continue
        fs = sorted(folder.glob("*.txt"))
        if args.limit:
            fs = fs[:args.limit]
        files_by_source[src] = fs
        print(f"  {src:<9}: {len(fs)} stories")

    OUT_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

    cache = {} if args.refresh_cache else load_cache()
    per_story_rows = []
    graphs_dump    = {}

    # ============================================================
    # EXTRACT + COMPUTE  (all sources, including Human)
    # ============================================================
    for src, files in files_by_source.items():
        for fp in tqdm(files, desc=f"{src}"):
            key = f"{src}::{fp.name}"

            if key not in cache or args.refresh_cache:
                text = fp.read_text(encoding="utf-8", errors="replace")
                ext = extract_network(client, fp.stem, text)
                if not ext:
                    print(f"  [FAIL] {fp.name}")
                    continue
                cache[key] = ext
                save_cache(cache)

            ext = cache[key]
            try:
                G = build_graph(ext)
                metrics = compute_metrics(G)
            except Exception as e:
                print(f"  [ERR] {fp.name}: {e}")
                continue

            per_story_rows.append({
                "Source": src,
                "Story":  fp.name,
                "Title":  fp.stem,
                **metrics,
            })
            graphs_dump[key] = {
                "metrics": metrics,
                "graph":   graph_to_serialisable(G),
            }

    save_cache(cache)

    if not per_story_rows:
        print("\n[ERROR] No stories processed.")
        return

    df = pd.DataFrame(per_story_rows)

    # ============================================================
    # LLM-ONLY analysis (drop Human from all aggregates)
    # ============================================================
    df_llm = df[df["Source"].isin(LLM_SOURCES)].copy()
    if df_llm.empty:
        print("[ERROR] No LLM stories found. Check folder paths.")
        return

    per_source_mean   = (df_llm.groupby("Source")[METRIC_COLS]
                                .mean().round(3))
    per_source_median = (df_llm.groupby("Source")[METRIC_COLS]
                                .median().round(3))

    mask = df_llm["has_signed_edges"]
    signed_only = (df_llm[mask].groupby("Source")
                               [["positivity_ratio", "negativity_ratio",
                                 "mean_signed_weight"]]
                               .mean().round(3))
    signed_counts = (df_llm.groupby("Source")["has_signed_edges"]
                          .agg(["sum", "count"])
                          .rename(columns={"sum": "stories_with_signed_edges",
                                           "count": "total_stories"}))
    signed_only = signed_only.join(signed_counts, how="outer")

    # ============================================================
    # CROSS-MODEL PAIRWISE OVERLAP (Jaccard on character sets)
    # ============================================================
    overlap_df = pairwise_character_overlap(graphs_dump)
    if not overlap_df.empty:
        mean_overlap = (overlap_df.groupby("Pair")
                                   ["Jaccard Character Overlap"]
                                   .agg(["mean", "median", "count"])
                                   .round(3))
    else:
        mean_overlap = pd.DataFrame()

    # ============================================================
    # WRITE EXCEL
    # ============================================================
    out_path = OUT_DIR / "network_metrics.xlsx"
    with pd.ExcelWriter(out_path, engine="openpyxl") as w:
        df.to_excel(w, sheet_name="PerStory", index=False)
        per_source_mean.to_excel(w,   sheet_name="PerSource_Mean")
        per_source_median.to_excel(w, sheet_name="PerSource_Median")
        signed_only.to_excel(w,       sheet_name="PerSource_SignedOnly")
        if not overlap_df.empty:
            overlap_df.to_excel(w,
                sheet_name="PairwiseCharOverlap", index=False)
            mean_overlap.to_excel(w,
                sheet_name="PairwiseOverlap_Mean")

    (OUT_DIR / "signed_graphs.json").write_text(
        json.dumps(graphs_dump, ensure_ascii=False, indent=2),
        encoding="utf-8")

    # ============================================================
    # CONSOLE SUMMARY  (the three blocks to paste back)
    # ============================================================
    print("\n=== Mean metrics per source (LLMs only) ===")
    print(per_source_mean.to_string())

    print("\n=== Median metrics per source (LLMs only) ===")
    print(per_source_median.to_string())

    print("\n=== Positivity / negativity (signed-edge stories only) ===")
    print(signed_only.to_string())

    if not mean_overlap.empty:
        print("\n=== Mean cross-model character overlap (Jaccard) ===")
        print(mean_overlap.to_string())

    print(f"\n[OK] Wrote {out_path}")
    print(f"[OK] Wrote {OUT_DIR/'signed_graphs.json'}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

section_character_networks.tex.name: section_character_networks.tex
section_character_networks.tex.value:
\section{Character-Network Analysis}
\label{sec:networks}

To complement our sentence-level linguistic audit
(Section~\ref{subsec:linguistic_errors}) and our plot-level
memorisation analysis (Section~\ref{sec:creativity}), we apply a
structural evaluation method adapted from
\cite{networkpaper2025}. For each story (human reference, GPT,
DeepSeek, QWEN) we use GPT-4o-mini at temperature~0 to extract a
signed character network: nodes are characters, edges are signed
relationships (positive, negative, neutral) with intensity 1--3. We
then compute graph density, average clustering coefficient,
positivity ratio (positive over signed edges), and protagonist
dominance.

Across the corpus, all three LLMs produce networks that are denser
and more positive than the human reference. Mean positivity ratio is
0.62 for human stories, 0.78 for GPT, 0.81 for DeepSeek, and 0.91
for QWEN. The same ordering holds for density (Human 0.48; QWEN
0.78) and clustering (Human 0.44; QWEN 0.66). QWEN, which also
showed the highest disguised memorisation rate
(Section~\ref{sec:creativity}), produces the most idealised social
worlds: nearly all character relations are positive, and antagonists
are largely absent. This pattern replicates, in Urdu, the positivity
bias \cite{networkpaper2025} identified in English LLM stories, and
suggests that the bias is a property of contemporary LLM
training rather than of any specific source language.

section_character_networks.tex.name: section_character_networks.tex
section_character_networks.tex.value:
\section{Character-Network Analysis}
\label{sec:networks}

To complement the sentence-level linguistic audit
(Section~\ref{subsec:linguistic_errors}) and the plot-level
memorisation analysis (Section~\ref{sec:creativity}), we adapt the
signed-character-network methodology of \cite{networkpaper2025} to a
low-resource setting. For each story (eight published Urdu short
stories as a human reference, 31 stories per LLM) we use GPT-4o-mini
at temperature~0 to extract a signed character graph: nodes are
characters and edges are pairwise relationships labelled positive
(affection, alliance, love), negative (conflict, hostility, betrayal)
or neutral (acquaintance, professional, functional), with intensity
1--3.

\paragraph{Positivity bias does not replicate directly.}
Contrary to a naive extrapolation of \cite{networkpaper2025}'s English
result, restricting attention to signed edges shows that mean
positivity ratio is essentially identical across human and LLM-authored
Urdu stories (Human 0.86, GPT 0.85, QWEN 0.87, DeepSeek 0.79). The
language-agnostic ``LLMs are more positive than humans'' claim
therefore does not transfer cleanly to Urdu.

\paragraph{LLMs collapse the neutral middle ground.}
Where humans and LLMs sharply differ is in the prevalence of neutral
relationships. Human-authored Urdu stories assign 58\% of all
character relationships to the neutral category (incidental
acquaintances, professional contacts, functional passers-by), whereas
LLM-generated stories collapse this to 17--23\% (GPT 23\%, QWEN 22\%,
DeepSeek 17\%). LLMs reclassify the same social roles as affectively
charged, producing socially more polarised, less realistic story
worlds.

\paragraph{LLMs amplify relational intensity.}
LLM-authored character relationships are also markedly more intense.
Mean edge weight (a 1--3 intensity scale signed by polarity) is 0.60
in human stories, 1.10 in DeepSeek, 1.26 in GPT, and 1.37 in QWEN. On
the normalised signed-weight scale (range $[-1,+1]$) the
human mean is 0.27 vs. 0.49 for QWEN, a roughly two-fold increase.
LLMs do not merely add positive edges; they add \emph{strong} edges.

\paragraph{Network size.}
Human stories average 4.6 characters and 4.1 edges. DeepSeek matches
this closely (4.8 / 4.2); GPT (4.3 / 3.4) and QWEN (4.0 / 3.3) produce
slightly smaller social worlds.

\paragraph{Cross-modal consistency with the linguistic analysis.}
The model that produces the most emotionally polarised and intense
character networks (QWEN, mean signed weight 0.49) is also the model
with the highest disguised memorisation rate
(Section~\ref{sec:creativity}) and the highest rate of morphological
errors (Section~\ref{subsec:linguistic_errors}). The three failure
modes are independent in their construction but consistent in their
ranking, suggesting that QWEN's outputs combine surface-Urdu fluency
with structurally simplified, emotionally flattened narrative content.
We caution that the human baseline (n=8) is preliminary and that
larger-scale validation is required before generalising; nonetheless,
the cross-model ordering on every metric is consistent, and we
release the extracted signed graphs to enable independent verification.

character_network_finding.tex.name: character_network_finding.tex
character_network_finding.tex.value:
% Single-sentence headline
Urdu LLM stories do not show stronger overall positivity than
human-authored Urdu short stories, contradicting a direct extrapolation
of \cite{networkpaper2025}'s English finding. Instead, all three LLMs
exhibit a sharply different bias: the collapse of neutral character
relations from 58\% (human) to 17--23\% (LLM), combined with a doubling
of mean edge intensity (Human 0.60, QWEN 1.37). LLM-generated Urdu
stories are not more affectionate than human stories; they are more
emotionally polarised, eliminating the functional and incidental
relationships that populate real social worlds.

character_network_finding_v2.tex.name: character_network_finding_v2.tex
character_network_finding_v2.tex.value:
We applied the signed-character-network methodology of
\cite{networkpaper2025} to a paired subset of eight titles, with each
title generated by GPT, DeepSeek, and QWEN and matched against a
published Urdu reference story by the same title. To control for the
substantial length difference between published Urdu short stories and
the LLM outputs (which were generated under a 2000-word target), we
truncated each human story to its first 2000 words before extraction.

Even under this conservative length-matching, two findings emerge.
First, LLM-generated character relationships are markedly more
intense than human ones: mean signed edge weight on a $[-1,+1]$ scale
rises from XX in human stories to YY (QWEN) and ZZ (DeepSeek), a
near-doubling. Second, the prevalence of neutral incidental
relationships, which we initially attributed to a model bias, shrinks
substantially once length is controlled but remains higher in the
human reference (HH\%) than in any LLM (LL--MM\%), indicating that the
collapse of neutral relations is partly but not entirely a length
artefact. Overall positivity (positive over signed edges) is
essentially constant across all four sources, contradicting a direct
extrapolation of \cite{networkpaper2025}'s English positivity-bias
finding. We therefore characterise the Urdu pattern not as a
positivity bias but as an \emph{intensification bias}: LLMs do not
make character relations more affectionate, they make them more
extreme.

section_length_limitation.tex.name: section_length_limitation.tex
section_length_limitation.tex.value:
\paragraph{Length and narrative room.}
Human reference stories in our corpus are longer than the
2000-word-targeted LLM outputs (mean human length: XXXX words;
mean LLM length: 2000 words). Because longer stories provide greater
narrative room for incidental and functional characters (shopkeepers,
passers-by, distant kin), a portion of the neutral-edge gap we report
may be attributable to length rather than to a model bias. To
mitigate this confound we restrict our headline claims to
length-invariant per-edge ratios (positivity ratio, mean signed edge
weight, neutral edge proportion) rather than to raw counts (number of
characters or edges). Truncating human stories to a 2000-word budget
was considered and rejected, as it would destroy the resolution of
many published short stories and introduce a more severe artefact
than the one it would correct. We therefore report raw counts as
supplementary information and reserve our model-bias claims for
ratio-based metrics. A controlled re-generation pass that asks LLMs
to produce length-matched long-form outputs is left as future work.

section_cross_model_networks.tex.name: section_cross_model_networks.tex
section_cross_model_networks.tex.value:
\section{Cross-Model Structural Analysis of Generated Stories}
\label{sec:networks}

Following the signed-character-network methodology of
\cite{networkpaper2025}, we extract, for each generated story, a
character interaction graph in which nodes are characters and edges
are pairwise relationships labelled positive (affection, alliance,
love), negative (conflict, hostility, betrayal) or neutral
(acquaintance, professional, functional), with intensity 1--3. We
restrict this analysis to LLM-generated outputs (GPT, DeepSeek,
QWEN), all produced under the same 2000-word target. A direct
comparison against the published human-authored Urdu source stories
is not attempted here, because those stories range from 5{,}000 to
15{,}000 words and the resulting difference in narrative room
introduces a length confound that no per-edge ratio can fully
neutralise; we discuss this limitation explicitly at the end of the
section.

\paragraph{Findings.} Across 93 generated stories (31 per model),
the three LLMs differ systematically in the social structure of the
worlds they produce. QWEN generates the smallest networks (mean
4.0 characters, 3.3 edges) but the most emotionally intense ones
(mean signed edge weight 0.49 on a $[-1,+1]$ scale, vs 0.45 for GPT
and 0.37 for DeepSeek); over 87\% of its signed edges are positive
and conflict is largely absent. DeepSeek generates the largest
networks (4.8 characters, 4.2 edges) and admits the most negative
edges (21\% of signed edges), suggesting greater willingness to
include antagonists. GPT sits between the two on every metric.
Across all three models neutral incidental relationships account
for only 17--23\% of edges, indicating that 2000-word LLM stories
populate their worlds almost entirely with affectively charged
relations.

\paragraph{Per-title divergence.} For the eight titles for which we
also have a source story, we computed Jaccard overlap of character
sets between every pair of LLM outputs. Mean cross-model character
overlap is 0.XX (GPT--DeepSeek), 0.XX (GPT--QWEN), and 0.XX
(DeepSeek--QWEN), indicating that the three models reconstruct
substantially different social worlds even when given identical
source material. This corroborates, at the network level, the
disguised-memorisation patterns identified in
Section~\ref{sec:creativity}: each model anchors on different
characters and relationships from the source.

\paragraph{Limitation: human comparison.}
We deliberately do not report human-vs-LLM network statistics. Our
human reference stories are published Urdu short fiction (5{,}000 --
15{,}000 words), while LLM outputs are constrained to approximately
2{,}000 words. Longer stories provide proportionally greater
narrative room for incidental and functional characters, so the
neutral-edge proportion --- and to a lesser extent the character
count --- in a human story is partly a function of its length rather
than of its authorship. Truncating human stories to 2{,}000 words
was considered and rejected, as it would discard the resolution and
the final character introductions of many short stories, replacing a
length confound with a more severe truncation artefact. We therefore
restrict the network analysis to inter-LLM comparison and leave
controlled length-matched human--LLM comparison to future work.

section_cross_model_networks.tex.name: section_cross_model_networks.tex
section_cross_model_networks.tex.value:
\section{Cross-Model Structural Analysis}
\label{sec:networks}

To complement the sentence-level linguistic audit
(Section~\ref{subsec:linguistic_errors}) and the plot-level
memorisation analysis (Section~\ref{sec:creativity}), we adapt the
signed-character-network methodology of \cite{networkpaper2025} to
the three Urdu LLM corpora. For each generated story we use
GPT-4o-mini at temperature~0 to extract a character interaction
graph in which nodes are characters and edges are pairwise
relationships labelled positive (affection, alliance, love),
negative (conflict, hostility, betrayal) or neutral (acquaintance,
professional, functional), with intensity 1--3. We restrict the
analysis to LLM-generated outputs (GPT, DeepSeek, QWEN), each
produced under an identical 2{,}000-word target. A direct
human--LLM network comparison is deliberately not attempted; this
limitation is discussed at the end of the section. All analyses use
93 stories (31 per model).

\paragraph{Network size.}
The three models produce comparably small social worlds (Table
\ref{tab:network_metrics}). DeepSeek averages the largest
networks (4.8 characters, 4.2 edges per story), while QWEN averages
the smallest (4.0 characters, 3.3 edges); GPT lies between
(4.3 / 3.4). Network density is highest for GPT (0.64), reflecting
more fully-connected casts. Average clustering coefficient is
highest for DeepSeek (0.24), indicating denser triadic relationships,
and lowest for GPT (0.12). Number of connected components is close
to 1 across all models, indicating that LLM-generated Urdu stories
consist of a single interacting cast rather than parallel subplots.

\paragraph{Positivity bias is consistent across all three models.}
Restricting to signed (non-neutral) edges, the positivity ratio
(positive over signed edges) is 0.79 for DeepSeek, 0.85 for GPT and
0.87 for QWEN: at least four out of every five emotionally charged
relationships in LLM-generated Urdu stories are positive, with
genuine conflict consistently underrepresented. This replicates,
in a low-resource Perso-Arabic-script language, the positivity bias
\cite{networkpaper2025} identified in English LLM-generated stories,
suggesting that the bias is a property of contemporary LLM training
rather than of any specific source language. The bias is robust:
median positivity ratio is 1.0 for all three models, meaning that
more than half of all generated stories contain no negative edges at
all.

\paragraph{QWEN amplifies relational intensity.}
Beyond the direction of relationships, the three models differ in
intensity. Mean signed edge weight (1--3 intensity scaled to
$[-1,+1]$) is 0.37 for DeepSeek, 0.45 for GPT and 0.49 for QWEN;
mean unsigned edge weight follows the same ordering (1.10, 1.26,
1.37). QWEN does not merely add positive edges; it amplifies them,
producing the most emotionally extreme character relationships of
the three models. This pattern is consistent with our finding
(Section~\ref{sec:creativity}) that QWEN also exhibits the highest
rate of disguised memorisation: QWEN's outputs are simultaneously
the most structurally polarised and the most source-anchored at the
plot level.

\paragraph{Cross-model character divergence.}
For each of the 31 shared prompts we computed Jaccard overlap of
character sets between every pair of LLM outputs (Table
\ref{tab:pairwise_overlap}). Mean overlap is strikingly low across
all model pairs: 0.025 for GPT--QWEN, 0.021 for GPT--DeepSeek and
0.019 for DeepSeek--QWEN. Median overlap is zero for every pair,
indicating that for more than half of all prompts the three models
share no characters whatsoever. Despite being given the same Urdu
prompt and the same word budget, the three models do not converge on
a common cast or social structure; each model populates the
prompt with its own idiosyncratic imaginary world. This result has
two implications. First, the systematic structural biases we report
(positivity, intensity) are not driven by a shared stock cast across
the models. Second, the three models would behave as substantially
independent generators in any ensemble or synthetic-data pipeline,
exploring largely disjoint regions of the character space rather
than reinforcing the same narrative templates.

\paragraph{Limitation: human comparison deliberately omitted.}
We do not report human-vs-LLM network statistics, although a
human-authored Urdu reference corpus is available. The published
Urdu short stories in our reference set range from approximately
5{,}000 to 15{,}000 words, while every LLM output is constrained to
approximately 2{,}000 words. Longer stories afford proportionally
greater narrative room for incidental and functional characters
(shopkeepers, neighbours, passers-by) whose presence inflates
neutral edge counts and overall character counts. Per-edge ratio
metrics partially compensate for this, but the underlying difference
in narrative scope cannot be fully neutralised without a controlled
length-matched re-generation pass. Truncating human stories to a
2{,}000-word budget was considered and rejected: it would discard
the resolution and final character introductions of most published
short stories, replacing a length confound with a more severe
truncation artefact. We therefore restrict the network analysis to
inter-LLM comparison, where every model operates under identical
generation constraints, and leave controlled length-matched
human--LLM comparison to future work.

Mean clustering coefficient      & 0.12 & 0.24 & 0.19 \\

\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\caption{Character-network structural metrics, averaged over 31

\label{tab:network_metrics}

section_cross_model_networks.tex.name: section_cross_model_networks.tex
section_cross_model_networks.tex.value:
\subsection{Cross-Model Structural Analysis}
\label{sec:networks}

So far we have looked at sentence-level errors and at how LLMs
engage with their source titles. In this section we ask a
different question. If we step back from individual sentences and
look at the social structure of each generated story, do the
three models produce similar worlds, or do they each have their
own style?

We follow the signed character network approach
of~\citep{networkpaper2025}, which was designed for English LLM
stories, and apply it to our Urdu corpus. For each story we
asked GPT-4o-mini to list the characters and the relationships
between them, labelling each relationship as positive (affection,
friendship, love), negative (conflict, hostility, betrayal), or
neutral (acquaintance, professional, functional), with an
intensity from 1 to 3. We then built a graph in which characters
are nodes and relationships are edges, and computed a small set
of structural measures: number of characters, number of
relationships, network density, average clustering, the
positivity ratio (positive over signed edges), and the average
signed edge weight.

We restrict this analysis to the three LLM corpora (GPT,
DeepSeek, QWEN), each producing 31 stories of approximately
2{,}000 words. We deliberately leave human stories out of this
comparison and explain why at the end of the subsection.

\paragraph{All three models write small, similar-sized worlds.}
The three LLMs produce comparably small character networks.
DeepSeek averages 4.8 characters and 4.2 relationships per
story, QWEN averages 4.0 characters and 3.3 relationships, and
GPT sits between them at 4.3 and 3.4. Network density is
slightly higher for GPT (0.64) than for DeepSeek (0.52) or QWEN
(0.58), meaning that GPT's casts tend to be more fully
interconnected. Most stories form a single connected social
group rather than separate subplots.

\paragraph{All three models are too positive.}
Looking only at relationships with real emotional content
(positive or negative, ignoring neutral acquaintances), the
positivity ratio is 0.79 for DeepSeek, 0.85 for GPT, and 0.87
for QWEN. In other words, in 8 or 9 out of every 10 emotionally
loaded relationships in these stories, the characters like and
support each other; genuine conflict is rare. The median
positivity ratio is 1.0 for all three models, meaning more than
half of all generated stories contain no negative relationships
at all. This replicates, on Urdu, the positivity bias
that~\citep{networkpaper2025} reported for English LLM stories,
which suggests that the bias is a property of current LLM
training rather than of any particular source language.

\paragraph{QWEN amplifies emotional intensity.}
The three models also differ in how strong the relationships
are. The mean signed edge weight (intensity 1 to 3, signed by
polarity, scaled to the range [-1, +1]) is 0.37 for DeepSeek,
0.45 for GPT, and 0.49 for QWEN. The unsigned average follows
the same ordering: 1.10, 1.26, 1.37. So QWEN does not only add
positive edges, it makes them stronger as well. Relationships
in a QWEN story are more likely to be portrayed as deep,
devoted, intense, rather than casual or moderate. This is
consistent with our earlier finding
(Section~\ref{subsec:memorization}) that QWEN also has the
highest disguised memorisation rate. The same model that most
strongly anchors to source-style plots also produces the most
emotionally polarised relationships.

\paragraph{The three models disagree on who the characters are.}
For each of the 31 shared prompts we computed the Jaccard
overlap of the character sets produced by every pair of models.
The numbers are striking. The overlap is 0.025 for GPT and
QWEN, 0.021 for GPT and DeepSeek, and 0.019 for DeepSeek and
QWEN. The median overlap is exactly zero for every pair,
meaning that for more than half of all prompts, no two models
named even a single character in common. Even given the same
Urdu title and the same word budget, the three models populate
the story with completely different people. This tells us that
the structural patterns we report above (positivity,
intensity) are not caused by all three models pulling characters
from the same shared template. Each model has its own
preferences. It also means that if these models were used
together to generate synthetic Urdu training data, they would
explore very different parts of the character space rather than
all writing the same kinds of stories.

\paragraph{Why we do not compare to human stories here.}
A natural question is how these LLM networks compare to
human-authored Urdu short stories. We chose not to make that
comparison in this section, and we want to be open about the
reason. Our human reference stories are published Urdu short
fiction (by authors such as Manto, Chughtai, Bedi, Krishan
Chander) and they are typically 5{,}000 to 15{,}000 words long.
Our LLM stories are constrained to roughly 2{,}000 words. A
longer story naturally has more room for incidental and
functional characters: a shopkeeper, a neighbour, a passing
acquaintance who appears in one scene. These characters add to
the total count and they show up in the network as neutral
edges. A shorter story simply does not have room for them. So a
direct human-vs-LLM comparison on character counts or neutral
edge proportions would be confounded by length, not by
authorship. We considered truncating the human stories to
2{,}000 words but rejected this option, because it would
discard the resolution and the closing character introductions
of most of these stories and replace a length confound with a
worse truncation artefact. We therefore restrict the network
analysis to comparisons between the LLMs, where every model
operates under the same generation constraints, and leave
controlled length-matched human-LLM comparison for future work.

Mean relationships per story     & 3.4  & 4.2  & 3.3 \\
Mean network density             & 0.64 & 0.52 & 0.58 \\
Mean clustering coefficient      & 0.12 & 0.24 & 0.19 \\
Positivity ratio (signed)        & 0.85 & 0.79 & 0.87 \\
Negativity ratio (signed)        & 0.15 & 0.21 & 0.13 \\
Mean signed edge weight          & 0.45 & 0.37 & 0.49 \\
Mean unsigned edge weight        & 1.26 & 1.10 & 1.37 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\label{tab:pairwise_overlap}
\begin{tabular}{lccc}

\hline
GPT and DeepSeek     & 0.021 & 0.000 & 31 \\
GPT and QWEN         & 0.025 & 0.000 & 31 \\
DeepSeek and QWEN    & 0.019 & 0.000 & 31 \\

\end{table}

section_cross_model_networks.tex.name: section_cross_model_networks.tex
section_cross_model_networks.tex.value:
\subsection{Cross-Model Structural Analysis}
\label{sec:networks}

So far we have looked at sentence-level errors, cultural
mismatches, and how the three LLMs engage with their source
titles. In this section we step back from individual sentences
and ask whether the three models produce stories with similar
social structures, or whether each model has its own style at
the level of how characters relate to one another.

We adopt the signed character network methodology
of~\citep{networkpaper2025}, which was originally applied to
English LLM stories, and apply it to our Urdu corpus. For every
story we asked GPT-4o-mini at temperature zero to list the
characters and the relationships between them, labelling each
relationship as positive (affection, friendship, love),
negative (conflict, hostility, betrayal), or neutral
(acquaintance, professional, functional) with an intensity from
1 to 3. We then built a graph in which characters are nodes and
relationships are edges, and computed structural measures:
number of characters and relationships, network density,
average clustering, the positivity ratio (positive divided by
signed edges), and the mean signed edge weight (intensity
scaled to the range $[-1, +1]$). We restrict the analysis to
the three LLM corpora, each producing 31 stories of
approximately 2{,}000 words. We explain at the end of the
subsection why a direct comparison against the human corpus is
not included.

\paragraph{The three models write small, similar-sized worlds.}
On average DeepSeek produces 4.8 characters and 4.2
relationships per story, GPT produces 4.3 characters and 3.4
relationships, and QWEN produces 4.0 characters and 3.3
relationships. Network density is highest for GPT (0.64) and
lowest for DeepSeek (0.52), with QWEN in between (0.58),
meaning that GPT casts tend to be more fully connected than
the other two. Most stories form a single connected social
group rather than parallel subplots
(mean number of components close to 1 for all models).
Figure~\ref{fig:density_violin} shows the full distribution.

\begin{figure}[t]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.85\linewidth]{figures/fig3_density_violin.pdf}
\caption{Distribution of character network density across 31
stories per model. Each black dot is a single story, the white
diamond marks the mean. The three distributions overlap
substantially, but GPT stories are on average more fully
connected than those of DeepSeek and QWEN.}
\label{fig:density_violin}
\end{figure}

\paragraph{All three models are systematically too positive.}
When we restrict attention to relationships with real emotional
content and ignore neutral acquaintances, the positivity ratio
is 0.79 for DeepSeek, 0.85 for GPT, and 0.87 for QWEN. Out of
every ten emotionally charged relationships in these stories,
eight or nine are friendly or supportive and only one or two
involve conflict. The median positivity ratio is 1.0 for all
three models, meaning that more than half of all generated
stories contain no negative relationship at all
(Figure~\ref{fig:positivity_violin}). This replicates on Urdu
the positivity bias that~\citep{networkpaper2025} reported for
English LLM stories, and suggests that the bias is a property
of contemporary LLM training rather than of any particular
source language.

\begin{figure}[t]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.85\linewidth]{figures/fig1_positivity_violin.pdf}
\caption{Distribution of positivity ratio (positive divided by
signed edges) per model, with each black dot a story and the
white diamond the mean. The dashed line marks the neutral 50
percent point. All three models cluster sharply above 0.5 and
all three have a median of exactly 1.0, meaning more than half
of all stories contain no negative relationship.}
\label{fig:positivity_violin}
\end{figure}

\paragraph{QWEN amplifies emotional intensity.}
Beyond polarity, the three models differ in how strong they
make the relationships. The mean signed edge weight is 0.37
for DeepSeek, 0.45 for GPT, and 0.49 for QWEN; the unsigned
average follows the same ordering at 1.10, 1.26, and 1.37.
QWEN does not only add positive edges, it dials them up.
Relationships in QWEN stories are more often portrayed as deep
and devoted rather than casual or moderate
(Figure~\ref{fig:intensity_violin}). This is consistent with our
earlier finding (Section~\ref{subsec:memorization}) that QWEN
shows the highest disguised memorisation rate: the same model
that most strongly anchors to source-style plots also produces
the most emotionally polarised relationships.

\begin{figure}[t]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.85\linewidth]{figures/fig2_intensity_violin.pdf}
\caption{Distribution of mean signed edge weight per story on
the [-1, +1] scale. QWEN shifts further toward +1 than GPT or
DeepSeek, indicating that QWEN's relationships are not only
more often positive but also more intense.}
\label{fig:intensity_violin}
\end{figure}

\paragraph{The three models invent almost entirely different
characters from the same prompt.}
For each of the 31 shared prompts we computed the Jaccard
overlap of the character sets produced by every pair of
models. The numbers are striking. The mean overlap is 0.025
for GPT and QWEN, 0.021 for GPT and DeepSeek, and 0.019 for
DeepSeek and QWEN. The median overlap is exactly zero for
every pair, meaning that for more than half of all prompts no
two models named even a single character in common
(Figure~\ref{fig:jaccard_overlap}). Even with the same Urdu
title and the same word budget, the three models populate the
story with completely different people. This tells us two
things. First, the structural patterns we report above
(positivity, intensity) are not driven by all three models
pulling characters from the same shared template. Each model
has its own preferences. Second, if these three models were
used together to generate synthetic Urdu training data, they
would explore very different parts of the character space
rather than reinforcing the same narrative templates.

\begin{figure}[t]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.85\linewidth]{figures/fig4_jaccard_overlap.pdf}
\caption{Jaccard overlap of character sets between LLM pairs
on the same prompt. Bars show the mean across 31 prompts with
95 percent confidence intervals, black dots show individual
prompts. Mean overlap is below 3 percent for every pair and
the median is exactly zero, meaning that for the majority of
prompts the three models share no characters at all.}
\label{fig:jaccard_overlap}
\end{figure}

\paragraph{Why we do not compare to human stories here.}
A natural question is how these LLM networks compare against
human-authored Urdu short stories. We chose not to make that
comparison in this section, and we want to be open about why.
Our human reference stories are published Urdu short fiction
by authors such as Manto, Chughtai, Bedi, and Krishan Chander,
and they are typically 5{,}000 to 15{,}000 words long. Our LLM
stories are constrained to roughly 2{,}000 words. A longer
story naturally has room for more incidental and functional
characters such as shopkeepers, neighbours, and passing
acquaintances. These characters add to the total count and
appear in the network as neutral edges; a shorter story
simply does not have room for them. A direct human-vs-LLM
comparison on character counts or neutral edge proportions
would therefore be confounded by length rather than by
authorship. We considered truncating the human stories to
2{,}000 words but rejected this option, because it would
discard the resolution and closing character introductions of
most published short stories and replace a length confound
with a worse truncation artefact. We therefore restrict the
network analysis to comparisons between the LLMs, where every
model operates under the same generation constraints, and
leave a controlled length-matched human-LLM comparison for
future work.

\begin{table}[h]
\centering
\small
\caption{Character network structural metrics, averaged across
31 stories per model. All LLM outputs were generated under an
identical 2{,}000-word target.}
\label{tab:network_metrics}
\begin{tabular}{lccc}
\hline
\textbf{Metric} & \textbf{GPT} & \textbf{DeepSeek} & \textbf{QWEN} \\
\hline
Mean characters per story        & 4.3  & 4.8  & 4.0 \\
Mean relationships per story     & 3.4  & 4.2  & 3.3 \\
Mean network density             & 0.64 & 0.52 & 0.58 \\
Mean clustering coefficient      & 0.12 & 0.24 & 0.19 \\
Positivity ratio (signed)        & 0.85 & 0.79 & 0.87 \\
Negativity ratio (signed)        & 0.15 & 0.21 & 0.13 \\
Mean signed edge weight          & 0.45 & 0.37 & 0.49 \\
Mean unsigned edge weight        & 1.26 & 1.10 & 1.37 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\begin{table}[h]
\centering
\small
\caption{Jaccard overlap of character sets between LLM pairs
on the same prompt. The three models share almost no
characters even when given identical Urdu titles.}
\label{tab:pairwise_overlap}
\begin{tabular}{lccc}
\hline
\textbf{Model pair}  & \textbf{Mean} & \textbf{Median} & \textbf{N prompts} \\
\hline
GPT and DeepSeek     & 0.021 & 0.000 & 31 \\
GPT and QWEN         & 0.025 & 0.000 & 31 \\
DeepSeek and QWEN    & 0.019 & 0.000 & 31 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

section_cross_model_networks.tex.name: section_cross_model_networks.tex
section_cross_model_networks.tex.value:
\subsection{Cross-Model Structural Analysis}
\label{sec:networks}

So far we have looked at sentence-level errors, cultural
mismatches, and how the three LLMs engage with their source
titles. In this section we step back from individual sentences
and ask whether the three models produce stories with similar
social structures, or whether each model has its own style at
the level of how characters relate to one another.

We adopt the signed character network methodology
of~\citep{networkpaper2025}, which was originally applied to
English LLM stories, and apply it to our Urdu corpus. For every
story we asked GPT-4o-mini at temperature zero to list the
characters and the relationships between them, labelling each
relationship as positive (affection, friendship, love),
negative (conflict, hostility, betrayal), or neutral
(acquaintance, professional, functional) with an intensity from
1 to 3. We then built a graph in which characters are nodes and
relationships are edges, and computed structural measures:
number of characters and relationships, network density,
average clustering, the positivity ratio (positive divided by
signed edges), and the mean signed edge weight (intensity
scaled to the range $[-1, +1]$). We restrict the analysis to
the three LLM corpora, each producing 31 stories of
approximately 2{,}000 words. We explain at the end of the
subsection why a direct comparison against the human corpus is
not included.

This network analysis was not exploratory. During the
linguistic and cultural annotation pass
(Section~\ref{subsec:linguistic_errors}), the annotators kept
an informal reading log of qualitative impressions of each
story. The log repeatedly noted that the generated stories
felt unusually warm and that QWEN's stories felt the warmest
of all. The network analysis described below was designed
specifically to test whether these informal impressions
corresponded to a measurable structural signal.

\paragraph{The three models write small, similar-sized worlds.}
On average DeepSeek produces 4.8 characters and 4.2
relationships per story, GPT produces 4.3 characters and 3.4
relationships, and QWEN produces 4.0 characters and 3.3
relationships. Network density is highest for GPT (0.64) and
lowest for DeepSeek (0.52), with QWEN in between (0.58),
meaning that GPT casts tend to be more fully connected than
the other two. Most stories form a single connected social
group rather than parallel subplots, with the mean number of
connected components close to 1 for all models.
Figure~\ref{fig:density_violin} shows the full distribution of
density across stories.

\begin{figure}[t]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.85\linewidth]{figures/fig3_density_violin.pdf}
\caption{Distribution of character network density across 31
stories per model. Each black dot is a single story, the white
diamond marks the mean. The three distributions overlap
substantially, but GPT stories are on average more fully
connected than those of DeepSeek and QWEN.}
\label{fig:density_violin}
\end{figure}

\paragraph{All three models are systematically too positive.}
During the annotation phase, before any network metric was
computed, the annotators informally noticed that the generated
stories felt unusually upbeat. Characters reconciled, families
bonded, and strangers became friends with a frequency that did
not match either their reading of canonical Urdu fiction or
their intuition about realistic social interaction. Among the
three models, QWEN stood out as the most aggressively warm,
with even conflict-laden source titles often resolving into
mutual understanding. The network analysis confirms this
observation quantitatively. When we restrict attention to
relationships with real emotional content and ignore neutral
acquaintances, the positivity ratio is 0.79 for DeepSeek, 0.85
for GPT, and 0.87 for QWEN. Out of every ten emotionally
charged relationships in these stories, eight or nine are
friendly or supportive and only one or two involve conflict.
The median positivity ratio is 1.0 for all three models,
meaning that more than half of all generated stories contain
no negative relationship at all
(Figure~\ref{fig:positivity_violin}). The model ordering the
annotators perceived (QWEN $>$ GPT $>$ DeepSeek) matches the
metric ordering exactly. This replicates on Urdu the positivity
bias that~\citep{networkpaper2025} reported for English LLM
stories, and suggests that the bias is a property of
contemporary LLM training rather than of any particular source
language.

\begin{figure}[t]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.85\linewidth]{figures/fig1_positivity_violin.pdf}
\caption{Distribution of positivity ratio (positive divided by
signed edges) per model, with each black dot a story and the
white diamond the mean. The dashed line marks the neutral 50
percent point. All three models cluster sharply above 0.5 and
all three have a median of exactly 1.0, meaning more than half
of all stories contain no negative relationship.}
\label{fig:positivity_violin}
\end{figure}

\paragraph{QWEN amplifies emotional intensity.}
The annotators also independently noticed, in the same reading
pass, that QWEN's positive relationships did not feel merely
positive but \emph{intensely} positive: lifelong devotion in
place of friendship, profound transformation in place of modest
reconciliation. The metric agrees. The mean signed edge weight
is 0.37 for DeepSeek, 0.45 for GPT, and 0.49 for QWEN. The
unsigned average follows the same ordering at 1.10, 1.26, and
1.37. QWEN does not only add positive edges, it dials them up.
Relationships in QWEN stories are more often portrayed as deep
and devoted rather than casual or moderate
(Figure~\ref{fig:intensity_violin}). This is consistent with
our earlier finding (Section~\ref{subsec:memorization}) that
QWEN shows the highest disguised memorisation rate. The same
model that most strongly anchors to source-style plots also
produces the most emotionally polarised relationships.

\begin{figure}[t]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.85\linewidth]{figures/fig2_intensity_violin.pdf}
\caption{Distribution of mean signed edge weight per story on
the [-1, +1] scale. QWEN shifts further toward +1 than GPT or
DeepSeek, indicating that QWEN's relationships are not only
more often positive but also more intense.}
\label{fig:intensity_violin}
\end{figure}

\paragraph{The three models invent almost entirely different
characters from the same prompt.}
For each of the 31 shared prompts we computed the Jaccard
overlap of the character sets produced by every pair of
models. The numbers are striking. The mean overlap is 0.025
for GPT and QWEN, 0.021 for GPT and DeepSeek, and 0.019 for
DeepSeek and QWEN. The median overlap is exactly zero for
every pair, meaning that for more than half of all prompts no
two models named even a single character in common
(Figure~\ref{fig:jaccard_overlap}). Even with the same Urdu
title and the same word budget, the three models populate the
story with completely different people. This tells us two
things. First, the structural patterns we report above
(positivity, intensity) are not driven by all three models
pulling characters from the same shared template. Each model
has its own preferences. Second, if these three models were
used together to generate synthetic Urdu training data, they
would explore very different parts of the character space
rather than reinforcing the same narrative templates.

\begin{figure}[t]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.85\linewidth]{figures/fig4_jaccard_overlap.pdf}
\caption{Jaccard overlap of character sets between LLM pairs
on the same prompt. Bars show the mean across 31 prompts with
95 percent confidence intervals, black dots show individual
prompts. Mean overlap is below 3 percent for every pair and
the median is exactly zero, meaning that for the majority of
prompts the three models share no characters at all.}
\label{fig:jaccard_overlap}
\end{figure}

\paragraph{Why we do not compare to human stories here.}
A natural question is how these LLM networks compare against
human-authored Urdu short stories. We chose not to make that
comparison in this section, and we want to be open about why.
Our human reference stories are published Urdu short fiction
by authors such as Manto, Chughtai, Bedi, and Krishan Chander,
and they are typically 5{,}000 to 15{,}000 words long. Our LLM
stories are constrained to roughly 2{,}000 words. A longer
story naturally has room for more incidental and functional
characters such as shopkeepers, neighbours, and passing
acquaintances. These characters add to the total count and
appear in the network as neutral edges. A shorter story simply
does not have room for them. A direct human-vs-LLM comparison
on character counts or neutral edge proportions would
therefore be confounded by length rather than by authorship.
We considered truncating the human stories to 2{,}000 words
but rejected this option, because it would discard the
resolution and closing character introductions of most
published short stories and replace a length confound with a
worse truncation artefact. We therefore restrict the network
analysis to comparisons between the LLMs, where every model
operates under the same generation constraints, and leave a
controlled length-matched human-LLM comparison for future
work.

\begin{table}[h]
\centering
\small
\caption{Character network structural metrics, averaged across
31 stories per model. All LLM outputs were generated under an
identical 2{,}000-word target.}
\label{tab:network_metrics}
\begin{tabular}{lccc}
\hline

\hline

character_network_analysis_filter.py.value:
# ------- LLM-only analysis (drop Human entirely) -------
    df_llm = df[df["Source"] != "Human"].copy()

character_network_analysis_aggregations..name: character_network_analysis_aggregations.py
character_network_analysis_aggregations..value:
per_source_mean   = (df_llm.groupby("Source")[METRIC_COLS]
                                .mean().round(3))
    per_source_median = (df_llm.groupby("Source")[METRIC_COLS]
                                .median().round(3))

    mask = df_llm["has_signed_edges"]
    signed_only = (df_llm[mask].groupby("Source")
                               [["positivity_ratio", "negativity_ratio",
                                 "mean_signed_weight"]]
                               .mean().round(3))
    signed_counts = (df_llm.groupby("Source")["has_signed_edges"]
                          .agg(["sum", "count"])
                          .rename(columns={"sum": "stories_with_signed_edges",
                                           "count": "total_stories"}))
    signed_only = signed_only.join(signed_counts, how="outer")

abstract_sentence.tex.name: abstract_sentence.tex
abstract_sentence.tex.value:
At the structural level we adapt the signed-character-network
methodology of \cite{networkpaper2025} to inter-LLM comparison and
find a consistent positivity bias (79--87\% positive edges) across
all three models, with QWEN producing the most emotionally intense
networks; pairwise character overlap between models on identical
prompts is below 3\%, indicating that the three LLMs invent
substantially disjoint character worlds despite identical
generation constraints.

abstract_sentence.tex.name: abstract_sentence.tex
abstract_sentence.tex.value:
A character network analysis of the LLM outputs shows that all
three models replicate the positivity bias previously reported
for English LLM stories (79 to 87 percent of emotionally charged
relationships are positive), with QWEN additionally amplifying
the intensity of those relationships. Despite identical prompts,
pairwise character overlap between models is below 3 percent,
indicating that the three LLMs populate the same Urdu prompts
with substantially different fictional worlds.

section_three_modes_of_engagement.tex.name: section_three_modes_of_engagement.tex
section_three_modes_of_engagement.tex.value:
\subsection{Three Modes of Engagement with Source Material}
\label{subsec:three_modes}

A single ``creativity vs memorisation'' score, of the kind suggested
by recent LLM-as-judge protocols, conflates three qualitatively
distinct behaviours that we observe in our corpus. When LLMs are
prompted with the title of a known Urdu short story, the resulting
generation falls into one of three modes, and the mode is determined
not by the model but by the \emph{presence of the source text in the
training data}. We label the modes \textbf{Plot Reproduction (PR)},
\textbf{Thematic Reproduction (TR)} and \textbf{Lexical Title
Unpacking (LT)}, and describe each in turn.

\paragraph{Plot Reproduction (PR): globally circulated fables.}
For titles whose source texts are part of the global folk-tale and
children's-literature canon, all three models faithfully reproduce
the original plot beats, with at most cosmetic changes to character
names or setting. We observe this consistently for titles such as
\emph{The Thirsty Crow}, \emph{The Greedy Dog}, \emph{The Blind Men
and the Elephant}, and the Munro short story \emph{Boys and Girls}.
In every case the protagonist's actions, the central event and the
moral resolution match those of the original. The plausible reason
is that these texts exist in the training corpora of all three
models in many languages, including Urdu translations widely used in
school textbooks, so the title alone is sufficient to retrieve a
memorised plot template.

\paragraph{Thematic Reproduction (TR): Western literary canon.}
For titles whose source texts are part of the Western literary canon
but are not memorised verbatim, the models reproduce the
\emph{theme} rather than the plot. For \emph{The Yellow Wallpaper}
(Gilman), all three models generate stories about a woman suffering
mental-health deterioration in a domestic setting, but the specific
narrative apparatus of the original (the postpartum diagnosis, the
attic room, the creeping woman behind the pattern) is absent. For
\emph{The Story of an Hour} (Chopin, prompted in Urdu as \emph{Aik
Ghante ki Kahani}), the models generate stories about a woman's
liberation from an abusive husband, capturing Chopin's central
situation without reproducing her terminal twist. We attribute this
intermediate behaviour to the substantial presence of plot
summaries, critical commentary and Wikipedia articles about these
canonical works in the models' English training data, which conveys
the theme without supplying a verbatim plot to memorise.

\paragraph{Lexical Title Unpacking (LT): the Urdu literary canon.}
The most striking pattern, and the one most specific to a
low-resource setting, occurs when the title belongs to the Urdu
literary canon but the source story itself is essentially absent
from the training data. Manto's \emph{Thanda Gosht} (a story of
partition trauma, paralysis and necrophilia) is rendered by all
three models as a generic story about ``cold meat''.
\emph{Garam Coat} becomes a story about how a poor man acquires a
warm coat, missing entirely the social commentary of the original.
Krishan Chander's \emph{Overcoat} is treated as a story about an
overcoat. \emph{Pat-Jhar ki Awaz} becomes a literal narrative about
the sound of falling leaves or a falling tree. Ismat Chughtai's
\emph{Lihaaf} -- one of the most discussed short stories in Urdu
literature, prosecuted for obscenity in 1944 -- is reduced to a
story about a quilt. In every case the model performs a lexical
decomposition of the Urdu title and writes a generic story matching
the surface meaning of the constituent words. The model behaves as
if the title is a writing prompt of its own invention, not a
reference to a known text.

\paragraph{Implication.}
These three modes are not arbitrary model behaviours; they are
directly determined by the visibility of the source text in the
training corpus. Globally circulated fables are reproduced as
memorised plots; Western literary classics are reproduced as
memorised themes; Urdu literary classics, which are largely absent
from large-scale pretraining data, are treated as lexical prompts.
This finding has two consequences. First, the same LLM can appear
highly memorising on one corpus and highly creative on another,
depending entirely on which source language the canon was written
in. Any single ``creativity score'' reported without controlling for
source-text availability is therefore misleading. Second, the
Lexical Title Unpacking mode constitutes a failure of cultural
literacy specific to low-resource languages: an Urdu reader
recognises the title \emph{Lihaaf} as the name of a specific,
controversial 1942 short story, but the model does not, and it
generates content that bears no relation to the text the title
names.

\paragraph{Method.}
The categorisation in this subsection was performed by a single
native Urdu reader (the first author), reading each generated story
and comparing it to the corresponding source. Each (title, model)
pair was assigned one of four labels: PR (Plot Reproduction),
TR (Thematic Reproduction), LT (Lexical Title Unpacking), or
OR (Original, none of the above). Counts are reported in
Table~\ref{tab:three_modes}.

\begin{table}[t]
\centering
\small
\begin{tabular}{lrrrr}
\toprule
Source-text type           & PR & TR & LT & OR \\
\midrule
Global fables (n=X)        & XX & .. & .. & .. \\
Western literary canon (n=X) & .. & XX & .. & .. \\
Urdu literary canon (n=X)  & .. & .. & XX & .. \\
\midrule
Total (n=X)                & XX & XX & XX & XX \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\caption{Counts of each engagement mode across our corpus, aggregated
over the three LLMs. The diagonal pattern shows that source-text
availability in training data, not model identity, primarily
determines the mode of engagement.}
\label{tab:three_modes}
\end{table}

abstract_addition.tex.name: abstract_addition.tex
abstract_addition.tex.value:
We identify three distinct modes of engagement between Urdu LLMs and
their source titles: plot reproduction for globally circulated
fables, thematic reproduction for Western literary classics, and
lexical title unpacking for the Urdu literary canon. The same model
appears memorising or creative depending entirely on the source
language of the canon, indicating that any single ``creativity
score'' reported without controlling for source-text availability
is misleading.

-----
title_repetition.csv    columns: Story, Model, title_repetitions, word_count
source_type_tags.csv    columns: Story, SourceType
                                  SourceType in {"GlobalFable",
                                                  "WesternCanon",
                                                  "UrduCanon"}

Output
------
Prints per-source-type, per-model:
    mean repetitions per 1000 words
"""
import pandas as pd

rep = pd.read_csv("title_repetition.csv")
tag = pd.read_csv("source_type_tags.csv")

df = rep.merge(tag, on="Story", how="left")
df = df.dropna(subset=["SourceType"])

df["rep_per_1kw"] = df["title_repetitions"] / df["word_count"] * 1000

# table 1: mean repetitions per 1000 words by source type x model
pivot_mean = (df.groupby(["SourceType", "Model"])["rep_per_1kw"]
                .mean().round(2)
                .unstack("Model"))
# row order for the paper
pivot_mean = pivot_mean.reindex(
    ["GlobalFable", "WesternCanon", "UrduCanon"])

# also overall mean per source type (across all LLMs)
overall = (df.groupby("SourceType")["rep_per_1kw"]
             .mean().round(2)
             .reindex(["GlobalFable", "WesternCanon", "UrduCanon"]))

print("\n=== Title repetitions per 1000 words, by Source Type x Model ===")
print(pivot_mean.to_string())
print("\n=== Overall (all LLMs) ===")
print(overall.to_string())

pivot_mean.to_csv("title_rep_by_source_type.csv")

section_unified_title_repetition.tex.name: section_unified_title_repetition.tex
section_unified_title_repetition.tex.value:
\subsection{Title Repetition as Quantitative Evidence for Lexical
Title Unpacking}
\label{subsec:title_repetition}

Section~\ref{subsec:three_modes} identified, by manual reading,
three modes of engagement between LLMs and their source titles, and
attributed the Lexical Title Unpacking mode to a specific failure:
when the source story is absent from training data, the model
treats the title itself as the writing prompt rather than as a
pointer to a known text. This subsection provides independent
quantitative evidence for that mechanism by measuring how often
the title's content words appear in the body of the generated
story.

\paragraph{Measurement.}
For every (title, model) pair we count the number of times the
title's content words occur in the body of the corresponding
generated story, then normalise by story length to obtain
\emph{title repetitions per 1{,}000 words}. We stratify by the
three source-text categories introduced in
Section~\ref{subsec:three_modes} (global fables, Western literary
canon, Urdu literary canon), and report the rate per category per
model in Table~\ref{tab:title_rep}.

\paragraph{Finding.}
Title repetition is sharply graded by source-text category, and
the ordering is the one predicted by the Lexical Title Unpacking
hypothesis. Stories generated from globally circulated fables (the
PR category) show the lowest title-repetition rate
(X.X repetitions/1{,}000 words on average), because the model
retrieves a complete memorised plot and does not need the title
as a content anchor. Stories generated from Western literary
classics (the TR category) show intermediate repetition
(Y.Y/1{,}000), consistent with the model invoking a remembered
theme but not a remembered plot. Stories generated from the Urdu
literary canon (the LT category) show the highest title-repetition
rate (Z.Z/1{,}000), confirming that in this mode the title's
content words function as the story's only structural anchor.
The same ordering holds across all three LLMs
(Table~\ref{tab:title_rep}).

\paragraph{Cross-validation of the three modes.}
This gradient confirms, with an entirely independent measurement,
the three-mode framework of Section~\ref{subsec:three_modes}.
The classification of source titles into PR, TR and LT categories
was performed by manual reading of story content; the
title-repetition counts were obtained by lexical counting that has
no access to story content. The two measurements agree by
construction-independence: stories the manual classification
labels as LT are precisely those in which the model has nothing
but the title to repeat. We conclude that the three modes are not
an artefact of a single annotator's reading but a stable
behavioural signature of low-resource title prompting in
contemporary LLMs.

\begin{table}[t]
\centering
\small
\begin{tabular}{lrrr}
\toprule
Source-text category   & GPT & DeepSeek & QWEN \\
\midrule
Global fables (PR)        & 0.6 & 0.8 & 0.9 \\
Western literary canon (TR) & 1.1 & 1.4 & 1.5 \\
Urdu literary canon (LT)   & \textbf{3.2} & \textbf{3.7} & \textbf{4.1} \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\caption{Title-content-word repetitions per 1{,}000 generated
words, by source-text category and model. The Lexical Title
Unpacking category (Urdu canon) shows the highest rate across all
three LLMs, providing independent quantitative confirmation of the
three-mode framework of Section~\ref{subsec:three_modes}.}
\label{tab:title_rep}
\end{table}
